responseSet.title: On: The Comments of Erik Gann
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On: The Comments of Erik Gann

Gadi Taub

Gadi Taub

91905, Israel E-mail: gaditaub@zahav.net.il

Gadi Taub  

Dear Editors,

I agree with Erik Gann's criticism that perhaps my characterization of Freud's view at the end of the paper as ‘Hobbesian’ is a shorthand which does not do justice to the complexity of Freud's view. Freud indeed moved ‘beyond the pleasure principle’ whereas Hobbes did not. Still, Freud seemed also to have moved beyond his own conception of Ego Drives (which could have developed, and eventually did at Hartman's hands, into a theory which could see object relations as primary), finally coming to the position which makes them solely derivative of Drive discharge (erotic or aggressive). Even if we accept that there is no objectless drive, in the original formulation of drive theory the drive strives for discharge, and the object is the means, hence attachment to the object is still derivative. To the extent that one agrees that this is the case, then it would be inaccurate to describe Freud's view as relational according to the criterion my paper adopted from Greenberg and Mitchell — the question of whether one thinks of object relations as primary or derivative.

Freud's view then, even when extended beyond the pleasure principle, still sees all motivation as private self-interest in the narrow sense. It does elaborate Hobbes's view, but it does not challenge its basic assumption of egotism. And for this reason it does not challenge the whole framework of moral and political philosophy based on Hobbesian psychology. To this day, liberal individualism still assumes we are motivated by narrow self-interest alone, and therefore sees the basic question of moral and political philosophy in this way: ‘How can we arrange society (as well as families) so that self-interested monads would be able to get along together?’ But if getting along with others — attachments — is a primary motivation in itself, then the way to a different moral view opens up, and a serious challenge is posed to our conception of liberty as individual autonomy.

My point in bringing Hobbes into the discussion was that the relational challenge to classical drive theory also extends to Hobbes's assumption and makes the psychoanalytical controversy highly relevant to social and moral issues. In fact, the same argument between relational and drive theorists has already taken place long ago within the field of moral and political philosophy (at a time when there was no distinct discipline of psychology)

responseSet.title: Discussion Concerning Dr Pierre Marty's Paper
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Discussion Concerning Dr Pierre Marty's Paper

Marie Bonaparte's Contribution

We can't get away from narcissism! Psychosomatics itself is rooted in narcissism. It is narcissism to believe in our self-destructive powers! There seems to have been, on the individual level, something of what is happening on the collective level, what is happening for the psychosis of the year 2000, as our late colleague Schiff called this monumentally narcissistic notion that human beings were powerful enough to blow up the planet with an atom bomb!

M. Held's Contribution

My warm congratulations to Dr Marty for his fine lecture and the skill with which he has climbed from the specialized clinical terrain, familiar mainly from our hospital practice over the last several years, to the highest summits of knowledge itself. I freely admit: until now it had hardly occurred to me to apply to the field of psychosomatic medicine a methodology that proves so necessary in addressing the major problems of biology and psychology, which are both concrete and analytical in their epistemological aspects.

This leads me to wonder if, to return to the title of the paper we have just been given, the narcissistic difficulties encountered by the observer in psychosomatics are specific in nature or whether this is merely a fact of a very general kind. Now it certainly seems that identical difficulties appear in what are reputed to be the most exact sciences. There are very few theories and experiments into which the observing subject's affectivity, and particularly his narcissism, do not intrude.

In numbers theory (do mathematical entities exist beyond the counting and calculating mind?) in the transition from deductive mechanics and a priori to experimental mechanics, for example, in extending the application of Carnot's second principle (of the depletion of energy), narcissistic resistances long delayed the acceptance of a science that casts doubt on the unity and eternity of matter (see L. Brunschvicg, 1972, Les étapes de la philosophie mathématique, p. 305 et seq.). A fine example of narcissism was given by the late Lecornu, a member of the Institute, professor of applied mechanics at the École Polytechnique. In a discussion of Einstein's theories at the Academy of Sciences, Lecornu indignantly exclaimed (or approximately — I quote from memory): ‘We don't want any of those theories here coming to upset our old mechanics’, revealing his narcissistic anxieties about the disintegration of what had always founded his self-construction as a man of science. This brings to mind what our master and friend Leuba said about those patients who rebel violently against their analyst's chaotic undertakings at the beginning of the analytic treatment to which they have apparently submitted of their own free will.

Traum, Wahn und Mikrowelten. Affektregulierung in Neurose und Psychose und die Generierung von Bildern1 [Dreams, Delusions and Micro-worlds: Affect Regulation in Neurosis and Psychosis and the Creation of Images] by Ulrich MoserBrandes & Apsel, Frankfurt, 2008; 220 pp; €24.90

Joachim Danckwardt

Joachim Danckwardt

Im Buckenloh 2, D-72070 Tübingen, Germany
E-mail: JFDanckwardt@t-online.de
http://danckwardt.homepage.t-online.de

Joachim Danckwardt  

In their article Computer simulation of a model of neurotic defence processes, Ulrich Moser and Ilka von Zeppelin (Moser, 1969; von Zeppelin and Moser, 1973) suspended the traditional interpretative access to dreams and undertook a descriptive coding approach. They thus began to formulate models for the creation and transformation of dreams (Moser and von Zeppelin, 1991, 1996). With Thomas Stompe in the present book (Chapter 3), this approach is extended to the phenomenon of delusions. Here Moser, emeritus professor of psychology and clinical psychology at Zurich University, and a psychoanalyst of the Swiss Psychoanalytical Society/IPA, summarizes this further stage of his research that uses precise methods for coding phantasies. I will discuss this in more detail below, and begin by just indicating a few important premises. Moser conceives dreams and delusions to be virtual micro-worlds. They occur mainly in (linguistic) images and linguistic scenarios. These are shaped by affects. They are partly overlain by thoughts and reflections. In the images, the subject moves across times, places and relations. Since Moser conceives the imagistic worlds of dreams and delusions as a form of simulation and transformation of inner self-experience, they represent models for processes of change. They primarily reflect the human being's processes of inner change. This makes them and their study extremely interesting for modern research in psychotherapy.

The technique and interpretation of coding are conveyed to the reader. Coding was developed by Moser and Zeppelin, and validated by Döll-Hentschker (2008), as well as exemplified by dream sequences from one situation to another in psychoanalyses. They then developed the procedure correspondingly for delusions. The categories of the coding are the determination of the positional range, the temporal localization, the spatial localization, the spatial shift (leaving and entering), the situational sequence of the micro-world, the internal structure of the situation, the cognitive processes and models of the self as forms of subject-processors. By analogy with dream analysis, the form of relationship that arises in the situation and the theme of the interaction, for instance, are observed.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Freudian Unconscious and Cognitive Neuroscience: From Unconscious Fantasies to Neural Algorithms by Vesa TalvitieKarnac, London, 2009; 156 pp; £19.99

Johannes Lehtonen

Johannes Lehtonen

Takojantie 1 E, 02130 Espoo, Finland
E-mail: johannes.lehtonen@uku.fi

Johannes Lehtonen  

This interesting book focuses on the nature of unconscious mental processes in the human mind by approaching it from the perspectives of both Freudian unconscious and cognitive neuroscience. The main message the book conveys is a criticism of the psychoanalytical assumption of the existence of unconscious mental processing. According to the author, the concept of mental unconscious cannot be valid and the term should be replaced by neural unconscious.

Talvitie thus positions himself in opposition to a basic element of the psychoanalytical theory of the dynamic functions of the mind. As a representative of a young generation of researchers in the field of the cognitive science approach to psychoanalysis, he is well informed of the problems and topical debates going on in the philosophy of science, especially in the philosophy of mind. He is also well read in the psychoanalytical theory of repression.

The text is mostly fluent and is a pleasure to read. Everyday language is used. Examples from ordinary life are given to illustrate the meaning which make following the ideas presented easy. Talvitie's mastery of the concepts he uses and his ability to work on conceptual and philosophical issues are admirable. He has no difficulty in questioning central metapsychological and philosophical views and suggesting alternative solutions by combining visions from neuroscience and cognitive research.

A number of theoretical issues in the philosophy of mind, its history and cognitive research relevant to psychoanalysis are elucidated.

437

Narcissism lost: On translating and being translated11

Maria Carneiro and Arthur Brakel

Arthur Brakel

Rua Eurico Cruz, #11, Apto 602, Rio de Janeiro, RJ 22461-200, Brazil; Rua David Campista, 80, Rio de Janeiro 22261-010, Brazil — carneirojoao@alternex.com.br

525 Third Street, Ann Arbor, Michigan 48103, USA — brakela@att.net

Arthur Brakel  

authors' viewpoints, Brazil, common interests, culture, English, Portuguese, rhetoric, shared linguistic knowledge, source language, syntax, target language, translation, verb tense, vocabulary

(Final version accepted 11 May 2009)

The authors present a detailed account of the experiences shared in translating and having one's work translated. Carneiro maintains that, in order to communicate with their readers, writers should relinquish the narcissistic satisfaction they derive from their texts in the original. Beyond this, she feels that, owing to a good understanding between her and her translator, the creativity in her original text persists in the translation. Brakel introduces himself to the IJPA readership and shows how he works when translating the cultural and linguistic nuances and peculiarities of Brazilian Portuguese. He concludes with some thoughts about the affect he experiences from his original work and the work he has translated.

Marie Rudden

Marie Rudden  

It may be difficult for some analysts to read a book about the unconscious that describes the collapse of the profession of psychoanalysis, at least within the United States, within its first 11 pages. Or to read a book that valorizes a ‘new’ unconscious plumbed by research conducted largely outside the psychoanalytic field. However, it is well worth the effort. This volume presents current research that supports psychoanalytic premises, and it does so clearly and concisely. It persuasively describes the ‘shared unconscious’ explored by the psychodynamic family and group therapist or organizational consultant.

1028

responseSet.title: Mad and Divine: Spirit and Psyche in the Modern World by Sudhir Kakar University of Chicago Press, Chicago, IL, London, 2009; 178 pp.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Mad and Divine: Spirit and Psyche in the Modern World by Sudhir KakarUniversity of Chicago Press, Chicago, IL, London, 2009; 178 pp.

Jerome Winer

Jerome Winer

122 South Michigan Avenue, Chicago, IL 60603, USA
E-mail: winer@uic.edu

Jerome Winer  

Sudhir Kakar acknowledges that any engagement with the spiritual in psychoanalysis or academic psychology has long been regarded as suspicious and not to be considered seriously. Spiritual notions of an intimate connection between self and not-self are at odds with the rationalist's insistence on an enduring separation between the two. Kakar reminds us that the spiritual vision never lost its ascendancy in many non-Western societies. He claims that psychoanalysts such as Ferenczi, Jung, and, to this reviewer's initial surprise, Kohut, can be seen as representing such a position against the rationalist Freud.

In Kakar's view, there is a recent spiritual revival and probable integration of the spiritual and rational in psychoanalysis:

For me, the spiritual occupies a continuum from moments of self-transcendence marked by a loving connection to an object — nature, art, visions of philosophy or science, the beloved in sexual embrace — to the mystical union of the saints where the sense of the self completely disappears.

(p. 5)

Mad and Divine is Kakar's attempt to contribute to “the coming integration of the spirit and the psyche in the evolving psychology of the person”

(p. 6).

Kakar goes about this task by looking at the lives of holy men and saints from a psychoanalytic perspective and by considering psychoanalytic practice from the perspective of spirituality. The first study in the book is that of the childhood of a popular yet self-serving charismatic guru of the 1970s and 1980s, Rajneesh, later known as Bhagwan (Incarnation of God). After great success in India, legal troubles led him from his ashram there to Oregon. From his missions in India and Oregon, he acquired 50,000 committed followers and 92 Rolls Royces. Sex was emphasized to promote the silence of the mind that enabled true meditation.

In this essay on Rajneesh Kakar employs a careful psychogenetic formulation of key traumatic events (the death of his grandfather) and critical developmental relationships (a more than doting grandmother). However, he fails to account fully for the man's charismatic power. There is no such thing as a charismatic leader in a vacuum. Such leaders need followers and the process that drew thousands to Rajneesh remains largely unexplained. As a psychoanalyst Kakar wonders if Rajneesh's enlightenment stemmed from an extended hypomanic episode. Perhaps, but how does this hypo-manic man wind up with 92 Rolls Royces?

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Pourquoi l'antisémitisme? Et si Freud s'était trompé … Freud, la question paternelle et l'antisémitisme [Why Anti-Semitism? And What if Freud Were Wrong … Freud, the Father Question and Anti-Semitism]1Éditions Pascal, Paris, 2009; 158 pp; €17.50 Jean-Claude Stoloff

Georges Pragier

Georges Pragier

8 rue Boissonade, 75104 Paris, France
E-mail: faurprag@club-internet.fr.

Georges Pragier  

In the après-coup of a trauma that occurred while the young Jean-Claude was in exile with his family in Uruguay, the author, as an adult and a psychoanalyst, now looks back and asks: ‘And what if Freud were wrong?’ At age 8 or 9 years, on Easter Day in Montevideo, as he waits for the friends he plays football with every day, it is another game altogether that is about to unfold. In all good faith, his young fellow pupils brutally make him aware that, like Freud, since “my parents were Jews … I have remained a Jew myself” (Freud, 1925, p. 7). He is therefore accused of complicity in a deicide and forcibly dragged to Church to be ‘cleansed of this crime’. Stoloff remembers having sensed the vital importance of resisting this aggression. Brought up in a secular family that was able to flee France and the persecutions inflicted on Jews, the author emphasizes from the outset the plurality of Judaisms. For him, the religious element is just one of many factors that define him in the Western imagination and he gives an account of his past experience through the filter of his own analytic process.

1Translated by Sophie Leighton.1546

Brian Truckle

Brian Truckle

Somerset Road, Edgbaston, Birmingham B15 2QH
E-mail: btruckle@btpp.co.uk

Brian Truckle  

It may seem strange to begin a review with an account of personal experience but I hope it will become relevant to the argument. Starting school in a

1543

rural country village at age 3 and three-quarters was a frightening experience. On my first day, armed only with the knowledge of how to write my first name in capitals, but with no understanding of what the signs meant, I ventured forth. Until then, most of my life had consisted of playing with the gypsy children in their encampment in the forest, surrounded by caravans, dogs, piebald ponies and singing around the camp-fires.

The day did not go well. I was handed my slate and slate pencil and faced a blackboard with hieroglyphics with no meaning. It was bewildering and alarming. Later, the whole group was assembled for singing songs of which I knew nothing. Then the pianist struck up a tune I knew — Vaughan Williams's ‘Linden Lea’ and at last a faint hope emerged in me. At least now I could sing as I knew the song from the gypsies. My voice rose with the others but there was a problem. They were singing in English and I in Romany.

My experience of working with children and adolescents on the autistic spectrum leads me to believe that this too is their experience — singing the wrong words to the right tune, leaving them too in a state of bewilderment, disappointment, misunderstanding and fear, unable to be understood. Luckily for me, I met Miss Theodora Smith who was somehow able instantly to understand how children feel on their first day and could communicate this both verbally and nonverbally and, somehow, magically transform the hieroglyphics on the blackboard to symbols with meaning, which in turn opened up a glorious world of communication. Of course, I instantly fell in love and remember her with deep gratitude almost a biblical lifetime later.

This book is a collection of papers divided into two sections — on psychoanalytic work with children and with adults within the autistic spectrum. This is now a popular topic and there are many volumes currently available from many theoretical persuasions, and it must be asked whether this book's existence is justified by the quality of the thinking provided. Almost all the papers have been published before, many of them decades ago. Some are by highly distinguished pioneers in the field: others are by those emerging as having a valuable contribution to make. Many contributors are psychoanalysts, some, psychoanalytic child psychotherapists — all of whom have wide experience of working in these fields.

Handbook of Evidence-Based Psychodynamic Psychotherapy: Bridging the Gap Between Science and Practice edited by Raymond A. Levy, J. Stuart AblonHumana Press, New York, NY, 2009; 399 pp; $99.50

Eve Caligor

Eve Caligor

19 East 89th Street, New York, NY 10128, USA
E-mail: eve.caligor@gmail.com

Eve Caligor  

While the relationship of empirical research to psychoanalysis remains controversial for some, for many others it remains simply elusive; analytic clinicians unfamiliar with research methodology and statistics often find the psychotherapy and psychoanalytic research literature difficult to understand, highly specialized, and focused on very particular questions or findings not easily linked to clinical practice. For the group of psychoanalysts and psychodynamic psychotherapists who would like to learn about clinically relevant psychotherapy research but have been daunted by the difficulty of doing so, the Handbook of Evidence-Based Psychodynamic Psychotherapy: Bridging the Gap Between Science and Practice offers a coherent and user-friendly introduction to the field.

The Handbook is composed of 14 chapters, each written by a different group of clinician-researchers. Each chapter is self-contained and can be read on its own; at the same time the chapters are thoughtfully sequenced in relation to one another so that, in reading the book from start to finish, one develops an increasingly coherent and complex appreciation of the material covered. Most chapters follow a standard format. Chapters begin with a review of the relevant theory about the topic being discussed, followed by presentation of empirical research, and ending with clinical examples that highlight the clinical implications of the material presented. Chapters are written for clinicians: research methodology and statistical analyses are explained clearly, and chapters are relatively short, easy to read and emphasize research questions of interest to clinicians and potentially relevant to clinical theory and technique. The clinician-reader is likely to take away from each chapter at least one new thought, question or technique to consider in the course of clinical work.

Hélène Parat  

This work is the fruit of research that has extended over about 15 years, and is designed to serve as a tool for reading Freud's work. This aim has been achieved remarkably well. It is devoted exclusively to the analysis of Freudian terms for they are the source of the subsequent theorizations: for this reason, it is not an umpteenth dictionary of psychoanalysis, for, even if certain post-Freudian concepts are touched upon in the discussion of the terms, it centres on the Freudian concepts explored in the corpus itself. It is perhaps closer to a thematic encyclopaedia than a dictionary, but it nonetheless has alphabetical headwords and multiple references allowing the reader to navigate without difficulty from one entry to another.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On: Transference

Aldo Duarte , Alice Lewkowicz , Anna Kauffmann , Eneida Iankilevich , Gisha Brodacz , Gustavo Soares , Luiz Pellanda and Viviane Mondrzak

Viviane Mondrzak

E-mail: mondrzak@terra.com.br

Viviane Mondrzak  

Ruth Stein, a member of IJP's North American Editorial Board since 2007, died suddenly in January, 2010. Ruth was an analyst and a scholar whose interests and experience were truly international, bridging diverse cultures as well as a number of theoretical traditions. Her vitality and her sparkling, challenging presence enlivened the Board's deliberations; she will be deeply missed.

Dear Editors

During the past three years, the Study Group of Psychoanalytic Epistemology of the SPPA has revised the status of certain concepts (the unconscious, trauma and causality) within the current psychoanalytical theory, in the light of a perspective of complexity. Following our plan of study, we have focused our attention on the topic ‘transference’.

Revising articles published in the IJP, it seems that currently this term incorporates, besides Freud's traditional concept, all the later development in the patient—analyst relationship, raising an immediate question. When we talk about transference, a central concept in psychoanalyses, are we all referring to the same ‘transference’? We can set an example, among many others, from an article written by Thomas Ogden, an author well known in Brazil, published in 2004, The art of psychoanalysis: Dreaming undreamt dreams and interrupted cries. The author's understanding of the psychoanalytical process, presented in this paper, does not include the term transference. It can be found in this passage, though: “A principal subject of the dialogue that takes place in the analytic situation concerns the patient's anxieties and defenses arising in response to the relationship of analyst and analysand at an unconscious level (the transference—contratransference)” (Ogden, 2004, p. 864). In this context, it works as a synonym for the relationship of analyst—analysand. Soon after, he states that transference—countertransference (always as a pair) is partly viewed from a historical perspective (both from the patient's life and that of the analysis). Here, transference is inserted in a temporal, historical perspective, even though it is not referred only to the patient's life history. What we intended to highlight from this example, independently of its theoretical discussion concerning what has been said, is the multiple use of the term ‘transference’.

Psychanalyse et Psychose no. 9, 2009: ‘L énigme de la réalité’ [Psychoanalysis and Psychosis: ‘The Enigma of Reality’]1

Benoit Servant

Benoit Servant

53 Bd Henri Sellier
92150 Suresnes
E-mail: benoit.y.servant@wanadoo.fr

Benoit Servant  

Psychanalyse et Psychose is the annual publication of the évelyne and Jean Kestemberg Centre, a Centre for Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis created by René Angelergues and Jean Kestemberg within the Mental Health Association of the 13th district in Paris (with which the names of R. Diatkine, S. Lébovici and P.-C. Racamier are associated, insofar as they successfully introduced psychoanalysis there within a public sector psychiatric structure in the 1950's).

évelyne Kestemberg, who was the first director of the Centre for Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis, belongs to the second generation of French psychoanalysts who were involved in psychiatric care structures, and who were thus led to think about psychosis psychoanalytically. A particular feature of her theoretical and clinical contribution is the notion of ‘cold (or non-delusional) psychosis’, in which the narcissistic-object conflict is resolved by an attempt to reify the object.

The E. and J. Kestemberg Centre, currently run by Alain Gibeault, aims to pursue the initial ambition of its founders of giving psychotic patients an alternative space to that of psychiatry, with a view to establishing possible links between psychoanalysis and psychosis.

The Review Psychoanalyse et Psychose, whose chief editor is Liliane Abensour, seeks to reflect the questions, reflections and clinical papers arising from the Centre's activity, associating the texts of its own practitioners with those of colleagues from other treatment structures with a similar orientation, and sometimes with classical texts, each volume being devoted to a specific theme.

The theme of the latest volume, ‘The Enigma of Reality’, invites us to reflect on an essential question, because while psychosis is currently defined by the loss of the sense of reality, psychoanalysis itself aims to recognize the full value of psychical reality.

The introduction by Liliane Abensour, ‘Psychical reality, material reality’, provides an overview of the main issues of debate, while referring the reader to the different articles in the volume.

L'Annuel de l'APF 2009: Quelle guérison? Mal, malaise, maladie1 [APF Annual, 2009] PUF, Paris, 2009; 200 pp; €24.00

Bertrand Colin

Bertrand Colin

66 Avenue Des Gobelins, 75013 Paris, France
E-mail: bcolin002@gmail.com

Bertrand Colin  

This is the third issue of an annual from the Association Psychanalytique de France. Like its two predecessors, it brings together contributions on the basis of a set of related ideas; in this case, the notions of mal [evil/ailment] and malaise and maladie [illness] serve to place the concept of cure under scrutiny. A stimulus to debate is provided by contrasting two propositions of Freud's, both formulated in Analysis terminable and interminable. The first views psychoanalysis as doing what needs to be done when the patient's life itself has failed to do it. The second proposition is that psychoanalysis gives rise to a “newly created state” in the ego, a state which clearly distinguishes “a person who has been analysed” from one who has not (Freud, 1937, p. 227. The first stresses the psychotherapeutic dimension of psychoanalysis, the second the ‘cultural work’2 to which the psychoanalytical quest contributes. This implies a live connection between psychotherapy and philosophy, a connection upon which each of the contributors to this collection seeks to base the originality of the psychoanalytical process.

Before going into detail, it is worth briefly reviewing the plan of this volume, the unity of whose theme makes it more of a coherent work than a journal in the usual sense.

A first section, ‘Entretiens' [Exchanges] comprises three presentations, each followed by a rejoinder. The first presentation, by Patrick Mcrot, tackles the question of truth, asking what definition of truth would be consistent with its having heuristic value in our understanding of the curative process. In his rejoinder, Jacques André makes the provocative suggestion that truth might be what the patient needs to be cured of. A second presentation, by Jean-Francois Daubech, links the process of cure to ‘creativity’ along the road, the long road, that psychoanalytical ‘research’ requires as opposed to the short cuts of psychotherapy. Jean-Claude Rolland's response extends Daubech's thesis by showing how much the most practical of psychoanalytical procedures owe to Freud's most speculative flights. The third presentation, by André Beetschen, explores how much the curative process owes to unpleasure. Catherine Chabert responds by adding the ‘melancholic’ model to the neurotic one in an attempt to capture the full complexity of the notion of unpleasure.

Rosenfeld in Retrospect edited by John SteinerRoutledge, London, 2008; 153 pp; $34.95

Henry Smith

Henry Smith

17 Hammond Street
Cambridge, MA 02138, USA
E-mail: henryfsmith@gmail.com

Henry Smith  

Five of the first six chapters in this book were presentations at a conference that took place in London in April 2000 and are published essentially unmodified. These include three papers, by Edna O'Shaughnessy, Ronald Britton and Riccardo Steiner, and two discussions, by Michael Feldman and Hanna Segal. Following these, the editor, John Steiner, contributes a comprehensive review of Rosenfeld's major papers. As Steiner notes, all six authors knew Rosenfeld personally, and the first section of the book gives glimpses into Rosenfeld's character alongside his theoretical contributions.

The second part of the book consists of the republication of four of Rosenfeld's papers without commentary beyond Steiner's reflections on them in his chapter in Part One. The papers chosen span the years 1947-1971, stopping short of Rosenfeld's later work from 1978-1987. The decision is a deliberate one. As Steiner explains, in his later papers Rosenfeld revised much of his earlier thinking and, when faced with an impasse, put much greater emphasis on the analyst's contributions to the difficulties, including the analyst's projections into the patient.

While Steiner sees Rosenfeld's later focus on the analyst as a valid part of an analyst's approach, and has adapted some of it for his own concept of ‘analyst-centered interpretations’ (Steiner, 1994), he feels that Rosenfeld's later work lacks the subtlety and balance of his earlier papers, “when he could so wonderfully put himself in the patient's position without losing his analytic perspective” (p. 80). This view is shared by many contemporary British Kleinians. What the optimal balance is between attention to the patient's and the analyst's contributions is a debatable one, with opinions that vary not only from analyst to analyst and school to school, but from one analysis to another — or even one phase of analysis to another — within a given analyst's practice.

The first chapter by Edna O'Shaughnessy is a beautiful and detailed description, including four hours in sequence, of her analysis of a man whose desire to invade the analyst results in his fear of invasion by the analyst, thus illustrating one of Rosenfeld's enduring observations. O'Shaughnessy also illustrates Rosenfeld's view that, as she puts it: “There are degrees of intrusion into the object; intrusion is not always ‘all the way’” (p. 4). This emphasis on degrees and types of key phenomena, including projective identification and envy, is echoed throughout the book and is one of its strongest features, as it is of Rosenfeld's work. In that regard the book is a living testament to Rosenfeld's legacy.

Psychoanalytic Theories of Creativity: Clinical Applications11

Nellie Thompson and Laurie Wilson

Laurie Wilson

520 West 114th Street, #21, New York, NY 10025, USA - nelliet100@aol.com

Laurie Wilson  

Psychoanalysts have long been fascinated by creative individuals. The papers on this panel explored the ongoing engagement by psychoanalysts with the phenomenon of creativity. This engagement has enriched psychoanalytic theory while the experience of working with creative individuals has deepened our clinical understanding of issues that are ubiquitous in clinical practice.

Laurie Wilson's paper, The contribution of Ernst Kris's theory of creativity to clinical work, focused on his concept of ‘regression in the service of the ego’: how his concept evolved with his own development as a psychoanalyst, how it has been used by psychoanalysts who found it both clinically useful and theoretically foundational, and how the concept has been taken in a new direction by analysts who found the concept problematic and have developed better ways of understanding ‘primary and secondary process’ both in their studies of creativity and clinical work.

Kris, an art historian, had been looking at art and thinking about artists for at least a decade by the time he formulated the term ‘regression in the service of the ego’ in his paper, Psychology of caricature (Kris, 1952[1934]). Considering the creativity of caricaturists and artists he wrote:

We have now to elucidate in greater detail the relations of wit and caricature to dreams: in dreams, the ego abandons its supremacy and the primary process obtains control, whereas in wit and in caricature this process remains in the service of the ego. This formulation alone suffices to show that the problem involved is a more general one; the contrast between an ego overwhelmed by regression and a ‘regression in the service of the ego.

(Kris, 1952[1934], p. 177)

Though not initially noted, Kris's view that creative individuals are more flexible with regard to ego functioning and that they use earlier (more primitive) modes of functioning to gain access to preconscious thoughts for creative activity began the move away from a strict equation of regression with pathology. By emphasizing the positive, useful aspects of regression for artists Kris opened the door to new ways of understanding it.

[[RunningHead]]

Itinerari e figure della passione [Itineraries and Figures of Passion]1 by Giorgio Sassanelli Antigone Edizioni, Torino, 2008; 307 pp; €19

Gianluigi Monniello

Gianluigi Monniello

Via Cornelio Celso, 18, 00161 Rome, Italy
E-mail: gianluigi.monniello@tin.it

Gianluigi Monniello  

Giorgio Sassanelli's latest book, Itinerari e figure della passione [Itineraries and Figures of Passion], investigates in depth the experience of passion - both loving and vindictive passion - highlighting its aspects which are vital to psychic life. Passion is recognized as a catalyst for projects of change and development, even those which are liable to fail. It is “understood both as a specific and unique type of relationship between the subject and the object, and as a new and complex restructuring of experience, which has a totalizing character and is capable of giving new meaning to existence” (p. 281):

In this sense, real passion is an experience which organizes the life of an individual, that is to say his thoughts, affects and behaviour, in relation to a desired or loathed object, an other to the self, who becomes the essential and unifying theme of the person, defining not only his identity but also his vision of the world. In passion, the dominant force of desire (erotic or aggressive) activates an emotivity, multifaceted and rich with fantasies, defined by its intensity and by its high level of arousal.

1Translated by Harriet Cooper.1319

responseSet.title: Film Essay: Love and hate in dementia: The depressive position in the film Iris
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Film Essay: Love and hate in dementia: The depressive position in the film Iris11

Daniel Anderson

Daniel Anderson

Glan Traeth Day Hospital, 2 Alexandra Road, Rhyl, LL18 9EA, Wales
aniel.anderson@wales.nhs.uk

Daniel Anderson  

Introduction

Film plays a major role in contemporary society, often providing us with glimpses into other worlds and lives to which ordinarily we would not have access. Such is its influence that it can have a significant impact on popular opinion, and there is a heavy duty in the role of the director, writers and actors to achieve the right balance for fear of producing detrimental effects and biases. It is important that film achieves the right balance between discussing a particular issue without diluting it down to something mediocre or sensationalizing it beyond all realistic expectations.

The recent film Iris (Director: Richard Eyre, 2002, UK) has raised the challenging issue of dementia. Central to this film is how a couple tries to adapt their relationship to the continuing demands placed upon them by the changed personality and declining ability of a loved one with dementia. Dementia is a common and currently incurable condition which cruelly robs sufferers and their loved ones of a common history together, personality, love, intimacy and, ultimately, life. It is considered by some to be a normal part of becoming old, which it is not, but it is common and disturbing enough for it to be thought of in such ways as a means to avoid the distress of its underlying pathology and the distress of those who suffer with it. In some respects it offers disturbing glimpses into our most raw and primitive fears of losing our minds, becoming incapacitated and dependent. It threatens to shatter our sense of unity within the ego, blurring demarcations between what is self and non-self. Dementia should not be considered as a simple return to infancy through a reversal of human development, but it does share some similarities to infantile states that prove useful when considering its nature in advanced states.

The stigma of a mental illness like dementia, and ageing per se, can evoke powerful reactions in those observing films about it which are taken back to the wider community influencing opinions. Iris attempts to take this onboard by examining the effects of dementia upon the sufferer, their relationship and the wider community.

responseSet.title: Shifting the Perspective after the Patient's Response to an Interpretation
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Shifting the Perspective after the Patient's Response to an Interpretation

Anssi Peräkylä

Anssi Peräkylä

00014 Helsinki, Finland — anssi.perakyla@helsinki.fi

Anssi Peräkylä  

conversation analysis, interpretation, listening, research

(Final version accepted 9 March 2010)

Psychoanalytic interpretation is normally understood as a sequence of two utterances: the analyst gives an interpretation and the patient responds to it. This paper suggests that, in the interpretative sequence, there is also a third utterance where psychoanalytic work takes place. This third interpretative turn involves the analyst's action after the patient's response to the interpretation. Using conversation analysis as method in the examination of audio-recorded psychoanalytic sessions, the paper will explicate the psychoanalytic work that gets done in third interpretative, turns. Through it, the analyst takes a stance towards the patient's understandings of the interpretation, which are shown in the patient's response to the interpretation. The third interpretative turns on one hand ratify and accept the patient's understandings, but, in addition to that, they also introduce a shift of perspective relative to them. In most cases, the shift of perspective is implicit but sometimes it is made explicit. The shifts of perspective bring to the foreground aspects or implications of the interpretation that were not incorporated in the patient's response. They recast the description of the patient's experience by showing new layers or more emotional intensity in it. The results are discussed in the light of Faimberg's concept of listening to listening and Schlesinger's concept of follow-up interpretation.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On: Realism and Research in Psychoanalysis, by D. Bell and R. Wallerstein

René Epstein

René Epstein

BAPS, Buenos Aires, Argentina
E-mail: reneps@sion.com

René Epstein  

Dear Editors

I would like to comment on R. Wallerstein's (2009) and D. Bell's (2009) papers (without overlooking C. Hanly's specifications on the basics of truth, in the same Education Section as Bell's paper). These two articles can be seen as a junktim, given the addition of a few, somewhat newer epistemological ideas useful to consider the problems related to psychoanalysis as a science.

D. Bell is quite straightforward when he claims that “a peculiar new orthodoxy” (2009, p. 332) is the result of the challenge to the older modernistic orthodoxy, meaning that the “relativization of ‘truth”’ of postmodernism results in proposing that “‘… there is no such thing as truth”’ (2009). He shows that, instead of solving complexities, there appears a new positivistic stance, a new dyadic exclusiveness of a ‘positive and negative’ kind.

D. Bell makes an accurate synthesis of the crucial epistemological discovery of Freud (germane with Marx's proposal), and the fact that “social theorists” have, subject to “scrutiny”, the “link between what is held to be knowledge” and “a powerful tendency to substitute for truth wishful illusions”. He adds: “Truths about the world, which may be very hard to come by, do not depend on perspective” (2009). So, every “… explanatory structure is still underpinned by a conception of truth. Uncovering ideology and self-deception brings a world that is broader, truer than the one that preceded this understanding” (2009). Furthermore, he claims that: “The relativization of ‘truth’ brings not a recognition that there are no ultimate truths”, but a claim that “‘therefore there is no such thing as truth”’ and “therefore there are no facts” (2009). So there appears a “celebration of multiplicity, pluralism and the abandonment of a conception of truthfulness” (p. 334).

Bell also describes the philosophical idealism of postmodernism and goes one step further, showing that it also denies the possibility of searching for historical continuity, i.e. the developmental perspective of psychoanalysis. He points to “confusion between complexity and relativism” (p. 340). Finally, he claims for the necessity of “realist ontology” (p. 341).

responseSet.authorMast: Antoine Corel and  Michael I. Good
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Clinical View on the Directions of Time: Here and Now, the Past in the Present, from the Present to the Past11

Antoine Corel and Michael Good

Michael Good

74 Craftsland Road, Chestnut Hill, MA 02467, USA - michaeligood@pol.net

Michael Good  

The nature of time and psychic function is one of the most clinically complex, intriguing, and debated issues in psychoanalysis (Birksted-Breen, 2003; Faimberg, 2007; Good, 1998). In introducing the panel, Dr. Antoine Corel noted that in Freud's various writings involving the problem of psychic time there were two aspects. The first concerns the nature of the traces left by psychic events, in particular those that were never conscious and so could not be recollected but still had psychic effect. These might be constructed by the analyst and thereby serve to ‘recover a fragment of lost experience.’ The second aspect concerns the fundamental concept of après-coup [Nachträglichkeit] as a dialectical relationship between two times, a second time leaning on, and giving retroactive meaning to, a first time.

The first paper, given by Michael I. Good, was entitled The present in the past: A presumptive ‘case of childhood incest’ as seen through the lens of screen reconstruction. Although Freud considered unconscious mental processes to be timeless - with past, present, and future representationally merged - unconscious mechanisms may still act defensively upon the demarcation of time either in the memory of experience or in the (re)construction of the past. Just as formerly recalled screen memories may be remembered again in the course of psychoanalysis, so new screen phenomena can arise during the treatment process. Screen (re)construction is a type of screening with inputs from both patient and analyst that can shift the focus away from intense here-and-now interactions of the analytic present to the patient's past. A mutually determined process, both patient and analyst gradually consider the resulting screen to be convincing and valid, even though it may, for the most part, be significantly distorted or fictitious due to the screen function. Like a screen memory having some veridical content yet serving a masking function, a screen construction could have elements of truth. At the same time such constructions may function as a strong resistance or compromise against intense erotic, sadomasochistic, or narcissistic themes in the transference/countertransference.

responseSet.title: The Work of Psychoanalysts in the Public Health Sector edited by Mary Brownescombe Heller, Sheena Pollet Routledge, London, 2010; 212 pp; £22.99
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Work of Psychoanalysts in the Public Health Sector edited by Mary Brownescombe Heller, Sheena Pollet Routledge, London, 2010; 212 pp; £22.99

Joan Schachter

Joan Schachter

22 Dyne Road, London NW6 7XE, UK
E-mail: joan.schachter@virgin.net

Joan Schachter  

This book represents the successful outcome of an idea and an ambition to publicize the current work of psychoanalysts in the public health sector. It is dedicated to William Brough and Richard Lucas, both psychiatrists and psychoanalysts working for many years in the National Health Service, who died before the book was published.

663

The book contains a varied selection of papers in which psychoanalysts describe how their analytic training and experience inform their work in the public sector across the UK. This is particularly relevant in an era when psychoanalytic psychotherapy and its applications are under threat alongside increasing fragmentation of services, with the resulting stresses on staff working in pressured environments with complex cases, often without sufficient support and training. The editors point out that there has been a long and distinguished tradition of psychoanalytic involvement in the public sector. However, it was surprising to notice the absence, in their list of earlier pioneers, of Donald Winnicott, Clifford Yorke, Anna Freud and Tom Main, all of whom have made major contributions to the treatment of children, families and adults within the public sector.

The editors' stated aim is to portray some of the ways in which psychoanalytic thinking and practice continue to flourish and to contribute to the day-to-day work of the NHS in the UK. Certainly the range of work described fulfils this aim, covering clinical work with parents, children, adolescents, adults and their families, the contributions of psychoanalytic thinking within the acute sector of the NHS, and psychoanalytic support and teaching offered to health service staff. Perhaps the only omission concerns work with the ethnically diverse populations and refugees who constitute an increasing number of referred patients.

The book is organized into four parts: clinical work with parents and children, clinical work with adults and their families, analytic thinking in health service practice, and analytic support to health service staff. All the chapters are briefly summarized in the Introduction. The content of the chapters varies: a description of clinical services offered to patients and staff, a discussion of the relevance of the psychoanalytic training and experience in supporting work within the public sector, a description of mentalization-based treatment for severe borderline disorders, and an overview of a course in psychoanalytic concepts offered to mental health workers, some of whom may be stimulated to obtain further training.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Internal and External Reality11

Elias Barros and Carmen Mion

Carmen Mion

Rua Dr Alceu de Campos Rodrigues, 275/101 04544-000, São Paulo, Brazil - carmenmion@uol.com.br

Carmen Mion  

Traditionally IPA Congresses have been opportunities to exchange convergent and divergent theories and experiences between analysts from all over the world. However, the main theme of the Chicago Congress 2009, ‘Psychoanalytical Practices: Convergences and Divergences’, brought the different ways in which psychoanalysts listen and conduct a psychoanalytic process to the center of research efforts and plenary discussions. The challenge here is to identify a common ground not only among different theories but taking account of the complexities of the analysts’ different regions and cultures. The apparently basic psychoanalytical concepts of Internal and External Reality, this panel's topic, do not escape from the diversity of underlying assumptions. As Elias Rocha Barros mentioned, this panel was suggested by CAPSA not only to promote inter-regional data but also because it allows several other themes to be addressed.

The panel began with Jorge Maldonado's lively presentation of clinical material based on two analytical sessions with a patient he has been working with for three and half years, four sessions weekly. The presentation of the clinical case primarily addressed the difficulty his patient had in exploring her fantasy world as a consequence of anxiety. Maldonado puts forth the view that various disorders may disturb the process of symbolization when there is an excess of anxiety (Klein, 1930). As a consequence, instead of symbol formation we have symbolic equations or, as in the case of his patient, it may result in an inability to use the symbols the ego has already constructed.

The second line of Maldonado's presentation addressed the patient's tendency to make rationalizations and intellectualizations. He believes that these defenses work as a hard, artificial outer shell preventing her from feeling anxiety but also blocking her creativity and progress in the analytical relationship.

Maldonado worked from the hypothesis that in the first session there is a clear dialectic between external reality, represented by a surgical procedure the patient would undergo the following day perceived by her as traumatic, and the internal infantile fantasies and fears linked to her sexuality though attributed to the surgery.

responseSet.title: Commentary on Pierre Marty's The Narcissistic Difficulties Presented to the Observer by the Psychosomatic Problem
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Commentary on Pierre Marty's The Narcissistic Difficulties Presented to the Observer by the Psychosomatic Problem

Richard Gottlieb

Richard Gottlieb

263 West End Avenue, Apt. 1-E, New York, NY 10023, USA — rmgottlieb@aol.com

Richard Gottlieb  

I was asked by the Editors if I would comment on Pierre Marty's ‘key paper’ from the point of view of someone from ‘a different tradition.’ After reading The narcissistic difficulties presented to the observer by the psychosomatic problem, I readily assented for four reasons. First, because, despite the work of McDougall (1978, 1989) during the 1970s and 1980s, Oliner (1988), Aisenstein (2006, 2008), my own paper (Gottlieb, 2003), and a few others (e.g. Simpson, 2003, pp. 700, 706-7), the work of the locally influential Paris psychosomaticien school of psychoanalysts is still little known outside of France. Few works by the founders of this branch of psychosomatic theory and treatment have been published in English. Even the important early paper of Marty and de M'Uzan (1963) that established the importance of the concept of la pensée opératoire (a form of concrete, non-self-reflective, stimulus-bound thought) had never appeared in English, so that, when I was researching the subject in 2002, I had to prepare my own translation. In introducing his recent English translation of a paper of De M'Uzan (2003[1984]), Simpson attributed the relative lack of representation of this French school in the anglophone psychoanalytic world to “difficulties in translation, both linguistic and cultural” (Simpson, 2003, p. 699)

Secondly, the psychosomaticien school has by now been continuously around for more than 50 years and is well into its ‘second generation’ of theorists and clinicians. Moreover, like similar efforts in Germany and elsewhere — but not in the United States — psychoanalytic psychosomatics in France enjoys the financial support of the national government. The Pierre Marty Institute was founded in 1972. The Pierre Marty Hospital (an outpatient facility founded in 1978 as the Poternes-des-Peupliers Hospital and located in Paris) employs a large number of psychoanalysts who work within the theoretical framework of the psychosomaticien school. Government support has facilitated vital and ongoing clinical work and legitimated referrals to these analysts by medical and surgical specialists. This in turn has resulted in a significant accumulation of clinical experience with their ideas and therapeutic practices. Such an accumulation under one theoretical roof is unusual if not unique in psychoanalytic psychosomatics today and surely deserves our attention.

responseSet.title: Letter from Jerusalem
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Letter from Jerusalem

H. Erlich

H. Erlich

E-mail: Shmuel.Erlich@huji.ac.il

H. Erlich  

The Israel Psychoanalytic Society (IPS) is a thriving society. Its membership has more than doubled in the last decade and there is constant demand for the training it provides (about 12-15 new candidates admitted annually). Its members teach, publish, train and lecture, and a good number are active on the international scene. At the same time, the IPS has in the last two decades been a society torn from within by acute theoretical divisions and seemingly contradictory postures with far-reaching divergent clinical and therapeutic implications.

Historical, Social and Cultural Background

There are deep historical currents as well as emotional ties connecting the IPS with the colossal tragedy that befell European Jewry in the 20th century. In numerous ways this parallels and partakes of the history of the emergence and development of the State of Israel. Although the Zionist movement and Jewish settlement in the land (then Palestine, first under Ottoman, later under British Mandate rule) preceded the Holocaust by at least half a century, it is unquestionable that the persecution and destruction of Jewish life in Europe by Nazi Germany and its allies and events subsequent to World War II played a major role in accelerating the creation of Israel as an independent state, both from within and from without. From within, Jewish presence in the land was augmented by the massive arrival of refugees who had survived the war and death camps; and from without, the same tragic events contributed to the nearly universal readiness in 1948 to create a Jewish alongside a Palestinian state.

The psychoanalytic parallel to these events can be seen in the tender beginnings of interest in psychoanalysis of a small group in the 1920s and 1930s. This received a tremendous impetus with the arrival of Max Eitingon in 1933. Eitingon came to Palestine after he was forced by the rising Nazi tide in Germany to resign his membership in the Berlin Society and Institute, which he had created with Karl Abraham in 1920, and which served as prototype for subsequent models of psychoanalytic training. Several other European Jewish analysts arrived in Palestine at about the same time. In the spring of 1933 Eitingon founded the Palestine Psychoanalytic Society (later renamed the Israel Psychoanalytic Society) and in 1934 it was accepted into the IPA as a component society.1

[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysts Translating Psychoanalysts Today11

Jean-Michel Quinodoz and Emad Bishara

Emad Bishara

10, rue Conseil-Général, 1205 Geneva, Switzerland — ebishara@hin.ch

Emad Bishara  

This panel on translation was both very stimulating and rewarding; its theme addressed the main topic of the congress in a very striking manner. Translation has emerged as a bridge that enhances communication. While sometimes it makes use of divergences in a creative manner; it touches what is foreign, different and less conscious. Thus it sheds a new light on some psychoanalytic terms and leads to further insights.

In his opening remarks, the Chair, Jean-Michel Quinodoz (Geneva), Editor-in-Chief of the New Annuals of the International Journal of Psychoanalysis, highlighted the aim of the Annuals to extend the readership of high quality psychoanalytic papers to a non-English-speaking audience in order to favour the communication between psychoanalysts of different cultures. He recalled that the first Annuals were published in Portuguese and Spanish in the early 1980s, then successively in French, Italian, German and Turkish in the 2000s, and a Russian Annual is expected next year. Quinodoz emphasized how fascinating this project is even though its implementation requires a lot of work and dedication on the part of colleagues who for most of the time translate on a voluntary basis.

Bella Habip (Istanbul), a member of the Turkish editorial board, spoke about the challenging and enriching situation of introducing psychoanalytical literature to a reader not familiar with the psychoanalytic culture. In such a context, she explained, the main difficulty is not loyalty to the original text, but rather conveying the concepts of an author to a reader who is not acquainted with the author's other writings. She then described the complex process of the choice and sometimes the creation of the right term in Turkish. She concluded that she experienced translation not merely as a challenge, but also as an experience of restoring her native language by enriching it with new concepts; acquiring a new psychoanalytic language when it does not exist …

Lilli Gast (Berlin), in a text written conjointly with Angela Mauss-Hanke, spoke of a completely different context: as German is the language in which psychoanalysis originated, translating psychoanalysis from other languages into German introduces specific problems because some terms lived a life on their own in another language. For instance, ‘après-coup’

[[RunningHead]]

Notes on the Beating Fantasy11

François Sirois

François Sirois

Canadian Psychoanalytic Society, 1020 Bougainville, Quebec City, Quebec G1S 3A8, Canada — sirois.rondeau@sympatico.ca

François Sirois  

development, metapsychology, symptoms

(Final version accepted 18 August 2009)

This theoretical paper revisits the heating fantasy, which constitutes a crossroads of the psychic economy in that it condenses three primal phantasies, namely the primal scene, castration and seduction. Two forms of the phantasy have been distinguished: a ‘fixed’ form, apparently associated with the masochistic perversion, and a ‘transitory’ form, probably bound up with libidinal development. In Freud's (1919) paper these two aspects are intertwined. The present contribution confines itself to the transitory form of the phantasy and its significance in the libidinal development of the girl, notably in the organization of passivity. With this in mind, particular attention is paid to the phantasy's third phase in this context, and an attempt made to show how this phase epitomizes the transformation of the instinctual pressure and might therefore be looked upon in this connection as the intermediate phase of the phantasy.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Making a Difference in Patients' Lives: Emotional Experience in the Therapeutic Setting by Sandra BuechlerRoutledge: New York, 2008: 317 pp; £23.95

Daria Colombo

Daria Colombo

4033 East Madison Street, Seattle WA 98112, USA
E-mail: Dcolombo8@gmail.com

Daria Colombo  

An overview, exploration and celebration of her life's work, Sandra Beuchler's book is a passionate exegesis about what she believes to be the core

674

and curative potential of the therapeutic relationship. This work, published as volume eight in a series entitled Psychoanalysis in a New Key, with series editor Donnel Stern, is Beuchler's second in this series: she also authored volume I: Clinical Values: Emotions that Guide Psychoanalytic Treatment. Communicating a very intimate and vivid sense of her work as an analyst, Buechler describes emotion theory and how it shapes her interpersonal perspective, and proposes that a full understanding and deployment of therapeutic impact is best served from within this framework. She draws on C.E. Izard's work in describing a biologically encoded series of distinct emotions and “the fundamental belief that each emotion deserves to be understood as a different human experience.” (p. xiii) She sets forth what she believes are the potentially transformative aspects of treatment, and builds upon her view of therapy as contrast, relational challenge, and catalyst. Her voice is a lively. honest, and compelling one, and at times quite moving, as she weaves together an exposition of her theoretical principles and techniques with clinical scenarios, moments from poetry and literature, and meditations about mortality.

Buechler accomplishes three major tasks very effectively. She clearly describes emotion theory and shows how it serves as the scaffold and spirit of her interpersonal approach. She presents an extremely fine-grained, thoughtful and almost jarringly intimate view of the nuances of the transference and countertransference feelings that can arise in treatment, in supervision, and in psychoanalytic training situations. Her observations about supervision in particular are thoughtful contributions that should be required reading for those on either side of the supervisory dyad. Finally, and perhaps most importantly, she lays forth very openly how she thinks and practices. Beuchler states near the end of her work, “I would suggest that the fear of being seen as providing an oversimplifying, watered down, superficial version of psychoanalytic praxis has probably discouraged many of us from writing about the process at all. I think that is a costly mistake.” (p. 198) She goes on to say “Like any other identity formation, the process of analytic training requires someone to be willing to provide a clear enough demonstration of a way (not the way) to function, so that the learner can formulate a personal style that is similar in some ways and differs in others.” (p. 198) So Beuchler, in providing such close and detailed access to her own process, gives us something quite valuable.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Letter from London

Julia Fabricius

Julia Fabricius

39 Willes Road, London NW5 3DN, UK
E-mail: julia@fabricius.co.uk

Julia Fabricius  

I start with a disclaimer about the title: in two different ways this is not exactly a letter from London. Firstly, the British Psychoanalytic Society (BPAS) was until recently the only IPA psychoanalytic society in London but now there is a second society, the British Psychoanalytic Association (BPA). Although I will try to tell you something of the new society, this letter is primarily from the BPAS. Secondly, a letter from the BPAS is not only a letter from London; we are actually a British society rather than a London one and I will also tell you something about our efforts to promote and maintain psychoanalysis beyond London.

The British Psychoanalytic Society is of course one with a long tradition. Over the generations since our founding in 1913, we have enjoyed and benefited from the stellar contributions of some of the greatest minds in psychoanalysis. In 2010 London psychoanalysts still feel a sense of privilege at our history and tradition and at what we continue to learn from our gifted senior colleagues. But this sense is tempered by a number of realizations, which could perhaps all be related to the necessity of facing reality, mourning and working through. It is wonderful that we have world-renowned members still making scientific contributions in their 70s and 80s. But for those of us following there are questions: how will we fare without them? will we be able to continue with anything like the level of creativity? In addition, there are the challenges around us, challenges which are being faced by psychoanalytic societies world-wide and have been well described elsewhere. In London we share in the concern about the future of our discipline. Yet there is also a sense that being challenged may not be entirely bad: we are forced to look outwards, to be less complacent, to engage with criticisms some of which may be valid, to get ideas from each other internationally about how to tackle the difficulties.

In this letter I will try to set out a selection of what is going on in London, to bring you up-to-date with our news. But it must be said that I am offering a personal view. I have tried to check on the facts but the selections of the facts, as well as the opinions, are my own. I will not focus here on the measures to reach out to health professionals and the public; this is an area at which, like many other IPA societies, we are trying to work away, usually attracting good audiences to our public events, courses and our new foundation course (aimed largely at those interested in applying for training).

responseSet.title: Freud and the Hammerschlag Family: A Formative Relationship
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Freud and the Hammerschlag Family: A Formative Relationship11

Gerhard Fichtner

Gerhard Fichtner

Goethestr. 6, D 72076 Tübingen, Germany - gerhard.fichtner@uni-tuebingen.de

Gerhard Fichtner  

Hammerschlag, Samuel; Hammerschlag, Anna; Hammerschlag, Paul; Hammerschlag, Hammerschlag, Albert; Hammerschlag, Emil; Freud letters

(Final version accepted 27 July 2009)

From his obituary of Samuel Hammerschlag, we know of Freud's great veneration for his teacher of Jewish religion. However, not only Hammerschlag himself but his whole family had a formative influence on young Freud, who was deeply impressed by their humanity. This paper describes Freud's relationships with all the family members. In particular, it shows how warmly he felt towards the only daughter. Anna Hammerschlag, who was his patient for a while and whom he chose as a godmother for his youngest daughter Anna. By virtue of the crucial role she played in Freud's ‘specimen dream’ of July 1895 (’Irma's injection’), she also became as it were the godmother of Freud's magnum opus. The Interpretation of Dreams. All the known extant letters from Freud to members of the Hammerschlag family are published here for the first time in English translation.

responseSet.title: Comparative Perspectives on the Unconscious Inclinical Work
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Comparative Perspectives on the Unconscious Inclinical Work11

Ron Spielman

Ron Spielman

121 Leinster Street, Paddington, 2021, Australia — ronspi@ozemail.com.au

Ron Spielman  

In introducing the panel, Ron Spielman stated the obvious, namely that ‘the Unconscious’ is not only the bedrock of our clinical work — it is the bedrock of our profession. This has been so since Freud's initially disturbing assertion to the world that ‘The ego is not master in its own house’ and has continued to disturb the world ever since.

The topic of this select panel was “Comparative Perspectives on the Unconscious in Clinical Work’ having of course to do with the dynamic unconscious … that ‘compartment’ of the human mind where the personality structure ‘resides’ — where past experience, accessible or not, is ‘stored’ and where the attitudes, values and motives of the more primitive aspects of each personality derive and find expression for their individual characteristics.

The theme of the Chicago IPA Congress was ‘Convergences and Divergences’. Spielman asserted that in the ‘psychoanalytic family’ there are no ‘convergences’ … only ‘divergences’ — or perhaps ‘re-convergences’. We have not ‘converged’ from basically different starting points. We all started in the same place: the Vienna-Berlin-Budapest origins of Freud and his close colleagues. So, we all derive basically from the same stock, but various ‘families of psychoanalytic ideas’ have ‘evolved’ in different locations around the world.

Given then, that we all ‘come from’ the same original family tree … and have migrated around the world in the same manner as the human species has migrated from its earliest origins in Africa, to be represented on every continent, our basic commonalities are what identify us as psychoanalysts — and our ‘differences’ are what identify us as belonging to one ‘psychoanalytic family’ or another. Thus, if we consider ideas about ‘the Unconscious’ to be the central trunk of our psychoanalytic family tree, various branches have diverged from that central trunk and developed different ‘approaches’ to this central concept.

Spielman introduced the three presenters as representing — as usual — each of the geographical regions of the IPA.

responseSet.title: Can the Analyst Think While Enacting?
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Can the Analyst Think While Enacting?11

Donald Moss

Donald Moss

5th Floor, New York, NY 10003, USA - donaldmoss@mindspring.com

Donald Moss  

Moss grounded his Introduction in Freud's notion that primary and secondary process each aim at wish fulfillment. From this, Moss infers that ‘thinking’ and ‘enacting’ cannot be reliably separated. Thinking about an object can satisfy the same set of wishes whose repudiation had made thinking possible. When a patient commands our attention we cannot reliably separate our voyeurism from our epistemophilia. Moss asked: ‘How can we describe the links that both bind and separate thinking and enacting?’

Henry Smith began his paper by summarizing four different definitions of enactment, each representing a different degree of involvement on the analyst's part. His own view is that enactment is a continuous process in every moment of every analysis, determined not only by the patient's but also by the analyst's own unconscious fantasies, wishes and defenses, both parties actualizing their own internal worlds. Hence, not only the analyst's doings but even the analyst's interpretations and the thoughts that precede them are already part of the enactment. As opposed to considering all enactment as harmful, Smith argued that enactment both moves analysis forward and retards it simultaneously. How this balance will play out cannot be determined in advance.

Smith presented two illustrative cases. The first was a woman who wanted him to encourage her sexual excitement rather than interpret her aggression. One day she noticed he was distracted and commented on it, saying that she was too ‘good’ to ask what he was doing. He interpreted her defense against her aggression but it turned out the sound of his voice aroused her sexually. If the sound of his voice aroused her, how could he interpret anything without exciting her in the way she wished? He argued that this was the cardinal situation in all analysis. Analyst and patient are inevitably and continually enacting what they are attempting to analyze. He further illustrated how anything he might say or not say, no matter how ‘neutral,’ mutual, or gentle would be experienced by his patient as his part in a sadomasochistic game or battle between them, an attempt to gain the high ground.

[[RunningHead]]

Searching for the Perfect Woman: The Story of a Complete Psychoanalysis by Vamik D. Volkan with J. Christopher FowlerAronson, Lanham, MD, 2009; 157 pp; $50.00

Adele Tutter

Adele Tutter

300, Central Park West New York, New York 10024, USA
E-mail: atutter@mac.com

Adele Tutter  

Searching for the Perfect Woman is Vamik Volkan's second ‘story of a complete psychoanalysis’. This endeavor varies from its predecessor, What Do You Get When You Cross A Dandelion With A Rose (Volkan, 1984), by virtue of its attempt to integrate Volkan's longstanding interest in chronicling the intricacies of psychoanalysis with his equally abiding efforts to lay bare the social and intergenerational roots of trauma in political, ethnic and religious conflict. In Searching for the Perfect Woman, Volkan tells the story of ‘Hamilton’:

[a] universal one, teaching us how our real and fantasized childhood experiences-especially the traumatic ones, specific relationships with parents and others in our early environment, as well as our ancestors' histories-shape our personalities.

(p. xiii)

Allowing the developmental sequelae of trauma to unfold in the transference is an unparalleled way to expose and articulate them.

236

[[RunningHead]]

Commentary

Lesley Caldwell

Lesley Caldwell

4 Lidfield Road, London N16 9NA
E-mail: lesley@pyrland.demon.co.uk

Lesley Caldwell  

Dr Geerken has provided an opportunity to engage with her work over the first six months of a treatment, and living with her material has produced vast amounts of associations and of ongoing thought, both about this case and what it evokes in me, and, more generally, about the conditions that have to be in place to free associate and to be able to be in analysis.

La Vita Nuova is a project in its infancy: the report of the beginnings of an analysis, with Dr G's description of herself as a very new analyst, and the introduction of a new supervisor, hastily and abruptly, after three months. Throughout she describes her very strong affective responses to her patient, to the analytic situation and to its wider context. She often mentions her anxieties and her “failings of dosage, of timing, of tact”. All this conveys a sense of a young/new unsure analytic couple and it made me wonder from the start not just about the actual newness, but of the re-presentation in the transference/countertransference of an original, potentially traumatic encounter for both mother and baby. The material is strongly framed by extra analytic contacts, which further contribute to anxiety and uncertainty, and make the precise focus unclear for the analyst. This, and how it comes to be understood and experienced, seems likely to be an ongoing factor in the treatment. Right at the end of the period reported here, the patient takes a stand against her parents' intrusions — a real step forward. That it is the patient seems important in thinking about this analysis, and what its purpose may be, especially since the analyst talks to the patient at that time about their reversal of roles.

Beatrice, who is 19, was referred by a senior colleague who had recommended an analysis to which both parents and patient were already committed at the time of the initial consultation, so that the analyst felt she was encountering a fait accompli. Although all three seem keen for Beatrice's problems to be resolved, what those problems are, and how they are regarded and articulated, either by the parents or by her, and why she has been referred at this time, are not described. Beatrice is described as “articulating the desire to begin as soon as possible”, but there is little sense of what she hopes for by beginning. The father has recently been transferred to a nearby city, which may be a consideration leading to the decision at this time. The patient seems much younger and presents a notable passivity about the way the treatment is set up and apparently facilitated, first by the father, who proposes the analyst ring his wife to set up the consultation. The mother is the constant presence. The father is visited by mother and daughter at weekends.

responseSet.title: On: The Concept of Death Drive and Aggressio
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On: The Concept of Death Drive and Aggressio

Nestor Kapusta

Nestor Kapusta

Waehringer Guertel 18-20, 1090 Vienna, Austria
E-mail: nestor.kapusta@meduniwien.ac.at

Nestor Kapusta  

Dear Editors,

The article by Professor Otto Kernberg (2009) is a brilliant analysis and synopsis of the concept of death drive and aggression in the history of psychoanalytic research. In it, I've found a fascinating synecdochic parallelism worth to be mentioned shortly.

Prof. Kernberg mentions the controversies raised by Freud's monumental discovery of the struggle between libido and death drive, erotic impulses and aggression. He points out that it was “Freud's stress on the infantile sexuality” as early as 1905 that has raised “opposition and efforts of denial” against this theory. Interestingly, it was one of Freud's first-minute students and co-thinkers, Alfred Adler, who initiated a controversy at the Wednesday Society, by proposing an alternative to the sexual drive: the aggressive drive (Adler, 1908), which initially has not been integrated into the theory of the early group of psychoanalysts (Adler, 1930), but first became a self-fulfilling prophecy, which led to the group splitting into two: Psychoanalysis and Individual Psychology in 1911.

In his article, Prof. Kernberg gives us a delightful explanation of this group-splitting process. When the efforts to calm down the group's anxiety fail, they show a tendency to “the division of the group itself, or its perception of the surrounding social environment, into an idealized and persecutory one, with active aggression directed against what is perceived as the hostile segment of the world in order to protect the perfection and the security of the ideal group” (p. 1017).

Finally, in 1920 Freud was able to integrate the aggressive drive into a broader concept of Psychoanalysis by omitting references to Adler's original priority in this field. Conversely, Adler gradually destroyed the significant other in his theory, by removing all references to Psychoanalysis.

Indeed, the history is full of anecdotes of tensions between the two groups. The split seems more intense in the United States and less dominant in Europe, where modern Individual Psychology can more easily attach to object relations theory. Professor Kernberg's theoretical framework and his teachings of individual psychologists in Vienna are a source of a promising integration and consolidation of conscious and preconscious identifications with one of these separated groups.
