[[RunningHead]]

Rinsho Seishin Igaku no Hoho [The Method of Clinical Psychiatry]1 by Takeo Doi Iwasaki Gakujutsu Shuppannsha [Iwasaki Academic Press], Tokyo, 2009

Naoki Fujiyama

Naoki Fujiyama

6-25-8-501 Jingumae, Shibuya-Ku, Tokyo 150-0001, Japan
E-mail: fujiyama@naoki.name

Naoki Fujiyama  

The Method of Clinical Psychiatry was written by Takeo Doi, a psychoanalyst who introduced amae, or indulgent dependency — a concept he identified and explored — to the international psychoanalytic community (Doi, 1989). As the first original concept released from Japan, amae had a considerable

659

impact on Western psychiatric thought. Doi passed away on 5 July 2009, making it his last book dedicated to this field.

Doi had long served as president of the Japan Psychoanalytic Society. It is thus somewhat surprising that the book he ventured to publish during this period, The Method of Clinical Psychiatry, centres on psychiatry, not psychoanalysis. It is true that Doi started out as a psychiatrist. However, his major accomplishments to date focused on amae, a psychoanalytic concept, and the issues related to it. Some people may therefore wonder why he returned to psychiatry upon reaching the final years of his life.

Doi once told me: “You have become a psychoanalyst. But I think I am, after all, a psychiatrist.” I realize now that I did not fully understand what he truly meant to say at that time, especially the latter half of his remark. My feeling at the time was simply that of joy that Dr Doi was happy to see that I, a member of the younger generation, had become a fully-fledged analyst. When I read this book, though, I can see that his remarks are not that simple. It was also that, as a person who started his career in psychiatry and psychiatric treatment, he was throwing a question at me, about how I viewed the area between psychoanalysis and psychiatry. As is the case with most outstanding analysts, Doi's remarks — intentional or not — almost always have the implicit effect of evoking an essential question in the mind of the person to whom he is talking.

The Method of Clinical Psychiatry provides his own solutions to that question. I assume that, during this period, he felt impelled to give shape to his solutions in his own way. He was aware that his 17-year battle with a malignant tumour was about to come to an end. Nevertheless, this book does not, in any way, force his solutions on us. I recall Doi consistently asserting, at case seminars, that an analyst's interpretations are not made with the intention of indoctrinating his patients, but are made to produce question marks inside a patient's mind and encourage him/her to tackle these questions with an independent mind.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Telephone Analysis11

Charles Hanly and Jill Scharff

Jill Scharff

6612 Kennedy Drive, Chevy Chase, MD 20815, USA — jillscharff@theipi.org

Jill Scharff  

American, Argentinian, and British panelists presented the arguments for and against teleanalysis (a kind of remote analysis) and shared their experimental work in the practice and teaching of psychoanalysis using the telephone and Skype with video-camera. Audience members eagerly joined in a large group discussion to explore, challenge, argue against, and support the usefulness of teleanalysis in the practice and teaching of psychoanalysis. The panel held that psychoanalysis must adapt to the current social reality posed by the global economy and use its supporting information technology in order to consider the individual, exceptional needs of analysands in training who live in rural areas and in repressed cultures, as, for instance, Eastern Europe and China, executives who travel for work, and young adults who have grown up on technology. Technology enables psychoanalytic clinicians to relate to such analysands and to maintain the optimum frequency of analytic sessions for in-depth analytic work with analysands who are far from psychoanalytic centers. The panel objected to the claim that psychoanalysis is chasing after technology as an alternative to in-depth in-person work and that telephone analysis is not analysis. They asserted that psychoanalysts continue to value the study of the in-person analytic dyad but are adapting to cultural shifts by experimenting with the supplementary use of the telephone, videoconference, and Skype in their practice and teaching of psychoanalysis. Psychoanalysis has been responding to cultural developments since Freud, and then, as now, this responsiveness opens up new pathways of understanding.

The main questions explored were: Can psychoanalysts whose work depends on the harmonics of human interaction work in depth with a person in a circumstance in which they are alone in an office with a communication tool? Can they develop an image of the internal world of the analysand without nonverbal cues? Can there be effective affective attunement, an appreciation of resistance, work with transference and counter-transference? Can a training analysis on the telephone prepare a candidate so that the analysis of their own analysands is at a level of competence equivalent to that of a candidate in traditional analysis?

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Analytic Practice: Convergences and Divergences11

Cláudio Eizirik

Cláudio Eizirik

— ceizirik.ez@terra.com.br

Cláudio Eizirik  

analytic practice, analytic technique, common ground

The author discusses current convergences and divergences concerning analytic practice. After presenting a clinical vignette that can be understood differently according to different theoretical approaches, he discusses Wallerstein's proposal of a common ground in psychoanalysis and suggests that the present state of the art indicates that psychoanalysis is a pluralistic discipline, with different ways of training and practising it, and that the main challenge is to improve our ability to listen to and to learn from different approaches.

responseSet.title: Independent Traditions and Middle Groups in Contemporary Psychoanalysis
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Independent Traditions and Middle Groups in Contemporary Psychoanalysis11

Warren Poland and Valerie Laabs-Siemon

Valerie Laabs-Siemon

5215 North Ironwood Road, suite 115, Milwaukee, WI 53217, USA - laabs-siemon@earthlink.net

Valerie Laabs-Siemon  

Panelists Michael Parsons of the United Kingdom and Nancy Chodorow of the United States joined Chair, Warren Poland, and the IPA 46th Congress audience to examine aspects of Independent, Middle Group and multi-theoretical perspectives. Prominent themes included historical and cultural influences upon individual and group analytic identity, theory of mind and of analytic action. Participants discussed creative use of multiple theories and careful listening to patients.

Opening the panel, Warren Poland posed the question of how an analyst can learn from and fit into previous analytic traditions while, at the same time, protecting an individualistic and even creative frame of mind. In introducing the two panelists, he pointed out critical historical differences from within which the British and the American Independent Traditions separately arose, the intensity and rigors of post-World War II England with possibilities for growth and challenge outside as well as within the analytic establishment in the States.

Michael Parsons (UK) discussed his training experiences, the evolution of his understanding of what it means to be ‘part of an Independent tradition … and to form an analytic identity’. Parsons relies upon his freedom to create a psychoanalytic home without borders that does not limit him to an ‘alignment with any specific viewpoint’ but rather allows the depth and expansiveness of a foundation centered on ‘intellectual and human values rather than particular psychoanalytic doctrines.’ An analytic attitude that treasures uniqueness of each individual analyst, patient and the interactions between them is essential to ‘Independent’ analytic work. Working within an Independent tradition allows each analyst to create ‘a way of thinking and working that is individually his or her own’.

‘What comes from the patient and what the patient experiences as his or her own discovery in the analytic encounter matters more than anything that comes from the analyst.’ As the analysand experiences being listened to through the exchanges with the analyst, he or she learns to hear and be surprised by his or her own unique unconscious life.

responseSet.title: A Commentary on Mayer Subrin's Paper ‘Stumbling towards Termination’
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Commentary on Mayer Subrin's Paper ‘Stumbling towards Termination’

Ilany Kogan

Ilany Kogan

2 Mohaliver St, Rehovot 76304, Israel
E-mail: ilanyk@yahoo.com

Ilany Kogan  

I was pleased to receive the invitation to comment on Dr Subrin's clinical material in the ‘The Analyst at Work’ section of the International Journal of Psychoanalysis. I find this section, whose important purpose is to advance psychoanalytic communication across psychoanalytic schools, interesting and intriguing.

In his work, Dr Subrin presents clinical material from a sub-phase of termination, with musings on termination prior to the selection of a specific date for termination, and culminating with a record of the last week of analysis. As I, too, have explored the subject of termination (Kogan, 1996), the theme illustrated in Dr Subrin's article Stumbling towards termination is of special interest to me.

Dr Subrin's account impressed me with its frankness and admirable sincerity. I was also impressed by the holding ability of a sensitive analyst who accompanied his patient on a long and painful analytic journey, and by his contact with the patient's unconscious, as described in this fine work. However, I found the task I was asked to undertake quite difficult. In examining my reactions to Dr Subrin's interesting material, I discovered that engaging in a form of creative reflection by placing myself in the analyst's chair and allowing my associations to lead me in one direction or another creates the temptation to do the analyst's job in a different, ‘better’ way. Humorously referring to this temptation, Gardner states: “Whenever one analyst tells his or her work to another, it is tempting to the told analyst to tell the telling analyst what the telling analyst might have seen, could have seen, should have seen, might have done, could have done, should have done” (1984, p. 39). I, too, found myself caught up in this temptation.

I am also aware that to comment on somebody else's sessions without knowing much about the work done by both patient and analyst in their long analytic journey, and without being acquainted with the nuances, hidden messages, and relevant omissions, is, to say the least, presumptuous. I therefore offer my remarks and questions only as points to ponder, because they derive from my attempt to understand the patient and sense her analytic experience from an outsider's perspective, without having all the relevant information.

responseSet.title: The Kleinian Problematic of Narcissism
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Kleinian Problematic of Narcissism11

Ingrid Newstadt

Ingrid Newstadt

360 North Bedford Drive, Suite 419, Beverly Hills, CA 90210, USA — inewstadt1@yahoo.com

Ingrid Newstadt  

Ingrid Newstadt introduced the panel's interest in London Kleinian conceptualizations regarding clinical narcissism as understood by the panellists who represented the three IPA continental regions. One over-riding question related to the main theme of the IPA Chicago Congress: is an inter-regional dialogue possible on the various ways in which Kleinian thought regarding narcissism is articulated in the UK, North and South America?

In terms of covering both Abraham and Klein's work, the panel began with Robert Hinshelwood's paper, which described their work and how it set a framing context for later London Kleinian contributions. Klein carried on Abraham's commitment to investigate deeper, unconscious ‘primitive’ mechanisms' associated with the earliest years of life. Klein's emphasis was more on a ‘psychotogenic’ layer from which both neurotic and psychotic disorders develop. Unlike the American ego psychological approach, which evolved from a more ‘neurotogenic’ matrix, Kleinians focus (in Hinshelwood's view) on the “… incapacities of the ego, rather more than the conflicts within the ego.” This includes “… narcissistic issues, akin to psychotic functioning and other difficult personality problems.” This idea was strengthened by the considerable amount of clinical and conceptual work done on psychosis by the Kleinians in the 1940s and 1950s.

Hinshelwood elaborated how Klein expanded Freud's (1914) notion of narcissism, where the patient oscillated between ‘narcissistic’ and ‘object-related’ poles of experience as represented in the ‘paranoid—schizoid’ and ‘depressive’ positions. While Hinshelwood also endorsed an inter-regional discussion of varying perspectives on narcissism, he remained cautious about the risk of oversimplifying comparisons between the London Kleinian formulations and the work of analytic authors from other regions. Bringing together diverse points of view can also lend itself to neglecting an “unrecognized diversity of underlying assumptions.”

Hinshelwood also referred to the problems inherent in the process of being analytically socialized in any particular cultural context. There is no question that analytic training, Kleinian or otherwise, is different whether it is in London, Los Angeles or Sao Paolo.

responseSet.title: Ending Analysis: Comparative views
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Ending Analysis: Comparative views11

Nancy Kulish and Patricia Plopa

Patricia Plopa

4655 Pickering Road, Bloomfield Hills, Ml 48301, USA — pplopa4655@comcast.net

Patricia Plopa  

How does one conceptualize the ending of an analytic process? The moderator of this panel, Nancy Kulish, introduced this discussion by comparing the panel's task to the position of artists stepping back from the immediacy of their paintings in order to see their work from different perspectives. There is a timeless quality to an analysis, yet with a backdrop that it will end at some point — but at what point? Are there specific criteria that help in determining when to end treatment, and how does this affect technique? In order to explore these issues, Antonio Perez-Sanchez summarized an eight-year treatment, with particular emphasis on how ending the analysis was decided.

The patient was a man in his mid-30s who presented with intense anxiety, loneliness, somatic symptoms, and uncertainty about his sexual identity. While successful occupationally, he felt like an ‘imposter’. The patient settled into what appeared to be a good working analytic relationship, but Perez-Sanchez thought he was too compliant and suspected a hidden attitude of devaluation of the analysis. Perez-Sanchez presented a dream which he interpreted that illustrated this ‘secret perverse relationship with the analyst’ in which the patient was submitting to the analytic method, but inside himself was mistreating the analyst. As the sadomasochism was uncovered and worked with, the patient made progress, as evidenced by more realistic attitudes toward work, greater closeness with the analyst, and less tortured, sadomasochistic relationships in his life.

The patient brought up ending his analysis several times in the two years before a termination date was set — times Perez-Sanchez termed as a ‘flight forward’. The patient considered ending again in his sixth year. Leaving analysis frightened him and he felt some disappointment that the analysis had not provided what he initially expected — the capacity to create a family with children — but it had allowed him to become freer and more comfortable. The analyst suggested 18 months to work toward ending.

responseSet.title: Is the Transference Interpretation Still Central to Analytic Work?
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Is the Transference Interpretation Still Central to Analytic Work?11

Marie-France Dispaux

Marie-France Dispaux

14 avenue de Montalembert, 1330 Rixensart, Belgium - mariefrance@dispaux.com

Marie-France Dispaux  

Within the framework of the congress whose central theme was convergences and divergences in practice analytical today, the subject of our panel raised a crucial question about interpretation and transference, two pillars of our analytical work. In bond with the project of the congress which was to create, not a common basis, but to support an in-depth discussion on the way in which the various psychoanalytical cultures consider their practice, the panel allowed, starting from the clinical presentation, to confront and put in tension several points of view.

At the beginning of the panel, the clinical presentation of Alicia Leisse de Lustgarten (Venezuela) opened a field of very rich discussions. ‘Valentina’, the introduced patient, is a delicate and fragile patient who poses from the start the question of the tempo of interpretation and especially of interpretation in the transference which is likely to revive the traumatisms that the patient experienced. With tact and patience, Alicia reassures the patient to enable her little by little to approach her internal world. In one of the meetings presented, we see well how the patient tries to control her analyst and how the analyst arrives finally at the transference. Alicia makes us feel indeed the importance of the interpretation of the transference for her and, at the same time, the difficulty in formulating it with a patient who is so afraid of an emotional closeness. It is important to add that this difficulty goes beyond the psychoanalytic culture of the presenter as will be shown by the discussion.

To begin the discussion, Georg Bruns (Germany) gave us a very relevant clinical example in its manner of approaching interpretation ‘in’ the transference with difficult patients in order to create a space of ‘play’ which allows them gradually to find their own psychic space, before making an interpretation ‘on’ transference. For G. Bruns too, the interpretation of the transference is central to the work because it brings about a deeper psychic transformation.

responseSet.title: Migration and Identity: Different Perspectives
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Migration and Identity: Different Perspectives11

Ilany Kogan

Ilany Kogan

2 Mohaliver St., Rehovot, 36704 Israel - ilanyk@yahoo.com

Ilany Kogan  

This panel, which dealt with several aspects of migration and their impact on identity, consisted of four analysts from different countries of origin. The two presenters were analysts working in Munich - both members of the German Psychoanalytic Society, but from different backgrounds. Hediaty Utari-Witt, a psychoanalyst born in Indonesia and trained in Munich, has been working for the last ten years with an increasing number of patients from Asia (Thailand and Vietnam), as well as from Iran, Morocco and Nigeria; Marco Conci, a psychoanalyst of Italian origin, also trained in Munich, treats mainly Italian patients; Aisha Abbasi, the discussant of the two presentations, is originally from Pakistan, and lives and practises in the USA; Romanian-born Ilany Kogan, the chair of the panel, lives and works in Israel.

The presenters illustrated the theme by describing two different cases, which were conducted in different languages. Dr Utari-Witt's patient was a Brazilian woman living in Germany, and the analysis was conducted in German, a second language for both members of the analytic couple. Dr Conci's patient was a young Italian woman residing in Germany, and the treatment was conducted in Italian - the primary language of both analyst and analysand.

The panel focused on two main issues connected to migration: problems of unresolved mourning and the role of language in psychoanalysis with migrants.

In their classic book, Psychoanalytic Perspectives on Migration and Exile, Grinberg and Grinberg (1989) claim that migration may involve various traumatic factors and may even be experienced as a catastrophic change, exposing the individual who experiences it to a period of disorganization, pain and frustration, accompanied by anxiety. In his later extensive exploration of the topic, Akhtar (1999) states that migration from one country to another is a complex psychosocial process with significant and lasting effects on an individual's identity. He believes that, even under the best of circumstances, migration is a traumatic occurrence and, like other traumas, it mobilizes a mourning process. Much work has to be done in mourning the loss of people the immigrant leaves behind, as well as the loss of familiar surroundings, smells, tastes and sounds.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Melanie Klein in Berlin by Claudia FrankRoutledge, Hove, New Library of Psychoanalysis, 2009; 500 pp; £24.99

Ronald Britton

Ronald Britton

24 Hillfield Road, London NW6 1PZ
E-mail: RBpsych@aol.com

Ronald Britton  

This book gives us an unparalleled opportunity to see how Melanie Klein developed her method of the psychoanalysis of children: from her clinical notes, Berlin Clinic records and the unpublished texts in the Klein Archive alongside her published work. What later became known as ‘Kleinian analysis’ began with the pioneering analytic work that Melanie Klein did in Berlin with children between 1921 and 1926 and it was the foundation of the psychoanalytic theories which she developed later in London in conjunction with her colleagues.

When she moved to Berlin she was 38 years old, with three children and separated from her husband. She had little money, few friends and no patrons but what was on her mind was the idea that child psychoanalysis would reveal things as yet unknown, that she would discover them and a belief that they would be welcomed. She was encouraged in this by her first analyst and teacher Sandor Ferenczi in Budapest. She approached Karl Abraham at the Hague Congress of 1920, although she was still a ‘nobody’ in the analytic world, to ask about moving to Berlin. She must have received some encouragement as she quickly embarked on it. She had only been qualified as a member of the Budapest Psychoanalytic Society for two years when she moved to Berlin.

In 1921 Berlin was the most exciting psychoanalytic venue of the day under the presiding spirit of Karl Abraham: the first low fee/free analytic clinic had opened a year earlier; the Berlin Institute training was systematized with analysis, seminars and supervision and there were regular scientific meetings. When Melanie Klein arrived, her place in Berlin was so precarious that she gave the Polyclinic as her address. However, undeterred, she gave her first paper on child analysis in February 1921 and another entitled Early analysis at the International Congress in Berlin in 1922 (‘early’ meant analysis of young children).

responseSet.title: The Past as Resistance, the Past as Constructed
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Past as Resistance, the Past as Constructed11

Henry Markman

Henry Markman

2435 Russell St., Berkeley, CA 90707, USA — hcmarkman@aol.com

Henry Markman  

Henry Markman provided opening remarks concerning the various conceptions of time in psychoanalytic theory and clinical work. Psychoanalytic time is non-linear and discontinuous. Artists have vividly depicted subjective temporality. Joyce's character in Ulysses comments on Moses never entering the Promised Land: “He died with a great future behind him.” Joyce's masterpiece captures the interpenetration of past, present and future in each moment, often reversing the usual causal chain of time. Markman noted that Joyce's novel is a universe in a single day, where time is the central protagonist, ordered by the subject's (Bloom and Stephen's) unconscious. To quote Ulysses again: “Hold to the now, the here, through which all future plunges into the past.” Might we not say the same of psychoanalysis? The here-and-now in the session is such a moment, where multiple dimensions of time saturate the present and are ordered by the analysand's unconscious.

Markman noted the various meanings of the past in analytic discourse: as fact, i.e. as material reality; as historical truth; as evolving narrative; as repetition in the transference. Does the analysand acquire a past, a history through the course of analysis, and is this a central aspect of therapeutic action? Is this what we mean by construction? Or does he or she acquire an increasing repertoire of narratives and an altered, decentered relation to her unconscious? And what role does the analyst play which allows for the experience of past, present and future in each session in such a way that these links can be articulated?

The past as resistance can be viewed from the perspective of analysand: how he uses stories to close off the emergence of new experience or a deeper affective contact with past experiences. From the analyst's side, she may use the past as a safe place to engage the patient, especially during heightened transference moments or periods of uncertainty.

“Cláudio Laks Eizirik's main concern in addressing the problem of the past in the present moment was finding a way in which the analyst remains open to the emergence of the patient's unconscious. The Barangers' work on conceptualizing the field formed a foundation for Eizirik's point of view, and he quoted the Barangers (2008 [1961-2], p. 800)

responseSet.authorMast: Irene Cairo and  Susana Vinocur Fischbein
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis and Virtual Reality11

Irene Cairo and Susana Fischbein

Susana Fischbein

susyvinfisch@gmail.com

Susana Fischbein  

The Chair, Irene Cairo, opened the panel with some reflections related to the technical assumptions available to new generations. Her proposal for this panel was not only stirred by a personal experience but also influenced by her reading of the panelists' writings. They have all been long aware of the fact that new technology has forced psychoanalysts to reassess many of their views. Irene Cairo referred to Glen Gabbard's paper on Cyberpassion (Gabbard, 2001), which presaged how communication through the Internet would challenge many technical precepts. Julio Moreno was introduced through his book Being Human (Moreno, 2000), which tackles the concept of conflict and the traditional views of reality as being limited to material and psychic reality, now having to encompass virtual reality. Finally, Vincenzo Bonaminio presented his ideas on the way adolescents challenge the essential conservatism of the classical analytic stance.

Bonaminio's contribution was primarily focused on the significant effects on psychoanalysis of children and adolescents' new ways of communicating. This would be further illustrated by a 30 year-old clinical case considered to be the forerunner of the digital era. With a quote from a film,2 Bonaminio vividly described the analyst's position in the face of the new patients' suffering from ‘disturbances in their own subjectivity, in their possibility of being’. He chose to discuss adolescence because it functions as a cultural link between generations. Adolescents give analysts the chance to see how overwhelming and ‘ungraspable’ changes are in ways of thinking, of representing inner and external reality-how incipient and simultaneously abortive these may be. Adolescent discourse highlights the discontent of contemporary culture, together with the discontent of the analyst who has to face them. The impact of the unrepresented on the psychic fabric falls outside the ego's orbit; influencing and altering its structure. The outcome is absence or reduction of the transitional area between the realms of fantasy and reality. The progressive legitimization of an ‘autistic auto-sensuousness’ (Tustin, 1981)

responseSet.title: Distinguishing Psychoanalysis from Psychotherapy
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Distinguishing Psychoanalysis from Psychotherapy

Horst Kächele

Horst Kächele

8, 89081 Ulm, Germany — horst.kaechele@uni-ulm.de, http://www.horstkaechele.de

Horst Kächele  

Since its inception psychoanalysis has spread throughout the world as a scientific discipline and as a mode of treatment. Starting in the Western hemisphere it soon travelled from Vienna to Berlin, to Budapest, to London and Moscow (Luria, 1924 (Freud, 1918, p. 10); see Etkind, 2000) and Calcutta (Bose, 1921; see Vaidyanathan and Kripal, 1999). Psychoanalytic training crossed the Atlantic ocean conquering North America by people trained by Freud himself (Shakow and Rapaport, 1964). Already, in 1911, Freud reviewed a paper by Greve, a Chilean physician, which contains the first reference to psychoanalysis in Latin America, but it was Matte Blanco's great achievement to institutionalize psychoanalytic training since the 1940s (see Jiménez, 2002, p. 83). The Japanese analyst Doi (1971) wrote about ‘the anatomy of dependence’ on which he spelled out the specific Japanese concept of ‘amae’, and for this was granted the M. Sigourney Award in 2005. After the downfall of the Iron Curtain the former countries of the Soviet Union were quick to accept the missionaries of various psychoanalytic backgrounds. The Russian development provided a special case when president Jelzin, in 1993, signed an official document for the re-installation of ‘Russian psychoanalysis’ (Reshetnikov, 1998). At present we are witnessing the implantation of psychoanalysis in China promoted by various psychoanalytic groups that have been teaching there for a number of years (Gerlach, 2005; Varvin, 2008).

Given these developments it seems fair to speak of a process of globalization of psychoanalysis and its treatment practices. At a closer view one cannot avoid noting quite a diversity of what is covered by the term psychoanalysis in respect to treatment practice. Does it make sense to insist on a sharp distinction between psychoanalysis proper and psychoanalytically informed treatments, and what would be the impact for training by giving up the strict distinction?

Differences and similarities between psychoanalysis and potential offspring have been discussed since the time of Freud. On the one hand, Freud spoke of “analyses which lead to a favourable conclusion in a short time” enhancing the therapist's self-esteem (Freud, 1918, p. 10). These shorter treatments — later to be loosely called psychoanalytically informed or psychodynamic — have substantiated the medical impact of psychoanalysis, as they dominate the psychoanalytic therapies of today. To denounce them as “insignificant as regards the advancement of scientific knowledge” (as Freud referred to analyses that quickly ended favourably) (Freud, 1918, p. 10)

responseSet.title: The Italian Red Brigades and the Structure and Dynamics of Terrorist Groups
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Italian Red Brigades and the Structure and Dynamics of Terrorist Groups

Carole Tarantelli

Carole Tarantelli

Via Malta 6a, Rome, Italy 00198 — c.tarantelli@libero.it

Carole Tarantelli  

basic assumptions, group regression, negative pacts, terrorism, unconscious alliances, unconscious phantasy scenarios, valency, violent ideological groups

(Final version accepted 1 October 2009)

One of the problems in dealing with terrorism is that we have virtually no access to individual terrorists; only their actions are visible. The founders of the Italian terrorist group, the Red Brigades, on the other hand, have written about their experiences and have exhaustively explained their motivations. The author's premise is that these autobiographies and her interviews with several of the group's members give us access to the unconscious processes involved in the formation and operation of the group. After terrorist attacks, it is natural to ask whether the terrorists' capacity for collective violence is an indication of personal pathology. This paper argues that the relevant pathology in the terrorist enterprise is not that of the individual but that of the group. Relying on the theories of groups of Freud (1921), Bion (1961), Anzieu (1984) and Kaes (2007), the author argues that psychoanalytic theory is essential to understanding the motivations and actions of violent groups which otherwise remain obscure. Although the discussion has been confined to one terrorist group, the author hopes that it can also be useful for understanding the unconscious dynamics of other groups structured around an ideology which mandates the destruction of human life.

responseSet.title: Alter ego by Michel Neyraut Editions de I'Olivier, Paris, 2008; 203 pp; €L13.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Alter ego1 by Michel NeyrautEditions de I'Olivier, Paris, 2008; 203 pp; €L13.

Dominique Bourdin

Dominique Bourdin

E-mail: dominique@bourdin.biz

Dominique Bourdin  

Wishing to return to the fundamental concepts, Michel Neyraut, a member of the Paris Psychoanalytic Society, has published Alter ego, a brilliant essay on identification, or rather on the principle of identification. This book is a continuation of the author's fundamental reflections, first on Le transfert

1Translated by Andrew Weller.222

(Neyraut, 1974), then on Les logiques de I'inconscient(Neyraut, 1978) and on Les raisons de I'irrationnel(Neyraut, 1997).

The key issues and problems of identification are explored over ten chapters, beginning with commentaries on quotations in the first and concluding with the notion of identeme, which is discussed at the end of the book. This lesson on identification, in the strong sense of the term lesson, that is, of an essential transmission, draws much of its inspiration from the cinema, literature, painting, and even philosophy. This reference to culture is not an illustration, but is intrinsic to the exploration of the concept of identification. Identification owes its necessity to differences. If men were all identical basic particles, they would be the same, but this identification without remainder would be insignificant. The ‘conquerors of the identical' who babble on nowadays promise nothing but sheep that are so similar that one is worth all the others,2 and all the others are only of value owing to their number. On the other hand, the cinema distracts us from life by depicting it on another stage; “this similitude between two worlds is at the heart of identification” (p. 9). Michel Neyraut not only convinces us forcefully, but does so with exceptional elegance; and he presents with humour the most subtle and the complex ideas, articulating figures and concepts in a seamless fabric.

The use of a quotation is part of the problem of identification. Michel Neyraut chooses five. Voltaire's (1759) Candide identifies with himself, Baron de Thunder-ten-tronckh; with someone else, Cunégonde; and with a model, Pangloss, that is, with the part for the whole, for his province is worth the entire universe. Such is the path of happiness, even if everything is tending towards the worst … The next concerns the self-reduplication that is called oneself, which is at the heart of the Maine de Biran's (1807-1810) reflection on self-consciousness that integrates the passive and active elements of an internal experience with the help of a method which includes a return to the past, the taking into account of personal prehistory, and the analysis of sensitive data in the light of personal singularity. The quotation evokes the effects on the foetus of its mother's felt experience, and sees in it the knot of sympathy which unites two beings on the basis of the identity of organic functions and the sharing of simple passions. The third quotation is from Freud (1931) who, writing to Yvette Guilbert, says of Charlie Chaplin: “acting the sickly and clumsy boy, he plays only himself as he was in his grim youth: childhood is the unique source of all the roles, which are compositions of oneself; playing diverse roles, the actor embodies the conflicts of the first years of life and so puts the spectator back in touch with himself, reconciling him with his youth. Playing a role does not imply renouncing one's ego, which itself is the reflection of multiple identifications” (8 March 1931). Ricardo Reis, a heteronym for Pessoa (Pessoa and Reis, 1985), points out, moreover, that ‘there are more egos than myself’, which does not prevent one from existing and from speaking by silencing them.

responseSet.title: Psychoanalytic Treatment for Severely Disturbed Adolescents
responseSet.authorMast: Virginia Ungar and  Viviane Sprinz Mondrzak
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalytic Treatment for Severely Disturbed Adolescents11

Virginia Ungar and Viviane Mondrzak

Viviane Mondrzak

mondrzak@terra.com.br

Viviane Mondrzak  

The panel began with some words of the chair, Virginia Ungar, introducing the subject, the presenters and a brief summary of their ideas. She pointed out that it is important to have in mind that the passage from puberty to adolescence and from there to adult life implies very complex processes. These demand ‘psychic work’ from a mind that is indeed in a chaotic situation: at puberty the latency structure collapses and drives that come from a body that has become a ‘stranger’ burst in. This situation will imply a complex task of integration for the psyche. Nowadays it is not possible to approach the adolescent phenomenon only from the point of view of their inner world, or from the impact their body has on their mental organization: adolescence ties together the body, the psyche and the social. The time when psychoanalysis could infer meanings from a single standpoint, either the internal world or the environment, is long gone. The patient and the therapist are social creatures and both are influenced by the environment in which they live. Therefore, although the adolescent process has certain invariants, the transit from puberty to adolescence and from there to adult life will change according to the different social environments in which the individual will carry on the developmental process.

In her presentation, Irene Ruggiero states that an adolescent, who is not able to use his own psyche to deal with conflicts, has usually had a childhood with histories of intrusions of the object in their private space and an environment with an inability for symbolic processes. This picture frequently leads to the patient's difficulty in separating and setting personal boundaries. The boundary-forcing modalities used by severely disturbed adolescents and their families inevitably manifest in the therapeutic space too, put to the test by their acting out. In these situations, the analyst is strongly provoked to act and obliged to work on the boundary between the internal and external world, and between the subject and the object. In situations like this, the request for help for a child is deeply ambivalent because her transformative growth can be felt as a deadly threat to the family's balance, which is based upon shared and collusive defences.

responseSet.title: Battling the Life and Death Forces of Sadomasochism
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Battling the Life and Death Forces of Sadomasochism11

Harriet Basseches and Ellen Sinkman

Ellen Sinkman

LCSW 295, Central Park West, New York, NY 10024, USA — esinkman1@gmail.com

Ellen Sinkman  

Harriet Basseches, Chairperson, introduced the subject by stating that some sadomasochism is found in every case. It is a matter of differing degrees. She noted that it may be natural to find pleasure in pain, both one's own and others' pain, although it may be a difference in kind when the severity of the sadomasochism functions out of the bounds of human expectations and becomes perverse.

Basseches recounted Freud's (1917) implication that a child's built-in potential for sadomasochism derives from both sexual and aggressive strivings. Shengold (1989) argues for the centrality of Freud's (1919) anal stage of development in organizing much of character and functioning. Klein (1957) sees the infant as struggling from the outset between love and hate toward the internalized essence of the caregiver. These theoreticians note the possibility of pathological, sadomasochistically tinged outcomes.

Other authors have focused on sadomasochistic behavior as defensive and reparative (Bach, 1994), and on the primitive superego underlying sadomasochists' thinking (Wurmser, 2007). Basseches introduced panelists Nancy Goodman and Paula Ellman, who presented patients showing murderous transferential feelings and sadomasochistic features, as well as eliciting certain transference-countertransference dynamics. Three discussants commented on the cases.

Nancy Goodman used the metaphor of the Leviathan, a sea-monster, in her presentation, Living with the Leviathan, to convey forces of sadomasochism in her analysis of Mr. B. The Leviathan expressed active and passive forms, as well as enactments, of sadomasochism within Mr. B's psyche, her own psyche, and interactions of their minds. The giant squid had tentacles, a sucking and chewing mouth, a magical anal sphincter, and special visual capacity. Because confronting any difference between himself and another person ignited terror of destruction, Mr. B used disavowal to view the world. Disavowal helped him perversely to know and not know, and to use relationships as fetishes.

responseSet.title: The French Model at Work: Indication and the Jean Favreau Centre for Consultation and Treatment
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The French Model at Work: Indication and the Jean Favreau Centre for Consultation and Treatment

Ellen Sparer

Ellen Sparer

SPP, 57 rue de Turbigo, Paris 75003, France - easparer@gmail.com

Ellen Sparer  

French model, indication, Jean Favreau Centre, psychodrama, representation, tertiary processes

(Final version accepted 17 December 2009)

The goal of this paper is to present the way in which an analyst trained in the French Model at the Paris Psychoanalytical Society and working at its policlinic, the Jean Favreau Centre for Consultation and Treatment (CCTP), approaches the question of indication. After a brief examination of Freud's 1905 text on indication I will discuss certain concepts, such as representation and tertiary processes, which form the basis of our listening to today's patients. After presenting certain elements of the French Model I will then discuss a period which was a turning point in French psychoanalysis. Psychodrama, a psychoanalytically inspired treatment modality developed in France, which is useful when the capacity to represent is limited will be introduced. Two clinical illustrations of consultations at the CCTP will then follow.

responseSet.title: One-Person and Two-Person Conceptions of Attachment and their Implications for Psychoanalytic Thought
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

One-Person and Two-Person Conceptions of Attachment and their Implications for Psychoanalytic Thought

Paul Wachtel

Paul Wachtel

CUNY Distinguished Professor, Doctoral Program in Clinical Psychology, City College of New York, 138th Street and Convent Avenue, New York, NY 10031, USA — paul.wachtel@gmail.com

Paul Wachtel  

attachment, one-person theory, psychoanalysis, relational psychoanalysis, relational theory, two-person theory

(Final version accepted 13 October 2009)

There has been increasing interest among analysts in the possibilities of enriching psychoanalytic thought through fuller incorporation of attachment theory and research. This paper offers a clinical illustration of the ways in which attention to an attachment perspective can lead to novel and useful ways of addressing the patient's issues. It also presents a number of cautions that it is necessary to be alert to if attachment thinking is to achieve its full potential in advancing psychoanalytic thought and practice. Conceptions of attachment and approaches to its study and clinical use actually vary quite substantially. Some are more one-person, static, and categorical. Others are more two-person, dynamic, and focused on the process whereby attachment patterns develop and are maintained over time. This paper explores the distinction between these two versions of attachment theory and research with two aims in mind — first, to refine our understanding of the potential role that attachment thinking can play in advancing the psychoanalytic paradigm; second, to utilize the insights achieved through examining the attachment paradigm to consider some broader issues in the construction of psychoanalytic theory more generally and its relational variant in particular.

responseSet.title: Loving Psychoanalysis: Technique and Theory in the Therapeutic Relationship by Susan S. Levine Aronson, New York, NY, 2009; 155 pp; $40.00
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Loving Psychoanalysis: Technique and Theory in the Therapeutic Relationship by Susan S. LevineAronson, New York, NY, 2009; 155 pp; $40.00

Gurmeet Kanwal

Gurmeet Kanwal

275 Central Park West, Suite 1W, New York, NY 10024, USA
E-mail: kanbar@inch.com

Gurmeet Kanwal  

Psychoanalysis as a profession is unique in many ways, its impenetrability being one. Unlike a musician who can wax unselfconsciously about her love for music, or a scientist who can unhesitatingly express his fascination for science and the scientific method, or a clergyman who can unquestionably declare his passion for religion, a psychoanalyst advertising her love for psychoanalysis carries the unique burden of requiring a self-reflection and a self-examination that can be hard to sustain alongside an authentic passionate engagement. Perhaps it is because of this burden that we so rarely see a whole book dedicated to expressing and examining one's love for the practice of psychoanalysis. Loving Psychoanalysis: Technique and Theory in the Therapeutic Relationship by Susan S. Levine is an exception.

This book brings to mind another published 18 years ago — Janet Malcolm's (1981) book, The Impossible Profession. That book, written by a journalist reporting to a lay audience, and Susan Levine's book, written by a psychoanalyst sharing with other clinicians, could not be further apart. One makes an attempt to take outsiders in to the world of psychoanalysis, the other to bring the insides of the profession out to the surface. While one book emphasizes the negative and the impossible, the other raves about the positive and the possible. Yet it can be said that they are linked in some psychic sense that relates to our need to reveal what is esoteric, to explain what is mystifying, and to justify what insists on existing in spite of the odds. Could it be that Susan Levine's book is a response to the cultural gestalt created by Janet Malcolm's? Loving Psychoanalysis is a title that seduces the reader to wonder about the intention behind it. Is it about a psychoanalytic approach that considers ‘loving’ to be an essential component of therapeutic activity? Is it about the author ‘loving’ the field of psychoanalysis from an academic and intellectual perspective, or is it about ‘loving’ the experience of practicing as a psychoanalyst? (For one can certainly love the field but hate the practice of it.)

responseSet.title: On: Memory in a Labile State: Therapeutic Application
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On: Memory in a Labile State: Therapeutic Application11

Hugo Bleichmar

Hugo Bleichmar

Diego de León 44 — 3izq., 28006 Madrid, España
E-mail: bleichmar@aperturas.biz

Hugo Bleichmar  

Dear Editors

Previous to the papers by Nader et al. (2000) and Sara (2000), it was considered that memories, once fixated, were unchangeable and that whenever something was remembered a new inscription was added to that which is already in place. However, experimental work by these authors, subsequently confirmed fully, showed that, at the moment of remembering, this memory is in a labile state and is constructed totally anew. This process was termed ‘memory reconsolidation’. This means that each memory is not an addition to the series of memories related to this memory but an inscription: whatever is new is incorporated into the antecedent, which is restructured in toto, resulting in a single inscription.

1Translated by Susan Rogers.1524

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Discussion

Fred Busch's Response to H. Kächele D. Widlöcher

Fred Busch

Fred Busch

246 Elliot Street, Chestnut Hill, MA 02467, USA — drfredbusch@gmail.com

Fred Busch  

It is a pleasure to discuss the papers of my esteemed colleagues. While we may have disagreements, I applaud their clarity and the forcefulness of their arguments.

I will start with the work of Professor Kächele, and the extensive research he cites for his conclusions that there is a continuum between psychoanalysis and psychotherapy. As someone trained in research before my training in psychoanalysis, I have respect for and skepticism about research. I learned early about the differences between research conclusions and ‘Truth’, and to appreciate that research is designed by researchers who interpret the numbers. The numbers do not always tell the story; rather it is the researcher who tells the story of the numbers. For example, let us look at the Menninger Psychotherapy Research Project as described by Kächele as showing “no overwhelming differences in outcomes after supportive—expressive treatment, analytic psychotherapy and psychoanalysis,” while also noting the mixed use of techniques in different treatments (e.g. support). He goes on to mention that some have criticized the Menninger study as “ecologically invalid”, a term he seems to dismissively equate with not being the type of patients one sees in private practice. However, the full critique of the Menninger study's ‘ecology’ might have included what Bachrach et al. (1991) stated:

The major limitations of the study include the unrepresentative patient population largely not suited for psychoanalysis by current standards, the use of student analysts, the significant modifications required by most, if not all, of the analyses, and the relatively small sample. The study did not provide as detailed opportunities for the in-depth study of psychoanalytic process as might be available today, and generally only impressionistic data were provided in exploring the analyst's contribution to treatment.

(p. 884)

Further, Kächele reports from the Leuzinger-Bohleber et al. (2003) study: “The German follow-up study on a substantial sample on high and low frequency psychoanalytic therapies has supported the notion that intensity of treatment per se does not account for satisfying lasting outcome”. However, Leuzinger-Bohleber (2003) follow this by saying: “We have to stress that this finding does not mean that psychoanalysis and long-term psychoanalytic treatment lead to the same results because, within the design of our study, we could not ask this research question” (p. 270, italics added). I find similar discrepancies in Kächele's conclusions regarding the Ablon and Jones (2005)

responseSet.title: Marie Bonaparte, her first two patients and the literary world
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Marie Bonaparte, her first two patients and the literary world11

Rémy Amouroux

Rémy Amouroux

Unité Douleur, Hôpital Trousseau, 26 av. Du Dr Arnold Netter, 75571 Paris, France — amouroux.remy@gmail.com

Rémy Amouroux  

history of psychoanalysis, Italo Svevo, literature, Marie Bonaparte

(Final version accepted 1 December 2009)

Marie Bonaparte (1882-1962) played a critical role in the development of psychoanalysis in France. Her clinical activity is not well known yet she was one of the first female French psychoanalysts. The journalist-writers Alice and Valerio Jahier were Bonaparte's first two patients. She conducted this dual analysis with Rudolph Loewenstein (1898-1976). Alice and Valerio exchanged analysts on several occasions. During his analysis, Valerio began corresponding with Italo Svevo (1861-1928), the author of La Coscienza di Zeno, who imparted his doubts on the therapeutic merits of psychoanalysis. Valerio described his difficult analysis in his letters to Svevo. Bonaparte consulted Freud on the subject, but was not able to prevent Valerio's suicide in 1939. The Princess of Greece encouraged Alice in her vocation as a writer and enabled her to benefit from her connections in literary circles. On the margins of this unpublished story of the two analyses, which is based on archived documents recently made available, we discover the importance of the links which were formed — around Marie Bonaparte — between psychoanalysis and literature. In addition to Italo Svevo, we come across the acerbic writer, Maurice Sachs, as well as the famous novelist, Stefan Zweig.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Curse and Consequence: King Lear's Destructive Narcissism

Roy Schafer

Roy Schafer

455 North End Avenue, Apt 1211, New York, NY 10282, USA — royschafer@mindspring.com

Roy Schafer  

audience response, countertransference, defense, depressive anxiety, destructive narcissism, identification, repression

(Final version accepted 29 April 2010)

Shock, pain and antipathy are common audience responses to King Lear's violent abuse of Cordelia in Scene 1 of King Lear; however, the play then shifts so rapidly to other dramatic relationships and events that it tends to push these feelings out of mind. This shift is here regarded as a seduction to repress the fear and antipathy aroused by Lear. This effect opens the way to sympathetic identification with him in his subsequent humiliation, suffering and madness. These contrasting responses help build a tragic structure in which a more complex Lear becomes the victim of his curse on Cordelia. The seductive design resembles efforts by analytic patients to induce the analyst into repressively neglecting significant aspects of transference that require analytic attention. And Lear's bearing the consequences of his curse is likened to aspects of the decompensations of severely narcissistic patients. Additionally, to the extent that the audience has unconsciously identified with Lear's violence and participated emotionally in other painful scenes, as is likely to be the case, it has been unconsciously reacting as well with guilt and depressive anxiety. These reactions increase readiness to be diverted from destructive narcissism and responses to it.

responseSet.title: Stumbling towards Termination
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Stumbling towards Termination

Mayer Subrin

Mayer Subrin

4430 Quarton Rd, Bloomfield Hills, MI 48302, USA
E-mail: msubrin@comcast.net

Mayer Subrin  

I have found it useful to track the unfolding of an analysis from the opening phase founded on the creation of the therapeutic alliance, to the midphase engagement in and out of the transference of the central organizing fantasies, to the final ending, or termination phase begun in response to an awareness that change has taken place. ‘Stumbling towards termination’ is a subphase of termination. Termination has been contemplated but a specific date has not yet been chosen. When does it occur to the analyst that termination is possible, when to the patient? A sorting out takes place: what has changed, what has not, what has yet to be done, what will be left undone or for another time. It is to better understand ‘stumbling towards termination’ that I present this specific material, which begins with the musings on termination before a specific date was selected, culminating with a record of the last week of the analysis.

The patient had been in analysis with me for 13 years. She had presented herself to her first analyst as depressed, suicidal, thinking of killing herself by stabbing herself in the abdomen with a kitchen knife after undergoing two abortions, for lack of financial support from her husband and extended family. She fled the couch in that first analysis mid-analytic hour, telling the analyst she was leaving to further her professional education, but in fact she feared she would not be able to control her sexual impulses toward him which would ‘destroy his family.’ Following her first analytic appointment with me, as she drove home that evening in the dark, she ran into a tree, at a location where she knew a professional person of her own ethnic background had driven off the road into a pool of water and drowned.

In the 12th year of her analysis, termination was in the air but a date had not yet been set. In recalling the last hour before my usual Christmas/New Year's break, it became clear that during this period of ‘stumbling towards termination,’ every separation, brief or lengthy, became a test of the patient's terminability. The previous month my patient had been away in her family's homeland in Southeast Asia with her daughter, making arrangements for her wedding in June of this year, ordering invitations, buying traditional jewelery, dresses, etc. On her return to her adopted home in the United States, she reported how pleased she was with herself for the competent, mature self-assurance she displayed on this great trip. The ability to make this trip for the purpose of preparing for her daughter's marriage demonstrated that she had overcome the traumatic separation from her own mother when she first visited Southeast Asia as a 13 year old. My patient grew up in Africa as a member of a cultural and racial minority. Fear and anxiety were rampant in her entire community in anticipation of the upcoming political independence of her country.

responseSet.title: Penultimate Interpretation
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Penultimate Interpretation

Yair Neuman

Yair Neuman

P.O. Box 653, Beer-Sheva 84105, Israel - yneuman@bgu.ac.il

Yair Neuman  

Bion, O, symbolization, loophole, interpretation, interdisciplinary studies

Interpretation is at the center of psychoanalytic activity. However, interpretation is always challenged by that which is beyond our grasp, the ‘dark matter’ of our mind, what Bion describes as ‘O’. O is one of the most central and difficult concepts in Bion's thought. In this paper, I explain the enigmatic nature of O as a high-dimensional mental space and point to the price one should pay for substituting the pre-symbolic lexicon of the emotion-laden and high-dimensional unconscious for a low-dimensional symbolic representation. This price is reification - objectifying lived experience and draining it of vitality and complexity. In order to address the difficulty of approaching O through symbolization, I introduce the term ‘Penultimate Interpretation’ - a form of interpretation that seeks ‘loopholes’ through which the analyst and the analysand may reciprocally save themselves from the curse of reification. Three guidelines for ‘Penultimate Interpretation’ are proposed and illustrated through an imaginary dialogue.

All my life I have been imprisoned, frustrated, dogged by common-sense, reason, memories, desires and - greatest bug-bear of all - understanding and being understood.

(Bion, 1991, p. 578)

Q: I am wondering if there is a psychoanalytic way to the truth.

Bion: None whatever.

(Bion, 2005, p. 87)

responseSet.title: The Curious Case of Benjamin Button: Regression and the Angel of Death
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Curious Case of Benjamin Button: Regression and the Angel of Death

Sandra Fenster

Sandra Fenster

360 N. Bedford Drive, Suite 417, Beverly Hills, CA 90210, USA — sandrafen@mac.com

Sandra Fenster  

A curious phenomenon resides in the treatment of regressed patients. In the unconscious, sometimes insistent, desire to be a baby, there is more an impulse towards death than one towards life. I have come to think about the problem as a severe split between two parts of the personality at odds with each other: a healthy, but largely silenced baby trying to grow and a traumatized baby who wants desperately to retreat inside the mother, and to harbor itself in this inside space forever. What interferes with the impulse towards growth is a belief that any change brings catastrophic separation and abandonment. This film essay on The Curious Case of Benjamin Button gives me the opportunity to explore my views about a particular aspect of regression — one which involves idealization as a defense against separation, anxiety, frustration, and loss.

Regressed patients often have early trauma in their histories and do not easily accept the reality of an analytic relationship. Exquisitely sensitive to every nuance of separateness, they fiercely cling to an idealized version of the analyst — which is, of course, a defensive organization. The analyst is expected to be exclusively theirs, never leaving and never subjecting them to agonizing anxieties brought on by space between hours, weekend breaks, vacations, or jealous rivalries with other patients. Any inevitable separation is experienced as desertion. The analyst is felt to turn away coldly; to be completely gone; as good as dead. What comes into play is another early pathological defense: the caricature of a little grown-up, one who needs no one. Protecting themselves from the threat of needing an absent object, these patients are ‘old’ before they have even started to grow up. The movie version of The Curious Case of Benjamin Button speaks directly to these observations and to an important psychoanalytic question: how is regression, as a defensive organization against unbearable anxiety, related to the death instinct?

Psychoanalytic theorists have thought about regression over the history of psychoanalytic development. Freud saw regression as a “turning away from reality”, a “substitutive satisfaction obtained in phantasy” (1918, p. 54), arising from frustration of an instinctual gratification and leading to a “return to the objects first cathected by the libido” (1917, p. 340).

responseSet.title: On: Response to Rachel B. Blass’ Paper about the Value of Attempting to Define the Limits of Psychoanalysis
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On: Response to Rachel B. Blass’ Paper about the Value of Attempting to Define the Limits of Psychoanalysis

Yoram Hazan

Yoram Hazan

4 Cremieux Street, Jerusalem, 93108, Israel
E-mail: hazanfam@netvision.net.il

Yoram Hazan  

Dear Editors,

Psychoanalysis is first of all a therapeutic technique and a way of thinking freely and listening carefully to a patient who is in trouble and needs our help. An attempt to exclude from psychoanalytic ‘territory’ those who do not agree to make a distinction between that which is therapeutic and that which is psychoanalytic (Blass, 2010, p. 95) reminds me of the demand to make a distinction according to an angel's gender - whether it is a female angel or a male angel. This was reasonable for its time (Middle Ages), but sounds bizarre to us now.

Not surprisingly, the metaphor that is called to justify the argument for a distinction between a true psychoanalyst and one who is not a true psychoanalyst has a religious flavor: “The concept of Christianity may be defined very broadly to refer to … some form of belief in Jesus Christ” (p. 91). I wonder if Freud would have ever suggested that a true psychoanalyst is one, and only one, who believes in him.

If some analyses are therapeutically more successful than others, it does not mean that they are not psychoanalytical. Or, if a psychoanalysis is therapeutic it does not mean that it is not truly psychoanalytical. Any distinction between these two is at least unnecessary, if not destructive.

1280

[[RunningHead]]

Emotion and the Psychodynamics of the Cerebellum: A Neuro-Psychoanalytic Analysis and SynthesisFred M. Levin, editor Karnac Books: London, 2009, 253 pp; £24.99

Charles Fisher

Charles Fisher

341 Spruce Street, San Francisco, CA 94118, USA
E-mail: charlep@aol.com

Charles Fisher  

This remarkable text advances a bold and sweeping thesis about the human mind, accompanied by valuable insights, and small gems of summary and description. A simplified version of the book's major thesis is that there is a part of the mind that models and mirrors all other parts of the mental apparatus, interacts with other parts of the mind, and sometimes takes over decision making in difficult and critical situations.

It is important to state at the outset, that Emotion and the Psychodynamics of the Cerebellum: A Neuro-Psychoanalytic Analysis and Synthesis is a difficult book. The unusual structure of the book creates a work in which different portions are suitable for different groups of readers. The first four chapters of the book are written at a level appropriate for a general audience of psychoanalysts, students, or candidates interested in the relationship of psychoanalysis and neuroscience. In this introductory portion of the book, two chapters are multi-authored reviews of contemporary work on sleep and dreaming, written by Fred Levin and an impressive group of neuroscientists, including Jaak Panksepp. The rest of the book is written by Fred Levin alone. Chapters three and four provide a highly readable evaluation of a collection of articles on emotion which appeared in the first issue of the journal Neuro-Psychoanalysis, in 1999. Target articles by Mark Solms and Edward Nersessian (Freud's Theory of Affect: Questions for Neuroscience) and Jaak Panksepp (Emotions as Viewed by Psychoanalysis and Neuroscience: An Exercise in Consilience) are presented and discussed. Commentaries by Antonio Damasio, Andre Green, Joseph LeDoux, Allan Schore, Howard Shevrin, and Clifford Yorke are considered in an extremely insightful manner, providing a superb introduction to leading opinions within the new field of Neuro-Psychoanalysis. Up to this point, the reader has been provided with a superb introductory text. At the same time, this early section of the book presents a number of Fred Levin's provocative and useful ideas on sleep, dreaming, and emotion.

[[RunningHead]]

Editorial

Dana Birksted-Breen and Bob Micheles

Bob Micheles

Bob Micheles  

The quality of the Journal depends on its authors, its editorial board, and particularly its regional editors. The Journal has been immensely fortunate in the gifted and dedicated individuals who have been, continue in, and are now joining that last group. We write to tell you of recent changes.

Jorge Canestri has been appointed to the post of Editor for Europe starting on January 1, 2010. He graduated with first class honours in Medicine, Universidad Nacional de Buenos Aires, Argentina. Having emigrated to Italy in 1976, he is a training and supervising psychoanalyst both of the Asociación Psicoanalítica Argentina and of the Italian Psychoanalytical Association and is currently President of the Italian Psychoanalytical Association. He was director of the ‘Institute of Psychoanalysis’ of the Italian Psychoanalytic Association from 1992 to 1998 and Professor of Psychology of Mental Health at the Roma 3 University, and invited Professor at the Univerité Paris X, Nanterre. In 2004 he received the Mary S. Sigourney Award. He is a member of the Conceptual and Empirical Research committee of the IPA, Chair of the Working Party on Theoretical Issues of the E.P.F and a member of Advisory Board of The Philoctetes Center.

He has written or edited six books; the most recent, Putting Theory to Work: How are Theories actually used in Psychoanalytical Practice? is in press with Karnac Books of London.

Dr. Canestri has a long experience of working for the International Journal of Psychoanalysis, as a member of the European board and as Chair of the Education Section. With his fluency in many languages, his vast editing and writing experience and his extensive international contacts, we are grateful that he has accepted the position and we look forward to our collaboration.

Dr. Canestri replaces Dr. Jean-Michel Quinodoz, who had been the European Editor for a decade, ending in 2003, and who very kindly agreed to return to the post for 2009 while we searched for a new Editor. His long experience of the job coupled with his hard work were invaluable in this interim period. Jean-Michel Quinodoz undertook this task while continuing his work as Editor-in-Chief of the Annuals of the International Journal of Psychoanalysis and we are immensely grateful to him.

responseSet.title: Philosophy, Psychoanalysis and the A-Rational Mind by Linda A.W. Brakel Oxford University Press, Oxford, 2009; 197 pp; $59.95
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Philosophy, Psychoanalysis and the A-Rational Mind by Linda A.W. BrakelOxford University Press, Oxford, 2009; 197 pp; $59.95

Elliot Jurist

Elliot Jurist

138 Street and Convent Avenue, NAC 8/109, New York, NY 10031, USA
E-mail: Ejurist5@gmail.com

Elliot Jurist  

There are some psychoanalysts who would argue that psychoanalysis possesses an adequate theory of mind. There are other psychoanalysts who might question this assumption, pointing to a snapshot history of psychoanalysis from the id to the ego to the self, and observing that the mind is a relatively new concept to this sequence and trajectory. Knowledge of the mind is currently being transformed by emerging knowledge of the brain and, even if one is skeptical about incorporating knowledge of the brain directly into clinical work, the impact of knowledge of the brain on our understanding of the mind seems undeniable. There is no possible justification for the choice simply to ignore neuroscientific evidence about the brain and its interrelation with the mind. At the same time, it is reasonable to wonder whether only neuroscientific ideas about the mind matter. Philosophers and psychologists offer well-developed and clearly formulated ideas about the mind, and these ideas, like neuroscientific ideas, can be and have started to be imported into psychoanalysis in a fruitful way.

In Philosophy, Psychoanalysis and the A-Rational Mind, Brakel makes a strong plea for an interdisciplinary approach to the study of the mind. She believes that philosophical accounts would benefit from attention to the a-rational mind, particularly the realm of primary process thinking, but she also maintains that psychoanalytic thought can be deepened by the conceptual rigor of philosophy.

433

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Commentary on the Material Presented by Ingrid M. Geerken: A New Discourse, a New Love11

Carlos Barredo

Carlos Barredo

Armenia 2448 PB, Buenos Aires 1425, Argentina
E-mail: Calibar1@hotmail.com

Carlos Barredo  

One of the tasks analysts must undertake is bearing witness to their own experience. The construction of a testimonial narrative on clinical practice entails an act of generosity that facilitates dialogue. By fostering debate, which results in comments and various possible readings, such testimonials contribute to the enrichment of analysts' institutional work. In the narrative I am commenting here, Ingrid Geerken also shows a special talent for vividly and beautifully conveying the problems she must consider and solve in order to orient the setting in motion of her analysand's2 cure. I focus my discussion on some aspects of this rich and extensive picture of Beatrice's initial six months of analysis.

Combating ‘home analysis’

The details that illustrate how Beatrice settles into the analytic device — her father's call, the presence of the mother in the office and her attempts to get involved in conversations with the analyst, as well as the urging and pressure to “start” therapy — give us a clear sense of the reason for consultation. They allude to the events that have led this teenager to start analysis. Having been home-schooled, she has failed to follow the course set by the family ideal, which her sister seems to have ‘successfully’ completed — college, a boyfriend, and a degree in business. Her failure causes her to feel stuck, barred from continuing her journey on the path she should allegedly take.

The meaning of the term home-schooled and the fact that it is seen as an apparently natural phenomenon posed an initial question for me regarding cultural customs and their diversity. In my country and in the cultural milieu where I work, a young woman's inability to become part of and perform in public or private educational institutions is seen as an indication of serious and complex circumstances — intense phobias or insurmountable geographical obstacles, among others. Faced with a situation of this sort, we would believe that the socializing process was suffering serious disturbances.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Community of Schools: Psychoanalytic Influences on Peaceful and Academically Strong Schools in USA, Australia, New Zealand and Jamaica11

Stuart Twemlow

Stuart Twemlow

1006 Briarbrook Drive, Houston Texas 77042, USA - stuart.twemlow@gmail.com

Stuart Twemlow  

The panel presented a global effort to create peaceful learning environments for schools, using a framework based on psychoanalytic social systems theory. The presentations began with certain known facts about world-wide efforts to manage bullying. These have resulted in hundreds of programs which should in themselves be effective but in general are not; or, if effective, in a particular town when crossing cultural lines or even within a culture are often ineffective.

The presentation by Stuart Twemlow began with a study entitled Making schools safe again, a report on USA trials. For this presentation he redefined bullying from what he called a trielectic perspective. This shifts the focus on who needs to change from the individual victim and victimizer to the school as a whole, particularly the audience of bystanders who are generally not obviously involved or certainly not highlighted by the trouble caused by the bully-victim interaction. In shifting the focus to the bystander, they become active participants in the bullying process. Members of the community may become abdicating bystanders using rationalizations such as ‘I pay my taxes and teachers and police should keep schools safe and academically strong’ … In our studies the focus is on two elements. One is to improve mentalizing in the school community as a whole, and the other is to improve children's grasp of individual power dynamics that can cause power struggles, including bullying within the school system. We hypothesized that there is an inverse relationship between mentalizing and power dynamics so that a school in which power dynamics are managed in a friendly and helpful way, rather than acted out in power struggles, is a school in which mentalization can occur.

The Peaceful Schools Project of the Menninger Clinic and the Baylor College of Medicine, a three-year cluster randomized controlled trial, involved between 3000 and 4000 students in nine mid-Western elementary schools, K- 5, and some 300 teachers.

responseSet.title: How Translations of Freud's Writings Have Influenced French Psychoanalytic Thinking
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

How Translations of Freud's Writings Have Influenced French Psychoanalytic Thinking11

Jean-Michel Quinodoz

Jean-Michel Quinodoz

53a Chemin des Fourches, 1223 Cologny, Geneva, Switzerland — quinodoz.jm@tele2.ch

Jean-Michel Quinodoz  

French language, historical dimension, International Journal of Psychoanalysis, L'Année Psychanalytique Internationale, Œuvres Complètes de Freud, psychanalyse, translating Freud, translations

(Final version accepted 26 October 2008)

Translations of Freud's writings have had a lasting influence on psychoanalytic thinking in France. They have, all the same, given rise to some conceptual distortions as regards the ego and the id, the ideal ego and the ego ideal, and splitting. Lacan's ‘return to Freud’ certainly reawakened interest in Freud's writings; however, by focusing mainly on Freud's early work, Lacan's personal reading played down the importance of the texts Freud wrote after his metapsychological papers of 1915. The fact that there is no French edition of Freud's complete works makes it difficult for French psychoanalysts to put them in a proper context with respect to his developments as a whole. The Oeuvres Complètes [Complete Works] edition may well turn out to be the equivalent of the Standard Edition, but it is as yet far from complete — and, since the vocabulary employed is far removed from everyday language, those volumes already in print tend to make the general public less likely to read Freud. In this paper, the author evokes certain questions that go beyond the French example, such as the impact that translations have within other psychoanalytic contexts. Now that English has become more or less the lingua franca for communication between psychoanalysts, we have to face up to new challenges if we are to avoid a twofold risk: that of mere standardization, as well as that of a ‘Babelization’ of psychoanalysis.

responseSet.authorMast: Peter Blos and  Lee David Brauer
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Three Regional Approaches to the Worldwide Problem of the Decrease in the Number of Patients and Trainees11

Peter Blos and Lee Brauer

Lee Brauer

65 Memorial Rd, Suite 440, West Hartford, CT 06107 — lbrauer@attglobal.net

Lee Brauer  

In 2003, the IPA established a Development of Psychoanalytic Practice and Training (DPPT) Program to address the perceived crisis in psychoanalysis manifested by a widespread decline in the number of patients seeking analytic treatment and the number of candidates seeking psychoanalytic training in its existing component societies. The desired outcomes for the projects were to increase the number of patients seeking analysis and to increase the number of candidates in full training. Yearly submissions were invited with three-year funding awarded on a competitive basis. The three presentations at this panel represent different approaches to address these problems in the three IPA regions.

Isabelle Lasvergnas and Marie-Eve Lapointe of Montreal presented a description of the process of establishing a new low-fee psychoanalytic clinic within the Société Psychanalytique de Montréal (SPM). Their candid report revealed the struggles within the SPM (mid-1990s to 2006) in considering its self-definitions and boundaries and the concomitant problems in presenting itself to society at large as offering its treatment services. Initially, Montreal's psychoanalytic community was philosophically opposed to the clinic's emphasis on diagnosis and symptoms. It also feared that advertising and outside funding sources might unduly influence the goals and procedures of the clinic.

The clinic was established to serve a socially disadvantaged population, who in the public sector would be treated only with short-term treatments, cognitive therapy and medication. The minimum fee is 10-35 Canadian dollars per 45 minute session. The prospective patients treated would be youths, drop-outs, students, the unemployed, immigrants or single parents-all socially disadvantaged. This population was more likely to have deficient primary object relationships, archaic pathology, and excess and poorly differentiated drives. The clinic believed that this population could be treated using broadened psychoanalytic techniques.
