responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Listening to Hanna Segal: Her Contribution to Psychoanalysis by Jean-Michel Quinodoz Routledge, London, New York, NY, The New Library of Psychoanalysis, 2008; 171 pp; £19.99

Ricardo Stramer

Ricardo Stramer

23 College Crescent, London NW3 5LL, UK
E-mail: ricardo@stramer.freeserve.co.uk

Ricardo Stramer  

Jean-Michel Quinodoz is both a member of the Swiss Psychoanalytical Society and an Honorary Member of the British Psychoanalytical Society. In the present volume, he covers an overview of Hanna Segal's life and a good introduction to her clinical and theoretical work. The book is written in a very original style in that it offers an explanatory text of each concept and theory presented and combines this with a conversation with Hanna

440

Segal around the subject. It also includes discussions with analysts from different parts of the world to give an overview of the impact of her work.

The book is divided into ten chapters, which unfold according to the development of her conceptual work. It begins with a discussion of the early paranoid—schizoid and depressive positions and the central importance of the reparation drive. It then proceeds with the study of aesthetics and creativity and the development of symbolic thought followed by an examination of psychotic functioning, the function of dreams, and the application of psychoanalytic understanding to groups and politics.

The first chapter entitled ‘Hanna Segal: A Psychoanalytic Autobiography. From Birth in Poland to Psychoanalytic Training in London (1918-1947)’ shows how much her childhood experiences as well as her subsequent analysis with Melanie Klein impacted upon the development of her psychoanalytic thinking. Hanna Segal tells us of her traumatized babyhood when in her first three months of life her mother was very ill, Hanna would cry interminably and was weaned suddenly. Another very significant event was the death of her 4 year-old sister when she was 2 years old. She recounts how this trauma led her to have an overemphasized need to repair, which left her throughout her life with a tendency to inhibit her ambition and competitiveness towards peers.

Dr Segal comes across as a forceful and rebellious personality and not always modest. She states: “I think too that at that time Mrs Klein was particularly keen on having students who were both young and brilliant” (p. 12), referring to why Mrs Klein took her as a clinic low fee patient at the beginning of her analysis. She identifies five motivating factors in becoming a psychoanalyst: having both good and bad experiences in life; a successful identification with an internal parental couple; the capacity to work through the countertransference in order to be able to develop a sense of objectivity and be less narcissistically ambitious and more reparative in life. She also states something that might resonate with many analysts, that: “Analysts are people who have been displaced, as if they are looking for a home somewhere” (p. 14).

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis in a ‘Shame Culture’: Japanese Psychoanalytic Insights11

M. Etezady

M. Etezady

hetezady@msn.com

M. Etezady  

In his introduction, Dr. Etezady gave a brief overview of the psychoanalytic, phenomenological and cultural implications of shame. He noted that universally shame is defined as an aversive affective experience containing somatic, experiential and fantasy elements that, along with other essential affects including anxiety and guilt, serves a signal function in self and interactive regulation, empathy, mentalization, maintaining narcissistic homeostasis and adaptive or defensive mechanisms.

Shame is first observable, in its objective, behavioral, facial and body-language, after differentiation and not before the end of the first year, at the dawn of the emergence of intersubjectivity. This roughly coincides with the practicing sub-phase of separation-individuation process and the beginnings of upright locomotion. After the resolution of rapprochement, followed by consolidation from age 24 to 36 months, by the end of the third year, the attainment of the beginnings of self-and-object-constancy pave the way for triangulation and the passing of the oedipal complex. This leads to the formation of superego and ego-ideal as intrapsychic structures responsible for maintaining moral inhibitions as well as boosting ambitions and ideals. Fantasy content in shame is associated with the dread of being regarded as worthlessly deficient and humiliated in the eye of the observer. The observer is initially the mother, whose gleam in the eye affirms and supplants primary narcissism. By the time of latency, the critical eye can be internal and a source of conflict and neurotic compromise formation. Shame implies falling short of expected standards regarding whom one is, while guilt by distinction is related to atonement and punishment for transgression against others, in deed or wish, consciously or unconsciously.

Dr. Etezady left to the panel the task of addressing, in universal or culture-specific terms, questions of gender, social and moral boundaries, as well as matters of private versus public, personal versus non-personal and acceptable versus unacceptable in shame cultures.

[[RunningHead]]

But at the Same Time and on Another Level: Vol. 1: Psychoanalytic Theory and Technique in the Kleinian/Bionian Mode Vol. 2: Clinical Applications in the Kleinian/Bionian Mode by James S. GrotsteinKarnac, London, 2009; Vol. 1, 382 pp, vol. 2, 286 pp

Lawrence Brown

Lawrence Brown

37 Homer Street, Newton Centre, MA 02459, USA
E-mail: larry.brown8@comcast.net

Lawrence Brown  

James Grotstein is among the most prolific of American analysts, having published over 250 papers and numerous books since 1977 when his two-part paper on schizophrenia appeared in the International Journal of Psychoanalysis. In the introduction to the first paper Grotstein (1977) stated that his aim was to achieve an integrated perspective on the nature of schizophrenia but that this goal was limited by “an inability to reconcile all points of view from any one point of view” (p. 403). Indeed, he proceeded to offer a sweeping overview of psychoanalytic and non-psychoanalytic outlooks which not only demonstrated a vast knowledge of schizophrenia but also reflected an incredible capacity to integrate seemingly disparate frames of reference.

Many readers familiar with his work will find, as I do, that one inevitably has the reaction of ‘Hmm, I never quite thought of it that way,’ a feeling of surprise and wonderment I recall having when reading his interpretation of Michelangelo's Pietà (Grotstein, 2000). Grotstein noted the sadness in Mary's eyes as she looked into the face of Christ as though she knew the future torment awaiting her infant son and likens that experience to the analyst's countertransference upon realizing the emotional turmoil that the analysand must endure.

These two volumes, Grotstein's third book in the last three years, are aptly named But At the Same Time and On Another Level, a title that expresses the ease with which he pivots to approach the topic he is investigating — whether a theoretical point or the discussion of a patient — first from one angle and then from another. Grotstein's easy access to binocular vision is clear evidence of Bion's (1962) influence, and of other factors as well. Grotstein tells us at the outset of Volume 1 that he had four analyses: the first by an Orthodox Freudian who had been analyzed by Freud; the second, Fairbairnian/Object Relational, by an analyst analyzed by Sterba; the third by Bion who was analyzed by Rickman and Klein; and the last by an Orthodox Kleinian. Grotstein seems to have internalized all these influences and to have developed the impressive knack of observing one phenomenon from multiple vertices, including his own creative intertwining of different schools of thought.

responseSet.title: The Shining: All Work and no Play …
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Shining: All Work and no Play …

Noel Hess

Noel Hess

Flat 4, Mullion Court, 112 Finchley Road, London NW3 5JH — Noel.Hess@candi.nhs.uk

Noel Hess  

The narrative of Stanley Kubrick's well-known film of 1980 is deceptively simple: Jack Torrance, with his wife Wendy and son Danny, takes a job as caretaker to the Overlook Hotel, high in the Colorado Mountains, for five months during the winter while the hotel is empty. He is an ex-teacher and believes himself to be a writer, so this is an opportunity to write. During the course of their stay, he becomes mad and attempts to murder his family. He succeeds only in killing Halloran, the hotel's black cook, who returns to the hotel sensing danger. Jack's wife and son escape to safety. He freezes to death in pursuit of his son in the hotel's maze.

But there is more: the hotel has a history of murderousness. The previous caretaker, ten years earlier, has killed his wife and twin daughters. Also, Danny possesses a special gift, the shining, which allows him to see events from the past and to see into the future. He locates his ‘shining’ in his imaginary friend Tony. Halloran also possesses this ability.

The central characters, then, are Jack, Wendy, Danny and Halloran and, crucially, the Overlook Hotel itself, an extraordinary feat of interior design with its combination of maze-like corridors and huge, empty, cavernous spaces. The film explores the internal world of a man gradually driven to madness and murder; the themes of ‘play’ and ‘home’ are central to understanding this inner collapse. I want to discuss how we can understand his descent into madness, the form his madness takes, and the interesting complicity it generates in the audience.

In an interview Kubrick gave at the time of the film's release (Ciment, 2001), he stated:

Jack comes to the hotel psychologically prepared to do its murderous bidding. He doesn't have very much further to go, for his anger and frustration to become completely uncontrollable. He is bitter about his failure as a writer, he is married to a woman for whom he has only contempt, he hates his son. In the hotel, at the mercy of its powerful evil, he is quickly ready to fulfil its dark role.

responseSet.title: Reading Minds: Mentalization, Irony and Literary Engagement
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Reading Minds: Mentalization, Irony and Literary Engagement

Elisa Galgut

Elisa Galgut

Private Bag, Cape Town, South Africa — elisa.galgut@uct.ac.za or elisagalgut@gmail.com

Elisa Galgut  

interdisciplinary, interpretation, literature, mentalization, reading

(Final version accepted 2 November 2009)

The concept of mentalization has recently provided a fertile resource for thinking about various issues in psychoanalysis, including attachment, children s play, personality disorders and the work of interpretation within the analytic setting. Mentalization also provides fruitful ways of thinking about how we read. This paper will suggest that book reading is akin to mind reading: engaging with certain literary texts is akin to understanding the minds of others from the subjective perspective required by mentalization. This way of thinking about literature provides a useful way of understanding its value. The paper will focus specifically on the uses of irony and free indirect speech in Jane Austen s novel Persuasion. Austen's use of literary techniques provides a way of understanding the inner lives of her characters via the ironic voice of the implied author, and requires the reader to engage in the kinds of understanding and insight required for mentalization.

responseSet.title: Universal Fantasy in Latency: Separation, Attachment and Sexuality in Julio Cortázar's Bestiary
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Universal Fantasy in Latency: Separation, Attachment and Sexuality in Julio Cortázar's Bestiary11

Ellen Shustorovich and Lissa Weinstein

Lissa Weinstein

137th and Convent Avenue, New York, NY 10031, USA — shustor@yahoo.com

Lissa Weinstein  

interdisciplinary studies, psychoanalytic theory

(Final version accepted 18 March 2010)

This paper joins recent efforts to build a conceptual bridge between psychoanalysis and attachment theory. A psychoanalytic reading of Julio Cortázar's Bestiary, a story about a young girl sent away for a summer vacation, is used to conceptualize the relationship between attachment and sexuality during latency. Middle childhood is seen as a pivotal period when cognitive maturation, internalization of parental figures, increasing independence and importance of those outside the family make fantasy the central adaptive mode of functioning. As such, the literature on attachment, which focuses on observed events, has to be supplemented with investigations of the actively created fantasies of that age period.

responseSet.title: Clinical Approaches to Dreams
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Clinical Approaches to Dreams11

Olivier Bonard and Cordelia Schmidt-Hellerau

Cordelia Schmidt-Hellerau

obonard@gmail.com

Cordelia Schmidt-Hellerau  

Cordelia Schmidt-Hellerau introduced the panel by commending the fact that every IPA Congress contains presentations on dreams in the analytic session: which psychic creation contains more authenticity, more interconnections between desires and needs, and between life and death? Analysts are pleased by dreams that poetically open up to the patient all the aspects of his psychic apparatus. It happened that all the panellists chose to speak on how to approach the anxiety generated by dreams in the session; it was therefore on this difficult and highly topical aspect that the moderator focused the discussion.

Juan-Carlos Weissman (Argentina) chose to describe his oscillation between two interpretative standpoints towards a dream that, as often occurs, leaves the dreamer feeling anxious: one approach is to ask the patient about the meaning of the dream and wait for further perspectives to open up on the unconscious; the other seeks to relieve the patient of an anxiety that is in danger of hindering the continuation of the work in the session. Weissman decides on his strategy after taking in the quality of his patient's anxiety in the context of the transference at that moment and the difficulties of his existence; the interpretative inspiration that relies on the countertransference is also a factor in this choice. The interpretation of the dream thus follows the current needs of the treatment and resembles either a quest for meaning of the kind that Freud liked to practise or a quest for treatment, a goal prioritized by Ferenczi. The interpretative decision is often taken, however, after the meaning has begun to be investigated, when the analyst feels that the patient is reaching a critical point, that is to say, his anxiety is reaching a threshold beyond which it would be unproductive or harmful to continue the investigation. Such a quest for equilibrium in the session will lead to various types of interpretation that target defence mechanisms, the transference, the manifest content and the latent content in turn in order to reduce the anxiety level and allow the openness to the unconscious provided by the dream to be restored. Thus Juan-Carlos Weissman seeks the rational paths that lead him towards a real interpretative choice.

responseSet.title: Relative Motion: Translation and Therapy
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Relative Motion: Translation and Therapy

George Craig

George Craig

39 Ferrers Road, Lewes, East Sussex BN7 1PY, UK — gwcraig16@googlemail.com

George Craig  

alignment, latent, word-event

(Final version accepted 20 January 2009)

The central argument is that analysts/therapists and translators/interpreters have a great deal more in common than is commonly supposed. The key element in this sharing is their essential aloneness in the moment and act of interpreting, and their intellectual and affective response to that aloneness. What marks this off as both important and exceptional is its contrast with the untroubling world of study and the comforting world of professional membership and practice. The professional worlds are abundantly documented; the individual experience has had nothing like so much attention. The focus of the argument is on how significant meanings are established, in a context in which no meanings can be taken for granted. The purpose of the argument is to show that each kind of practitioner may have much to learn from the other.

responseSet.title: Women Analyzing Women: The Difficult Patient
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Women Analyzing Women: The Difficult Patient11

Giovanna Ambrosio and Teresa Flores

Teresa Flores

flores_mt@yahoo.com.br; giovanna.ambrosio@libero.it

Teresa Flores  

This panel was chaired by Giovanna Ambrosio, the overall Chair of the Committee on Women and Psychoanalysis (COWAP), who began by greeting everybody and by introducing the participants in this panel: Elyn Saks, Raquel Berman and Arlene Richards.

Elyn Saks began her autobiographical presentation, Her journey through madness, sharing with the audience her suffering related to her schizophrenic disorder and breakdown. She focused on her feeling of derealization, depersonalization and delusional ideas, bridging her psychological suffering and the vicissitudes of her life and of her family relationships and conflicts. She spoke of her several therapeutic experiences which combined both psychiatric and psychoanalytical approaches. Regarding the theme of the panel Elyn was asked to what degree the gender of the analyst was significant in her treatment. Having been treated by male and female therapists who naturally had different approaches, she focused on how both, independent of their gender, had provided her with a very maternal and paternal containment and holding, allowing a process of integration and identification. She emphasized as the most important issue the greater awareness she gained of her inner conflicts, insight and working through, allowing her a better knowledge of her inner world and how to deal with it.

Arlene Richards presented her paper, Psychoanalysis and desire, sharing with the audience one hour of a young woman's therapy, whose hallucinations, delusions and idea of reference had led to her diagnosis of schizophrenia. She had been in the psychoanalytic treatment for five years and had been medicated to relieve those symptoms. The analyst emphasized that her therapeutic aim was to try to eliminate the negative symptoms. This presentation raised some questions about what could be considered as a psychotherapeutic or a psychoanalytical approach as well as how to deal with a combined treatment of psychoanalysis and psychiatry.

Arlene Richards herself questioned whether her treatment of this patient could be considered psychoanalysis. Although they meet four times each week, usually face to face, on occasion they have left the office to walk around outside as they talked, and several times they have met with her mother to discuss what the family thinks of her treatment.

responseSet.title: The strange case of Dr Mantle and Dr Mantle: David Cronenberg's Dead Ringers
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The strange case of Dr Mantle and Dr Mantle: David Cronenberg's Dead Ringers11

Joaquin Cañizares

Joaquin Cañizares

172 Leith Walk Edinburgh, EH6 5EA, UK — joaquincanizares@hotmail.co.uk

Joaquin Cañizares  

Prologue

Confronting the reading of the film Dead Ringers (1988), the reader resists it by insisting that the nature of its psychological themes are open to all kinds of narrative interpretations with no prospect of reducing any one of them to a correct one. This is a way of stating what has always been tacitly known: that there is no single point of view but a tide of shifting ones, and, like waves breaking from one image or word to another, they bounce, covering and disclosing the ambiguities and complexities of human nature. The actual potential space of the analytic situation defeats the analyst's attempt to achieve a single theoretical point of view because the emerging narrative has simultaneously been dictated by the other listener — the analysand. For the analyst is not the only recipient of communication in the psychoanalytic dialogue. This point is neatly implied by Schafer: “The end product of this interweaving of texts is a radically new, jointly authored work or way of working” (1983, p. 219). It is equally evident that the analyst even influences the communication of the patient's material (Klauber, 1986).

In short, the point I want to make is that film analysis is something similar to what happens in an analytic session, and which has much in common with Bion's legendary invitation to attend the analysis without “memory or desire” (Bion, 1970, p. 41).

Fortunately, however, this film offers to this reader a ‘front row view’ of a comprehensive range of emotions that allures one to reflect, and respond psychoanalytically to the interweaving of what, at first, seems complex and puzzling, helping to bring about a sense of an overall recognition and understanding. What follows, then, is doubtless a highly personalized response to a remarkably instructive film about the fascinating mystery of twin brothers and their fate.

Dead Ringers

responseSet.title: Daniel Wildlöcher's Response to F. Busch and H. Kächele
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Daniel Wildlöcher's Response to F. Busch and H. Kächele

Daniel Widlöcher

Daniel Widlöcher

79, boulevard Vincent Auriol, 75013 Paris, France — d.h.widlocher@wanadoo.fr

Daniel Widlöcher  

The criteria that help us to establish links and differences between psychoanalysis and psychotherapy are obviously still very much the subject of debate.

For his part, Kächele emphasizes the fact that this derives from the ambiguity that was typical of Freud's approach to the question. For Busch, the issue is every bit as important for contemporary psychoanalysis as it was in the past — and, he maintains, it will be even more central in the future. They both argue — and I completely agree with them here — that our approach to this question ought to be in terms of dimensions rather than of categories. Instead of attempting to individualize methods and forms of treatment (ranging from psychoanalysis stricto sensu to ‘supportive’ psychotherapies), it would be better to say that each particular case lies somewhere between the ‘psychoanalytic’ dimension and its ‘psychotherapeutic’ counterpart, depending on the objectives pursued — from the discovery of unconscious formations all the way through to the lifting of symptoms. I would argue that what is fundamentally at stake here is how we define and understand the processes that enable us to characterize the variables that are to be taken into account.

Kächele suggests an approach that is above all empirical. He speaks of quantitative criteria — although, to my mind, he does not define these sufficiently clearly. Does this mean that they are indeed too complex, and that we have therefore to be satisfied with what each psychoanalyst sees as appropriate for any given patient? Kächele draws a parallel between this empirical flexibility — which is indeed essential — and the pluralism of practice and theory that is so much a feature of contemporary psychoanalysis. That approach, however, may well lead to the idea that, when all is said and done, the psychoanalytic dimension refers quite simply to what psychoanalysts do! But surely the only proper way to define a psychoanalyst is in terms of the training that he or she must have undergone. Kächele is fully aware of this difficulty inherent in his approach.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Child/Adolescent Analysts at Work in the Present Space11

Elizabeth Tuters

Elizabeth Tuters

72 Woodlawn Avenue West, Toronto, ON M4V1G7, Canada-etuters@sympatico.ca

Elizabeth Tuters  

These two clinically-based panels, designed to throw more light on the problems of treating aggression and sexuality, were organized by Elizabeth Tuters (Canada) for the IPA (COCAP) and the Association for Child Psychoanalysis Inc. (ACP).

Adolescent Panel

Nellie Vlietstra-Zock (Netherlands) was the first panelist. She presented riveting clinical material from her work with a late adolescent girl struggling with anxiety as to whether to live or die, which reflected unspoken family secrets. Carlos Barredo (Argentina) discussed this material framed by a modification of the phrase by Wittgenstein, ‘whereof one cannot speak…thereof one cannot stay silent’ (subtitle of History beyond Trauma [Davoine and Gaudillière, 2004]), who stated the cases presented therein had very similar features to the case presented by Vlietstra-Zock. Barredo commented that, in the brief space of a page of presented material, the narrative of the analyst places us in the center of the plot where the threads of multiple stories, past and present, intertwine. In these cases, managing the transference and the style of intervention constitutes a creative attempt by the analyst, yet this may indeed offer special difficulties for their analytic approach. In Barredo's opinion, acting out by way of repetition gives up glimpses of the past, not as archeological ruins, but as something new that, once extracted from the dead context, can become ‘a memory as it flashes up in a moment of danger’ (according to the poetic formulation of Walter Benjamin). What surfaces only as action is the counterpart of what the analyst perceives as silenced, taboo, suppressed in the narrative of the analysand's daily life. The suicide theme that hovers in the therapy sessions creates ongoing anxiety for both analyst and analysand. Barredo points out that only her analyst can help her in the path of casting off the blissful silence that keeps her alienated in deadening identifications. Only running the risk of following

responseSet.title: On: The Possibly Narcissistic Difficulties Among Scientific Cultures: A Response to Marty's 1952 Key Paper
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On: The Possibly Narcissistic Difficulties Among Scientific Cultures: A Response to Marty's 1952 Key Paper

Nathan Szajnberg MD

Nathan Szajnberg

161 W. 61st, St. Suite 4A, New York, NY 10023, USA E-mail: nmoshe@gmail.com

Nathan Szajnberg   Freud Professor, Wallerstein Research Fellow in Psychoanalysis

Dear Editors

Marty's key 1952 paper, The narcissistic difficulties presented to the observer by the psychosomatic problem and its introduction and commentary raise at least two, possibly related issues (Aisenstein and Smadja, 2010; Marty, 2010[1952]). First, while Marty uses ‘stomach ulcer’ as one of his two modela of psychosomatic illness and even mentions physiological “hypermotricity, congestion and hypertonia and hypersecretion” [p. 344], neither contemporary commentators mention the landmark discovery of Heliobacter pylori (H. pylori) and its seminal role in the pathophysiology of gastric and peptic ulcer and stomach cancer (Marshall and Warren, 1984). This would be the physical factor that Alexander speculated was associated with the personality profile of those with these illnesses (Alexander and French, 1948). Subsequent research demonstrates an interaction between the H. pylori and variable stomach secretion in those subjects who develop these psychosomatic illnesses. While Gottlieb (2010) mentions the relationships between emotion and gastric secretion and also both Dunbar and Alexander's work from the mid-20th century, he does not mention these more recent advances.

This leads to the second issue. A way to account for this absence of discussing the H. pylori work almost two decades old is a significant cultural difference between French and Anglo-German science. Dyson (2006) argues persuasively that this difference in the physical sciences is due to differences in French versus Anglo-German models of how physical science should progress. French physical science has a tradition of reasoning through problems, beginning with Diderot, but exemplified by Pierre de Maupertuis (1698—1759), who ‘discovered’ the principle of least action. Anglo-German physical science focused more on experimental evidence, influenced in large part by Francis Bacon (who, ironically only wrote about empiricism). Dyson continues that this difference in conclusions about the physical world is resolved by the larger community of scientists, which resulted in a resonance between theorizing and seeking empirical proof.

responseSet.title: The Medea fantasy: An Inevitable Burden during Prenatal Diagnostics?
responseSet.authorMast: Marianne Leuzinger-Bohleber,  Nicole Pfenning and  Spiros Simitis
responseSet.authorCitation: Leuzinger-Bohleber, M., Pfenning, N. & Simitis, S.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Medea fantasy: An Inevitable Burden during Prenatal Diagnostics?11

Marianne Leuzinger-Bohleber , Nicole Pfenning and Spiros Simitis

Spiros Simitis

m.leuzinger-bohleber@sigmund-freud-institut.de

Spiros Simitis  

When Sigmund Freud published his reflections on modern civilization, for example, in Civilization and Its Discontents, he could not yet anticipate the enormous developments in modern technology in the life sciences in the 20th and 21st centuries. Do these developments confront us more and more with new facets of a Faustian seduction? Are we increasingly ‘playing God’ as many contemporary critical authors of modernity are claiming?

Technique has always been and still is part of culture and thus of human nature. Without techniques we would be unable to live and to master our lives. However, techniques, once developed, force us to make responsible decisions about whether and how we want to use them. We also have to find forms of coping with the ambivalences and dilemmas which are connected to most modern technologies.

The panel, ‘The Medea Fantasy: An Inevitable Burden During Prenatal Diagnostics?’, was devoted to the particularly sensitive field of prenatal and genetic diagnostics. Professor Spiros Simitis (Frankfurt/Main), who chaired the panel, has been the head of the German National Ethics Council. In his introduction he gave an overview on the current political debates on bio-ethics in the field of genetic and prenatal diagnostics (PND) and on how to use stem cells from embryos after artificial insemination. Achievements in genetic research produce ethical and moral dilemmas which need to be the subject of reflection and debate in modern societies. Denial of ambivalences that moral dilemmas arouse constitutes a threat to societies as well as to individual persons.

Stephan Hau (Stockholm), the first presenter, gave an overview on an Euro-pean-wide study, Ethical dilemmas due to prenatal and genetic diagnostics (EDIG), which was carried out from 2005 to 2008 (Leuzinger-Bohleber, Engels and Tsiantis, 2008). A large interdisciplinary research team investigated the above-mentioned dilemmas.

responseSet.title: On: Fairbairn and Dynamic Structure
responseSet.authorMast: Graham S. Clarke and  Paul Finnegan
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On: Fairbairn and Dynamic Structure

Graham Clarke PHD and Paul Finnegan MD FRCPC

Paul Finnegan

47 Lord Holland Road
Colchester, Essex CO2 7PS, UK
E-mail: graham@essex.ac.uk

554 Spadina Avenue
Toronto, Ontario, Canada, M5S 2J9
E-mail: pfinnegan@sympatico.ca

Paul Finnegan  

Dear Editors,

We welcome and applaud Thomas Ogden's timely reminder (IJP 91: 101-18, 2010) that there is still much to learn from reading, and rereading, Fairbairn. We think that he presents useful examples of object relationships between dynamic structures (ego-structures and internal objects) as relationships between people — which is how Fairbairn understood them.

Fairbairn made what we think is a key statement about his overall approach in his only contribution to the Controversial Discussions:

… the explanatory concept of ‘phantasy’ has now been rendered obsolete by the concepts of ‘psychical reality’ and ‘internal objects’ which the work of Mrs Klein and her followers has done so much to develop; and in my opinion the time is now ripe for us to replace the concept of ‘phantasy’ by a concept of an ‘inner reality’ peopled by the Ego and its internal objects. These internal objects should be regarded as having an organized structure, an identity of their own, an endopsychic existence and an activity as real within the inner world as those of any objects in the outer world …

(King and Steiner, 1991, pp. 358-61)

It is instructive to note that this proposal was rejected by Susan Isaacs on behalf of the Kleinian group with the following comment:

Dr. Fairbairn … overemphasizes and distorts part of Mrs Klein's theories to the point of caricature. He over-substantifies internal objects and makes them far too independent, leaving wishes and feelings and the id generally out of account.

(King and Steiner, 1991, p. 458)

In his 1944 paper Endopsychic structure considered in terms of object-relationships, Fairbairn clearly states that his approach grew out his critique of impulse psychology and instinct theory:

In a word ‘impulses’ cannot be considered apart from the endopsychic structures, which they energize, and the object relationships, which they enable these structures to establish; and, equally, ‘instincts’ cannot profitably be considered as anything more than forms of energy, which constitute the dynamic of such endopsychic structures.

responseSet.title: “Till Destruction Sicken”: The Catastrophe of Mind in Macbeth
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

“Till Destruction Sicken”: The Catastrophe of Mind in Macbeth

Carole Tarantelli

Carole Tarantelli

Via Malta 6a, Rome, Italy 00198 — c.taran@mclink.it

Carole Tarantelli  

defense, enactment, annihilation, destructivity, catastrophe, malignant regression, omnipotence, violence

(Final version accepted 23 March 2010)

The author examines the tragedy of Macbeth from the vertex of its portrait of the effects of the hero's abandonment to the “blindest fury of destructiveness” (Freud, 1930, p. 121), using aspects of psychoanalytic thought to illuminate the theme. She affirms that Macbeth's immediate transformation in phantasy from loyal subject to future assassin threatens to flood his psyche with the uncontainable energy of destruction and thus to produce a psychic catastrophe. The remainder of the play is then examined as a representation of Macbeth's attempts to defend himself from that catastrophe: the only objects whose existence he can tolerate are those that cannot challenge his possession of the crown, which leads him to attempt to destroy all opposition. Since this is impossible, his alternative is to denude his mind of its perception of the reality in which the actions of others will inevitably produce future transformations which he is unable to control. The play's last soliloquy anticipates the final state, the repose of emptiness; it portrays a mind, emptied of emotion, looking at a world which it has denuded of meaning.

responseSet.title: On: A Confusion of Tongues between Psychoanalysis and Philosophy
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On: A Confusion of Tongues between Psychoanalysis and Philosophy

Erik Gann

Erik Gann

3628 Sacramento Street, Suite A, San Francisco, CA 94118, USA E-mail: Egan2000@aol.com

Erik Gann  

Dear Editors,

I read with great interest the sophisticated essay by Gadi Taub (2009), whose clarity of exposition did, indeed, illuminate some of the key confusions in the debate between the so-called Drive Theories and the Relational ones. I was, however, left with two lingering, troubling impressions that spring from Taub's ‘Afterthought: Psychoanalysis and its Social Context,’ the last section of the paper. It is here in my reading that Taub's own personal, moral commitment in the theoretical debate breaks through in the reference to “Freud's Hobbesian conception of human beings as egotistic, pleasure-seeking monads” and, then, shortly after, “the portrait [Freud's] of humans as isolated self-interested units.” Certainly, the justification sought by some philosophically, as Taub so eloquently clarifies, of a radical autonomy of the individual might result in this kind of extremist, narcissistic pursuit.

But, to my understanding, that description suggests a misconception, and one that is widespread in our field and beyond, of the Freudian conceptualization of the Instinctual Drive, as delineated in Instincts and their vicissitudes. The definition of the Instinctual Drive includes four components: source, pressure, aim and object. The object is intrinsic to the ‘drive’; there is no objectless drive (whether the object be another person, one's self, one's own body part, a non-human part of the environment, etc.). Freud's drive theory is a relational theory, albeit that the ultimate source is postulated as the organism's need for biological homeostasis, which from the beginning requires attachment and leads in the mature organism hopefully to meaningful and intimate attachments. Seen in this light, the Freudian position does reflect a complex network of basic motivational forces in the adult, as Taub so clearly indicates earlier in the essay in the discussion of ‘mixed models.’

As for ‘monads’ and ‘isolated self-interested units’ — the discourse at that instant seems more uncharacteristically reductionistic than in the rest of the carefully deliberated articulations. Even given the inextricably intersubjective matrix in which we humans are enmeshed, no human being has ever nor will ever know or experience the inner world of another. That private realm can only be approximated, even in the hands of the most empathic and intuitive other. Whether it is the ‘long shadow of Thomas Hobbes' or a Cartesian being that only knows he is there if he can sense his own process of mentation, call it monadism or self-interest — as the kids say, it is what it is.

responseSet.title: La Vita Nuova
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

La Vita Nuova

Ingrid Geerken

Ingrid Geerken

Cleveland Psychoanalytic Center, Heights Medical Building, 2460 Fairmount Blvd.
Suite 201, Cleveland Heights, OH 44106, USA
E-mail: ingrid.geerken@gmail.com

Ingrid Geerken  

When I retrieve Beatrice from the waiting-room, her mother is there too. As her mother routinely attempts to engage me in conversation, Beatrice stands with her head hung, fidgeting. When I ask her in the session how she feels while her mother and I speak, she answers, after a thoughtful pause: “In the middle.” Beatrice is 19 years old but comes across as prepubescent. She is slender with big brown eyes and wears pre-teen clothes: tight jeans, an undersized hoodie and tennis shoes. In our interactions she is tense and suspicious, but strikes me, too, as fierce and bright.

Beatrice was referred to me by a senior colleague who had already recommended an analysis. Her father, a businessman who had recently been transferred to a nearby city, called me to discuss the possibility of treatment. He then asked that I call his wife to set up the initial appointment. In retrospect, it may have been important to have Beatrice call me herself. In this early interplay, I had placed Beatrice “in the middle” between her parents and unwittingly reproduced the dynamic we would struggle with in these first six months of treatment.

Although I structured the first sessions as an evaluation, both Beatrice and her parents were prepared to begin the analysis, and so, as Beatrice perched rigidly and nervously on the edge of the chair, she articulated the desire to begin as soon as possible. Because of this, the evaluation felt foreshortened, and I learned less than I might have from the initial interviews.

Beatrice did not have very much information about her childhood. She did, however, remark that she was a “bratty kid,” but that she gave it up (at about age 13) because “it was too tiring to be angry all the time.” Except for kindergarten, Beatrice has been homeschooled by her mother her entire life. Beatrice did not know the reasons for her homeschooling, nor did she think it a question to ask. She has a sister, two years older, also homeschooled, who has successfully gone to college, has a boyfriend, and is pursuing a business degree. Beatrice does not want to go to college and has not found a career she might want to pursue. At the time of the evaluation she had a job stuffing envelopes. She was also taking classes in Japanese, belly dancing, and capoeira, a Brazilian martial art. Beatrice feels “stuck.

responseSet.title: On: Contemporary Art and Hanna Segal's Thinking on Aesthetics by Adela Abella
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On: Contemporary Art and Hanna Segal's Thinking on Aesthetics by Adela Abella

Moran Shoham PHD

Moran Shoham

Derech-HaYam 113, Haifa, Israel
E-mail: moran9898@hotmail.com

Moran Shoham  

Dear Editors

I would like to say a few words about Dr. Abella's paper (IJP 91: 163-81), as these seem to belong in the context of its discussion. The paper presents notions of contemporary art via the works and writings/statements of Duchamp and Cage. Contemporary art, it is suggested, offers a shift of emphasis, from reparation of destructive impulses through form — as suggested by Segal — to an emphasis on the process of rethinking experience, which is more in line with Bion's conceptualization of learning from experience. Bion's notion of transformation is presented as a form of aesthetic truth, which to my mind is a valid and valuable point.

Still, a few points come to mind. It is not always clear that the relationship between an artist's statements to his works and creations is a straight and simple one. There is a way in which by the time an artist begins to grasp the meaning of his project he is likely to have moved to different one (Bloom, 1973; Frey, 1964). Indeed, the gap between meaning and its origin is clearly established within the area of psychoanalytic thinking and should perhaps also apply to an artist's comments about his work. Even taking the paper as a source of reference, Duchamp is said to suggest that one should approach ready-mades “as if you had no aesthetic emotion” (p. 173, my italics) — thus expressing complex relations between conscious intent and the end product. So what an artist says about his work and intent cannot be taken as an articulation of the comprehensive meaning and resulting impressions of his work. The appreciation of this complexity seems to be absent from the paper as the artist's comments are taken to represent the meaning of his work.

Duchamp, it is said, aims to move beyond “the tyranny of taste” (p. 172) to a realm of “total absence of good or bad taste” (p. 173) which opens the way to the process of free thinking that “stands on a different level than the beauty sought by classical art” (p. 173, Abella's phrasing).

responseSet.title: Horst Kächele's Response to F. Busch and D. Widlöcher
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Horst Kächele's Response to F. Busch and D. Widlöcher

Horst Kächele

Horst Kächele

8, 89081 Ulm, Germany — horst.kaechele@uni-ulm.de, http://www.horstkaechele.de

Horst Kächele  

F. Busch's effort to distinguish psychoanalysis from psychotherapy elaborates that “the goals and treatment outcomes differentiate the two forms of treatment”. He points out that the “methods of working become distinguished based upon these factors”. In his view “the process of knowing is as important as what is known”. It is easy for me to agree that, in relatively successful psychoanalytic treatments, what can be achieved is a way of knowing, and not simply knowing. Whether the parameters of psychotherapy make it unlikely that the psychotherapy patient can reach this goal depends on the way a treatment format is installed. The shorter a treatment the less likely this capacity may develop. The interesting comparison resides in the comparison of long-term psychoanalytic treatments with low and high frequency as the German catamnestic project (Leuzinger-Bohleber et al., 2003) has studied. They found that, in high-frequency patients, the capacity for self-reflection and the internalization of the function of the analyst was more comprehensive, the reflection of what has changed more differentiated and the development of potential resources more creative than in long-term psychoanalytic therapy of low frequency. As these treatments were performed by well-trained analysts we might take home the message from this study that long-term treatments (average of 4 years' treatment in both groups) do achieve similar similar symptomatic outcome, but the more intensive version provides better chances of developing the self-analytic function.

These findings of the German study correspond only partially to the findings from the Stockholm study by Falkenström and his collegues (2007) that Busch cites. They carefully studied a random sample of 2 × 10 patients from psychoanalysis and long-term psychotherapy (out of the total sample) and identified categories of different types of post-treatment developments. Results indicate that the variation within treatment groups was large, and that development may continue in several ways after termination. The most striking difference between psychoanalysis and psychotherapy was not, as hypothesized, in the self-analytic function, but in various self-supporting strategies described by former analysands, but not by former psychotherapy patients. However, only self-analysis was significantly correlated with posttermination improvement across both treatments (quoted from the abstract of Falkenström et al., 2007, p. 629).

On: Response to Letter from Epstein

David Bell

David Bell

Flat 4, Mullion Court, 112 Finchley Road, London NW3 5JH
E-mail: dbell@tavi-port.nhs.uk

David Bell  

Dear Editors,

I am very grateful for Dr Epstein's careful reading of my paper and particularly for drawing attention to the work of Roy Bhaskar. I have a very high regard for this work and Dr Epstein is quite right in pointing out that my approach is consonant with Bhaskar's ‘Transcendental Realism’ which has been an influence upon me, perhaps more than I realized.

Those who wish to read more about the relevance of Bhaskar's views to psychoanalysis will find the following articles very helpful (Collier, 1981; Will, 1986).

Thanks again to Dr Epstein.

responseSet.title: Destini delle Identità [Fates of Identities] by Lucio Russo Borla, Roma, 2009; 239 pp; €23.00
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Destini delle Identità [Fates of Identities] by Lucio RussoBorla, Roma, 2009; 239 pp; €23.00

Marina Breccia

Marina Breccia

Via San Ermolao, 1, 56011 Calci, Pisa, Italy
E-mail: marinabreccia@tiscali.it

Marina Breccia  

Only because I can connect a variety of given representations in one consciousness, is it possible that I can represent to myself the identity of consciousness in these

1550

representations; … the analytical unity of apperception is possible only under the presupposition of a synthetical unity.

(Kant, 1780[2003], p. 77)

Human beings are never fixed in space or time as others think, and are not to be found even where they themselves tell others to inquire. […] Among these temporalities, which would best accent the multiple dynamisms of our inner being? […] In the realm of fire, each of us becomes a brazier of beings. Where inner fire is concerned, our energy and life source, is not perhaps the most important rhythm that of ash which keeps tomorrow's fires burning?

(Bachelard, 1988 [1990], p. 19)1

The process of reading Lucio Russo's book Fates of Identities brought to mind these authors, so, following the unconscious, I decided to start with them. What they have in common, is, I think, on the one hand the issue of the unique and the multiple and on the other the issue of being, with its temporal dimension of becoming.

Lucio Russo states:

My intention is to integrate the horizontal line of historical research, founded on the origin of the ontological and fixed aspects of identity, with a genealogy of the emergence of forces which escape consciousness, following a vertical line. This means I will interrogate, more than already constituted identities, the formative processes which underlie them.

(p. 12)

Thus he goes straight to the heart of the issue, that is, he declares that he will consider identity from the point of view of an analyst. And so a question arises: But how does the psychoanalyst encounter all of this? How do psychoanalysts encounter identity? What of ‘the identity’ do they encounter?'

Lucio Russo shows that psychoanalysts encounter identity via the model proposed by Freud: by studying and becoming familiar with reality through pathology, and by interrogating their own unconscious, as is required as part of the analytic identity. Furthermore, I would say that the emergent aspect of identity puts identity into a relationship with everything which belongs to the unconscious.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On: The Comments of Emanuel Berman, Lewis Aron, Yoram Hazan and Steven Stern

Rachel Blass

Rachel Blass

14 Aberdare Gardens, London NW6 3PY, UK
E-mail: r.blass@ucl.ac.uk

Rachel Blass  

Dear Editors,

I agree with Emanuel Berman's claim that at present there exists much dialogue between analytic approaches within the literature; that no “stifling” of thought is taking place in this regard. However, this claim is irrelevant to my paper which is concerned with the absence of a dialogue of another kind, a dialogue over the essential nature of what constitutes psychoanalysis, what it is and what it is not. It this kind of dialogue that Berman does not want us to engage in, apparently preferring to regard psychoanalysis as inclusive of all possible definitions offered by those who consider themselves psychoanalysts. Berman acknowledges that there is a question of whether very “divergent schools could maintain their broader joint psychoanalytic identity and affiliation”, but he does not wish us to think about what defines this identity and it's precisely here that thought is indeed stifled.

Very strangely, Berman goes on to selectively ascribe to me some of the positions that I bring as examples in my paper, and on this basis he attacks what he presents as my own views on the definition of psychoanalysis and my not being “truthful” in professing to have concealed them. He speaks of my “empathic failure” in not recognizing that there is an issue of identity at stake (actually a central point of my paper) and he refers to my “hostility” to a range of theories. However, the paper does not in any way address my own views on how psychoanalysis should be defined. This is because, as I make very clear, the issue at hand is not my views, nor is it about hostility or affection for certain theories. It is rather about whether one may say of some theories or approaches that they are not psychoanalytical.

Berman's argument that there may be terrible consequences to defining psychoanalysis in a way that would exclude some approaches, as may be seen from the examples of the potential exclusion of Melanie Klein's approach in London and the exclusion of non-medical analysts in the US, is actually supported in my paper. I examine a series of problems with attempts to define psychoanalysis, and state that there is a “real danger that greater freedom to exclude would result in the exclusion of valuable views in the absence of adequate grounds for this” (Blass, 2010a, p. 97). But it is fallacious to suppose that, if exclusive definitions could have negative consequences, the attempt to define exclusively should be abandoned. On the basis of such logic no one would exercise because in some cases it causes heart attacks. Just as the choice is not between ‘not exercising’ and ‘having heart attacks’, so it is not between ‘not defining’ and ‘thoughtlessly excluding’. The possibility of thoughtfully defining does exist. In fact, it goes on to some extent all the time. While Berman would like to paint a doomsday scenario as the alternative to his limitless inclusive stance (e.g. “collective suicide”, expulsion “of members who do not sign a loyalty oath”), the fact of the matter is that some criteria of exclusion are always being applied, e.g.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Shuttle Analysis in the Han-Prakken Psychoanalytic Institute for Eastern Europe (PIEE)11

Christopher Bollas and Gilbert Diatkine

Gilbert Diatkine

gilbert.diatkine@wanadoo.fr

Gilbert Diatkine  

Since its creation in 2001 up to 2008, the PIEE has registered 143 training analysis projects. 53 of them were regular training analysis with local IPA members, authorized to analyse the candidates in their towns. 18 PIEE analysands have chosen exile in order to be analysed. Shuttle analysis, since its acceptance by the IPA as a method of training, is midway between these two solutions. It gives an analysand the opportunity to have training analysis abroad when there is no local training analyst, without enduring the miseries of emigration. 90 PIEE analysands have chosen shuttle analysis for their analytic training.

Registration

To be registered as a ‘PIEE analysand’, a person has first to fill in a registration form and, since 2008, to write a letter of motivation, explaining his or her reasons to wish to become a psychoanalyst, and how he intends to have a training analysis. The PIEE Board makes a first selection on the basis of this registration form and this motivation letter. If the applicant is successful in this first stage, he has two interviews with PIEE Training Committee members, usually during one of the Schools organized by the PIEE, about the applicant's analysability, the feasibility of the plan for the analysis, and the analysand's ability to face the specific stresses of shuttle analysis. Both interviewers report back to the PIEE Training Committee who takes the final decision of registration: refusal, delay or acceptance.

The geographical origin of shuttle analysands

The PIEE area includes all states formerly in the USSR and East European countries in which there is no IPA Study Group or Society (Bulgaria, Croatia and Slovenia).

42 shuttle analysands come from Russia, including 27 from Moscow, to which we have to add 14 applicants for regular analysis (non-shuttle) and seven who have had analysis in exile. The PIEE had registered 48 Moscow analysands before the Moscow group applied to become an IPA Study

[[RunningHead]]

The Soldiers Project: A Psychoanalytically-Informed free Clinic for Military Service Members and Their Families11

Kate Schechter

Kate Schechter

30 North Michigan Avenue, Suite 429, Chicago, IL 60602 USA - kschecht@uchicago.edu

Kate Schechter  

The panel ‘The Soldiers Project: A Psychoanalytically-informed Free Clinic for Military Service Members and their Families,’ chair: Kate Schechter (Chicago, USA) began with an introduction to the Project by its founder, Judith Broder, from Los Angeles, followed by a clinical case presentation by Broder's colleague Carol Tanenbaum. Pablo Cuevas Corona (Mexico) and Haim Knobler (Israel) then commented on Broder's introduction and Tanenbaum's case, allowing for the development of a comparative perspective on the contemporary psychoanalytic treatment of war trauma in various regions. Cuevas Corona and Knobler augmented their theoretical presentations with rich explorations of their own psychoanalytically informed treatment work with traumatized individuals in Mexico (with earthquake survivors and with soldiers) and Israel (with soldiers). This was a moving panel that had much of the feel of a working group since most of the members of the audience also treated survivors of severe trauma and contributed descriptions of such efforts in various countries.

The Soldiers Project, initiated in Los Angeles by Broder and colleagues and then copied in several other cities in the United States, provides free treatment to soldiers traumatized by their service in US military involvements in Iraq and Afghanistan. The soldiers who come to the Project are not for the most part in traditional psychoanalysis but in psychoanalytic psychotherapy at varying frequencies. A central emphasis of working with this group of individuals, all of the panelists agreed, was the need for the therapist to struggle with his or her own resistances to full exposure to profound traumatic suffering. As Tanenbaum described her own initiation into working with this population: ‘I had to deal with myself, because more and more my response to military violence was making me wish to turn away, to avoid seeing their trauma, safely splitting my orientation into a “me” and “them,” into “good” and “bad,” “righteous” and “evil.

responseSet.title: Winnicott's Foundation for the Basic Concepts of Freud's Metapsychology?
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Winnicott's Foundation for the Basic Concepts of Freud's Metapsychology?11

Martine Girard

Martine Girard

CHU Casselardit, TSA 40031, 31059 Toulouse, France — girard.m@chu-toulouse.fr

Martine Girard  

double dependence, foundation, metapsychology

In a recent paper, Fulgencio shows how Winnicott rejected the basic speculative concepts of Freud's metapsychology — Trieb, psychical apparatus and libido — and replaced them with non-speculative concepts that promoted a factual theorization. In this paper, the author examines some of Winnicott's concepts and attempts to demonstrate how, rather than replacing Freud's concepts, he provides a factual foundation for the metapsychology in the double dependence of the infant in care. Freud never actually disregards the necessity of early mothering but he takes it for granted. By differentiating between ego needs and id needs, ego-relatedness and id-relatedness, object-mother and environment-mother, Winnicott attempts to theorize what Freud takes for granted: the function of the holding environment as a framework for id-experiences and the function of object-presenting as a condition of reality-testing. Furthermore, by differentiating between pure male and pure female elements, he is also able to construct a highly speculative theorization in order to distinguish two basic principles: doing and being. Although the death drive is clearly rejected, this rejection follows from his theorization of double dependence. Consequently, the author suggests that Winnicott did not discard metapsychological concepts but theorized the conditions for using both these and the intrapsychic topography.

(Final version accepted 8 September 2009)

responseSet.title: Nainen Ja Viha [Woman and Hatred] by Elina Reenkola Minerva Publishing, Helsinki, 2008; 215 pp.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Nainen Ja Viha [Woman and Hatred]1 by Elina ReenkolaMinerva Publishing, Helsinki, 2008; 215 pp.

Maria Häkkinen

Maria Häkkinen

Veljestentie 10, 00730 Helsinki 73, Finland
E-mail: maria.hakkinen@pp.inet.fi

Maria Häkkinen  

Woman and Hatred, the third book of the psychoanalyst Elina Reenkola, continues her explorations of the female psyche. Naisen verhottu sisin (1997, translated into English as The Veiled Female Core in 2002) can be seen as an introductory work on the theme. Her second work, Intohimoinen nainen-psykoanalyyttisia tutkielmia halusta, rakkaudesta ja häpeästä [The Passionate Woman: Psychoanalytic Studies of Desire, Love and Shame] (2004) examines Freud's basic idea that the ego grows out of the body, and emphasizes woman as a subject who, starting from when she is a little girl, does psychic work to give meaning to her body.

Although the subtitle of the present book is Aggression as a Resource, Reenkola says in her Preface that she is trying to shed light on the dark sides of the female soul. I find this problematic, or at least challenging, since children are often on the receiving end of the darkest sides of female aggression, which easily leads to the wish to assign guilt. Sometimes mothers are blamed, sometimes psychoanalysts are accused of blaming mothers. In my view, the most important contribution of Reenkola's latest book is precisely its ability to open up the psychodynamics of these painful settings without being accusatory or assigning blame. This open-mindedness is crucial in helping us to understand the psychic distress and paralysis caused by unidentified and untreated female hatred. It is comforting to know that attempts are being made to survey the psychological well-being of pregnant women and small children and that help is already available for early attachment disorders. We need to be able to approach and understand the vicissitudes of unidentified hatred, especially when it is hidden behind idealized images of women.

Elina Reenkola writes in a reader-friendly way so readers do not necessarily need to be familiar with psychoanalytic concepts or be well versed in the book's themes in order to understand what they are reading.

responseSet.title: On ‘Affirming “That's not Psycho-Analysis!’”
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On ‘Affirming “That's not Psycho-Analysis!’”

Emanuel Berman

Emanuel Berman

Haifa 31905, Israel
E-mail: emanuel@psy.haifa.ac.il

Emanuel Berman  

Dear Editors,

Rachel Blass (2010) keeps emphasizing the value of truth. She calls for a “productive dialogue between opposing views” (p. 97), implying that the present climate of supposed ‘political correctness’ blocks such a dialogue, while her proposal for exclusive definitions will allow it. But the truth is the very opposite.

At present, within the IPA and on the pages of the International Journal of Psychoanalysis itself, such a dialogue indeed exists because analysts influenced by differing theoretical models co-exist within the same organizations and journals. If we accept Blass's wish for sharp definitions of what psychoanalysis is and what it is not, such a dialogue will come to an end because many points of view will be declared as ‘not truly psychoanalytic’ and therefore excluded and banished.

Blass's diagnosis of confusion “stifling thought” (p. 94) is unfounded. The fact that Kleinian psychoanalysis and Self Psychology (one of her prime examples; ibid.) differ considerably is well known and often thoroughly explored. The legitimacy of “overt expression of differences” (ibid.) is not at risk, as we all take it for granted. The question is whether such divergent schools could maintain their broader joint psychoanalytic identity and affiliation, as they do now, or should be forcefully broken apart, as Blass clearly wishes.

Blass says: “In this study I will not set forth my own views” (p. 84). Nevertheless, her views are quite explicit in this paper, and any informed reader will easily understand that she wishes to define psychoanalysis as “a practice based exclusively on the verbal interpretation of unconscious conflict” (p. 92) and therefore to exclude any “practice whose curative potential depends on the analyst fulfilling maternal needs which the patient consciously experiences to be absent” (ibid; the latter appears to be a gross caricature of Winnicott's views).

Her hostility to the views of Ferenczi, Balint, Winnicott, Kohut and Relational Psychoanalysis (with their condemned “emphasis on empathy rather than truth” [p. 82]) is quite transparent.

responseSet.title: The Analyst in Action: An Individual Account of What Jungians do and why They do it
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Analyst in Action: An Individual Account of What Jungians do and why They do it11

Warren Colman

Warren Colman

65 Gurney Court Road, St Albans, Herts AL1 4QU, UK — gcr65@ntlworld.com

Warren Colman  

boundaries, Jungian analysis, similarities and difference, symbols, the self

This paper describes some similarities and differences between contemporary approaches to analysis as practised by ‘Freudians’ and ‘Jungians’ in London today. It aims to contribute to mutual understanding between different schools of analysis by showing how the analyst's interventions can only be understood in terms of the theoretical context from which they arise (cf. ‘the analyst's preconscious’ as discussed by Hamilton [1996]). A discussion of five key themes of Jungian theory is followed by an account of clinical work with a patient who enacted her inner world through the use of material objects brought to the consulting room, presenting difficult technical dilemmas concerning boundaries and enactment. The paper aims to shows how these Jungian themes influenced the analyst's response, particularly in relation to ideas of symbolic transformation, the unknowable nature of unconscious processes and the purposive orientation of the self towards wholeness and integration.

(Final version accepted 18 August 2009)

responseSet.title: On: Responding to Rachel Blass' Article ‘Affirming “That's not Psycho-Analysis!” On the value of the Politically Incorrect act of Attempting to Define the Limits of Our Field’
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On: Responding to Rachel Blass' Article ‘Affirming “That's not Psycho-Analysis!” On the value of the Politically Incorrect act of Attempting to Define the Limits of Our Field’

Lewis Aron

Lewis Aron

78 Country Club Drive, Port Washington, NY 11050, USA E-mail: lew.aron@nyu.edu

Lewis Aron  

Dear Editors,

In her argument for a limiting definition of psychoanalysis, Rachel B. Blass (2010) creates a series of false dichotomies that threaten to polarize the field. Using the rhetoric of a call for ‘truth,’ she argues that we should define psychoanalysis based on its ‘essence,’ its ‘meaning,’ and its ‘nature.’ By contrast, she trivializes the alternative, which she dismisses under the rubric of ‘postmodernism,’ ‘relativism,’ or ‘social-constructivism,’ claiming that these approaches lead to a radical relativism in which we are left with nothing but opinions, one of which is as good as the next, and without rational arguments to mediate among them.

But this polarization is itself a misrepresentation and distortion of the ‘truth,’ since there are many versions of, and a variety of approaches to postmodernism and constructivism, and the version of ‘constructivism’ used by most analysts is not nearly as radical as Blass portrays. (Irwin Hoffman, among others, has articulated this position with great care throughout his work.) In critical-constructivism one expects that there may be more than one compelling way to see and interpret an event but that hardly means that anything goes. Some alternatives may be implausible. But even the plausible options are open to rational debate as to which is to be preferred, debate in which moral, aesthetic, and pragmatic considerations will have their place. Blass dismisses all of this subtle thinking, suggesting that social constructivism leaves us with nothing but personal opinions or preferences.

The question to my mind is not what is the truth, nature, or essence of psychoanalysis, but rather, what are the consequences of defining psychoanalysis one way or another and to whom? Under what social, economic, cultural, historical, political conditions does defining psychoanalysis in one way or another lead to benefits or disadvantages to patients, and/or to particular groups of professionals, including those who do indeed identify themselves as psychoanalysts of one persuasion or another? One could certainly have solid and rational arguments regarding these questions. Some answers would be better than others - for certain purposes. Context matters, and I assert that as a truth, one that can be debated on rational grounds.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Some Thoughts between Body and Mind in the Light of Wilma Bucci's Multiple Code Theory

Luigi Solano

Luigi Solano

Via Merulana 209, 00185 Roma, Italy— luigi.solano@uniroma1.it

Luigi Solano  

body-mind, Bucci, multiple code theory, nonverbal, psychosomatics, subsymbolic

(Final version accepted 1 July 2010)

The author proposes the usefulness of Wilma Bucci's Multiple Code Theory in clarifying some controversial issues in psychoanalytically inspired psychosomatics. Definition of a dialectic among different entities may appear difficult in an unitarian view of the organism, where body and mind are seen as having no kind of intrinsic existence, which may be differentiated from the organism as a whole, but as two categories having to do with the perspective of the observer. This aporia may find a solution in a redefinition of the body—mind relationship as that between symbolic systems and the subsymbolic system, both of which may be viewed as mind or as body depending on the point of observation. Similarly, somatic pathology, if we accept an unitary paradigm, need no longer be viewed as due to an influence of ‘mind’ on ‘body’: a definition of pathology as linked to a disconnection between different systems, as found in Bucci's theory, is proposed as a possible solution. Emergence of somatic symptoms, however, besides being witness to disconnection, may be seen as the subsymbolic first expression of an item of content, an attempt at reconnection, as already proposed, in a way, by Winnicott in 1949. This attempt has much better opportunities to succeed when it finds an adequate container, as in analysis. A clinical situation of this kind is presented.

responseSet.title: Notes on ‘Bemächtigungstrieb’ and Strachey's Translation as ‘Instinct for Mastery’
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Notes on ‘Bemächtigungstrieb’ and Strachey's Translation as ‘Instinct for Mastery’

Kristin White

Kristin White

Senegalstr. 10, 13351 Berlin, Germany — Kristin.White@t-online.de

Kristin White  

aggressive drive, Alfred Adler, Bemächtigungstrieb, game with the reel and string, instinct for mastery, power, translation

(Final version accepted 11 May 2010)

This short paper looks at Freud's use of the term ‘Bemächtigungstrieb’ and its translation by Strachey as ‘instinct for mastery’ when Freud was describing the motives behind his grandson's game with the wooden reel and string in Beyond the Pleasure Principle. The word ‘Macht’ [power], which is contained in the word ‘Bemächtigung’ points to Freud's difficult relationship with Alfred Adler, whose early theories on the aggressive drive and later theories on ‘striving for power’ were initially rejected by Freud. Looking at the changes in Freud's reception of Adlerian terms, some of which he later integrated into his own theory, throws light on his choice of the word ‘Bemächtigungstrieb’ in 1920, when he was just beginning to introduce his thoughts on the death instinct. A slightly different translation of the word ‘Bemächtigungstrieb’, one which takes these historical and theoretical aspects into account, could make these connections clearer for the English reader.

responseSet.title: Stumbling towards Termination in Clinical and Theoretical Psychoanalysis: A Commentary on ‘Stumbling towards Termination’
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Stumbling towards Termination in Clinical and Theoretical Psychoanalysis: A Commentary on ‘Stumbling towards Termination’11

Alessandro Garella

Alessandro Garella

Via Nicola A. Porpora 7/b, I-80128 Naples, Italy
E-mail: alegarel@unina.it

Alessandro Garella  

When Dominique Scarfone, the editor of ‘The Analyst at Work’ section, asked me to have another look at Mayer Subrin's paper and the commentaries of Ilany Kogan and Nilde Jacob Parada Franch, I reread Dr Scarfone's introductory editorial (Scarfone, 2008) as a guide to how I should proceed. My brief is twofold: on the one hand, I am to listen with a ‘clinical ear’ to what my colleagues have written, and, on the other, I am called upon to abstract a meta-commentary. Faced with problems of language and of the level of discourse (clinical, theoretical, and differences in clinical vertex), I realized that my task would comprise a kind of transcription, rather than translation, in abstract and general terms — because theoretical and thematic (subjects, hypotheses, and theories concerning the termination) — of descriptions, representations, and personal and therefore limited and particular appraisals. I would be required not only to present a clear-cut, personal, theoretical and clinical reading, but to transform the ‘fact’ of the texts sent to me — that is, other people's thoughts — into the representation of a discourse, namely a discourse of general applicability on the termination of analysis. My attention was therefore focused not on Dr Subrin's case history itself but on the relationship between the case history and the commentaries, with a view to demonstrating to the best of my ability its characteristic dialogic and dialectical quality. Underlying my attempt is the idea advanced by Winnicott (1965) that psychoanalysis, as research, is a collective enterprise, and that it is such not because of the (extremely partial) repeatability and confirmability of the relevant phenomena and procedures, but on account of the impossibility of defining a clear limit to its field. Because the psychic field produces itself, whereas one seeks to reproduce it in descriptions and theories, a very great number of observation points located apart in different spaces and times are required for its apprehension, and this is impossible for any individual observer to have.

responseSet.title: The Deconstruction of Primary Narcissism
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Deconstruction of Primary Narcissism

René Roussillon

René Roussillon

4 Rue Barrème, 69006 Lyon, France — rene.roussillon@wanadoo.fr

René Roussillon  

creativeness, destructiveness, found-created object, illusion, messengerdrive, narcissism, negative illusion, primary narcissistic illusion, objectification, reality-testing, subjectification, the double

(Final version accepted 1 September 2009)

The author examines Winnicott's contribution to Freud's concept of primary narcissism. In Mourning and melancholia, Freud laid the foundations for this contribution, but it was Winnicott who turned it into a clinically useful concept. There are three of Winnicott's ideas that can be seen as preliminary stages to his theory of transitional phenomena and illusion. They serve as an introduction to thinking about the analysis of the analysand's primary narcissism and the theoretical prerequisites that make the interpretation of primary narcissism possible. Through the exploration of three main points in Winnicott's writings the author shows how Winnicott's conceptualizations are both new and a continuation of Freud's thinking. His ideas are thus part of the overall theoretical pattern of Freud's metapsychology. The three main points are as follows:

In bringing maternal care and the presence of the psychic environment into the construction of primary narcissism, Winnicott made it possible to analyse narcissism. His ideas enable us to stand back from the characteristic solipsism of narcissism, which holds that everything comes from the self and only from the self. The latter concept tends to eliminate the role of the object and environment in the construction of the self. At the same time, by deconstructing the way in which the self is infiltrated by a certain number of narcissistic postulates, Winnicott made it possible to interpret the theory of narcissism itself.

Between the individual and the sense of self, Winnicott inserted the maternal object and her function as a mirror of affects who acts as a medium for the organization of self-identity. Primary identity is established through the construction and elimination of a narcissistic identification that becomes meaningful in the context of a primary homosexual relationship functioning as a ‘double’.

A process of differentiation that governs the discovery of the object is in a dialectical relationship with narcissistic identification. That process can be understood only in terms of the responses made by the primary psychic environment to the baby's primary aggression.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Body, Adolescence, and Psychosis11

Riccardo Lombardi and Marisa Pola

Marisa Pola

Via Dei Fienaroli 36, 00153 Rome, Italy — dr.riccardolombardi@libero.it

Marisa Pola  

adolescence, Bion, bodily countertransference, Ferrari, Matte Blanco, mind—body relationship, psychosis, symmetrical transference, transference on to the body

(Final version accepted 8 July 2010)

This article explores aspects of the clinical development of a male 17 year-old patient who had four weekly sessions of psychoanalysis during an acute psychotic crisis. In the context of arrested adolescent development, a psychotic crisis can present an opportunity to set the process of maturation in motion again. Adolescent psychosis is examined in the light of Bion's theories of catastrophic change — in which the container/contained relationship becomes explosive and overwhelming — as well as of Ferrari's and Matte Blanco's hypotheses on the mind—body relationship. The authors emphasize the role of denial of the body and its changes in the genesis of adolescent psychotic conflict, and show how the analytic relationship can offer crucial reverie conditions for promoting recognition of the body, bodily sensations and affects, as a prerequisite for the activation of an autonomous mental system. This clinical approach entails recognizing the urgent need to make room for the elaboration of intrasubjective relationships and for mind—body dialogue, transference interpretations being postponed. Clinical material and fragments of analytic dialogue illustrate the authors' hypotheses and demonstrate the patient's development towards recognition of his body and an incipient capacity to think associated with the perception of the limits set by time and reality.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Conceptual Framework from the Paris Psychosomatic School: A Clinical Psychoanalytic Approach to Oncology11

Marilia Aisenstein and Claude Smadja

Claude Smadja

72 rue d'Assas, 75006 Paris, France — mariliaais@hotmail.com

107 avenue du Général-Bizot, 75012 Paris, France

Claude Smadja  

breast cancer, death-drive, drive, ego-ideal, essential depression, mechanical functioning, mentalization, oncology, progressive disorganization, psychosomatics, regression

(Final version accepted 23 January 2010)

This article presents further clinical material from the Paris Psychosomatic School (Aisenstein, 2006). The Freudian foundations of psychosomatics are detailed and post-Freudian developments focusing on the contribution of the Paris Psychosomatic School arc outlined, in particular, the somatizing process as a result of regression and the somatizing process as a result of drive unbinding. The authors argue that the latter possibly gives rise to progressive and serious illness leading to death. The relationship of classical psychoanalysis to psychotherapeutic treatment from the angle of the Paris school is commented on. The authors then turn to two clinical presentations of women suffering from breast cancer. The method of evaluating the patients' capacities for undergoing psychotherapeutic treatment and their mental capacity for healing is discussed. The face-to-face psychoanalytic treatment undertaken with the second case is discussed. Finally, the authors recall Freud's insistence after 1920 on the opposition of the life drives and the death drives, which placed self-destruction at the centre of psychic functioning. They conclude that current research in biology and medicine, notably research concerning programmed cell death, will converge with psychoanalytic psychosomatics to illuminate somatizing processes and demonstrate the relevance of psychoanalytic treatment to patients who are capable of mental reorganization in the course of their illness and medical treatment.
