responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Coincidences in Analysis: Sigmund Freud and the Strange Case of Dr Forsyth and Herr von Vorsicht11

Maria Pierri

Maria Pierri

Via Mondonego 35, 35038 Torreglia (PD), Italy — maria.pierri@spiweb.it

Maria Pierri  

Anna Freud analysis, coincidences, death instincts, Forsyth-case, incest, occult, setting, story of psychoanalysis, telepathy, thought-transference

(Final version accepted 30 April 2009)

Freud's interest in thought transference opens the possibility for psychoanalytic research on the primary preverbal language and the maternal function, which the emphasis on verbal and paternal communication had hidden in the background of the setting. The author advances a new interpretation of coincidences in analysis and of the psychopathology of everyday life of the setting. Starting from a strange coincidence, new hypotheses are submitted following additional readings of the unpublished manuscript of the ‘Forsyth case’, recovered by the author, in regard to a significant moment of transformation, both in Freud and in psychoanalysis, at the end of the war. This phase corresponds first to a change of language, from German to English, as well as to the foundation of the International Journal of Psychoanalysis by Ernest Jones. In particular, the roots of the metapsychological turn of the 1920s are explored, together with the opening of private and productive thoughts in the area of ‘telepathy’ that joined Freud, Ferenczi, and Anna Freud in a true ‘dialogue of unconsciouses’. The free association between A Child Is Being Beaten, Beyond the Pleasure Principle, and the clinical experience with ‘Herr B.’ is outlined in order to understand Freud's heroic self-analysis at the time when he was treating his daughter Anna and grieving the death of his beloved Sophie.

responseSet.title: Contemporary art and Hanna Segal's thinking on Aesthetics
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Contemporary art and Hanna Segal's thinking on Aesthetics

Adela Abella

Adela Abella

217 route d’Annecy, 1257 Croix-de-Rozon, Switzerland — adela.abella@tele2.ch

Adela Abella  

applied analysis, art, history of psychoanalysis, reparation, sublimation, thinking, truth

From the point of view of the history of ideas in psychoanalysis, a major shift may be described from Freud's starting point considering art as sublimation of sexual desires on a largely objectal level to later developments emphasizing the presence in art of destructiveness and narcissistic conflicts. Segal's contribution represents a watershed in this evolution. Following Klein, Hanna Segal suggests considering art in relation to depressive anxieties and reparation in such a way that artistic activity may be seen as an attempt ‘to restore and re-create the loved object outside and inside the ego’ which implies a successful work of mourning accompanied by symbol formation. For Segal, these reparative processes are conveyed through formal beauty which represents the victory of reparation over destruction. Nevertheless, contemporary art demands that we consider the intervention, in art, of more raw and less symbolized/sublimated processes, including acting-out in often primitive, psychotic or perverse ways. Thus this paper unfolds in two directions: on the one hand, it examines the differences and continuities between Freud's and Segal's thinking whilst, on the other, the author presents some alternative ideas which stress the search for truth and new thinking in contemporary art.

(Final version accepted 22 May 2009)

responseSet.title: Reflection in Psychoanalysis: On Symbols and Metaphors
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Reflection in Psychoanalysis: On Symbols and Metaphors

Henrik Enckell

Henrik Enckell

Estnäsgatan 7 E 9, 00170 Helsinki, Finland - henrik.enckell@kolumbus.fi

Henrik Enckell  

archaeology, metaphor, reflection, symbol, symbolization, teleology

(Final version accepted 12 February 2010)

Psychoanalysis is an art of reflection, i.e. it tries to facilitate the subject's retrieval of his own self. The ‘material’ to be reflected upon consists of the products of human symbolization. But there are two views of reflection. In one, the self is searched in a temporal, structural and procedural ‘anterior’ (the model of archaeology). In the other the self is to be found in a still evolving meaning process, i.e. it resides in a ‘future’ (the model of teleology). Both these pictures are common in psychoanalysis. The aim of this paper is to study the figures of symbolization through the archaeology/teleology reflection model. The author tries to show that ‘symbol’ leans on archaeology while ‘metaphor’ comprises a teleological conception. In order to show the relevance of this finding, the author draws the outlines of both an archaeological and a teleological model in psychoanalysis. It is stated that the former builds on an inherent symbol model while the figure for the latter is metaphor.

responseSet.title: On: The Comments of Dr. Graham Clarke and Dr. Paul Finnegan
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On: The Comments of Dr. Graham Clarke and Dr. Paul Finnegan

Thomas Ogden MD

Thomas Ogden

San Francisco, CA 94118, USA
E-mail: thomas.ogdenmd@gmail.com

Thomas Ogden  

Dear Editors,

I am grateful to Dr. Clarke and Dr. Finnegan for drawing attention to the very telling exchange between Fairbairn and Isaacs during the Controversial Discussions. I find the quotation from Fairbairn to be so concise and pithy a statement of the differences between his ‘object relations theory’ and Klein's that I would like to take a moment to flesh it out in my own words.

Fairbairn begins by saying that: “The concept of ‘phantasy’ has now been rendered obsolete by the concepts of ‘psychical reality’ and ‘internal objects’” (King and Steiner, 1991, p. 359). I understand Fairbairn's statement to reflect his conception that what the infant meets in the ‘unsatisfactory’ mother is a real experience of emotional deprivation and not a phantasy of emotional deprivation. Even the best of mothers will sometimes misread her infant, at other times withdraw emotionally, and at still other times exhaust her capacity to give her infant all that he or she needs. And, of course, there are mother-infant relationships that are pathological to varying degrees. The experience of emotional deprivation leaves the infant feeling that he is not loved and that his love is not valued (Fairbairn, 1944). For Fairbairn, the infant's response to the unsatisfactory experience with the mother is not to engage in the phantasy of splitting off the good mother from the bad one, as Isaacs would have it. Rather, the infant responds by, in fact, splitting off suborganizations of the ego from the main body of the ego. In this way, the infant moves the experience of the unsatisfactory relationship with the mother from the world of external object relationships to the world of internal object relationships. By contrast with the phantasied ‘internal objects’ of Isaacs and Klein, Fairbairn's ‘internal objects’ have an “organized structure, an identity of their own, an endopsychic existence and an activity as real within the inner world as those of any objects in the external world …” (King and Steiner, 1991, p. 359).

responseSet.title: The Life Instinct
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Life Instinct

Nicola Abel-Hirsch

Nicola Abel-Hirsch

Unit 13 Apollo Studios, Charlton Kings Road, London NW5 2SB, UK - abelhirsch@waitrose.com

Nicola Abel-Hirsch  

binding, container/contained, death instinct, Eros, inhibitions, jouissance, life instinct, linking, negation, Plato's Symposium, sexual instinct

(Final version accepted 3 February 2010)

In psychoanalytic writing an oversimplified interpretation of Freud's concept of the life and death instincts sometimes colours the presentation. Roughly, there is an implication that the life instinct is ‘good’ and the death instinct ‘bad’. Freud however is clear that: “Neither of these instincts is any less essential than the other; the phenomena of life arise from the concurrent or mutually opposing action of both” (1933b, p. 209). In this paper I look in detail at the characteristics of the life instinct as conceptualized by Freud, and draw on Bion's work ‘on linking’ to elaborate Freud's view that binding is the life instinct's key characteristic. I suggest that there are pathological forms of both the life and death instinct if defused (separated off) from the other, and I explore a pathological variation of the life instinct in which binding is without the negation, rest, limit or end provided by the ‘opposing action’ of the death instinct. I consider an instance of the kind that any analyst might meet clinically, in which an inhibited patient experiences severe anxiety that life-giving connections threaten to proliferate indiscriminately and to an overwhelming intensity and size.

responseSet.title: Letter from Paris
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Letter from Paris

Marilia Aisenstein

Marilia Aisenstein

72 rue d'Assas, Paris, F-75006, France
E-mail: marilia.aisenstein@gmail.com

Marilia Aisenstein  

It is fair to say, I think, that French psychoanalysis today is rich and lively. To give a clear picture of the current psychoanalytic landscape, it is necessary to go back to the 1950s. After the World War II, in the enthusiasm of the post-war years, psychoanalysis nourished in Paris. At the time it was strongly influenced by ‘ego-psychology’, imported from Vienna by Lowenstein. At the Paris Psychoanalytic Society (SPP), founded in 1926 by Freud and Princess Marie Bonaparte, there were some strong personalities such as Sacha Nacht, Daniel Lagache and … a young analyst by the name of Jacques Lacan.

Gifted and charismatic, Lacan's appeal resided in his talent and culture: he wrote, taught and had many students and analysands. Sacha Nacht, the Society's president, was a charismatic clinician but a poor theoretician, and he and Lacan clashed violently: both had been analysands of Lowenstein who had just emigrated to the United States, along with Hartmann and Spitz.

The famous split occurred in 1954, and Lacan was obliged to leave the Society on account of his technical innovations which concerned the setting. The specific issue at stake was the ‘variable length session’, with Lacan defending the interesting but unacceptable idea that a session could be shortened and ended when important material emerged. The interruption would then acquire the value of an interpretation.

Excluded from the SPP, Lacan founded his own school, the École Freudienne de Paris (EFP) which he tried in vain to have recognized by the IPA. In 1963, a group of colleagues left him, receiving immediate recognition from the IPA as a component society, and became the French Psychoanalytic Association (APF).

Until its dissolution in 1980 when the Lacanian movement broke up into multiple groups, the EPF had been subject to numerous splits. I will just mention here the one that gave birth in 1967 to the association known as the ‘Quatrième Groupe’ or Fourth Group, which still defines itself today as non-Lacanian and non-IPA.

responseSet.title: Some Thoughts on the Concept of the Internal Parental Couple
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Some Thoughts on the Concept of the Internal Parental Couple

Serge Frisch and Christine Frisch-Desmarez

Christine Frisch-Desmarez

36 rue Tony Neuman, L-2241 Luxembourg — sfrisch@pt.lu

Christine Frisch-Desmarez  

Some patients are incapable of engaging in a relationship with a partner on a long-term basis. To explain these relationship issues, they invoke reasons that have to do with external reality rather than with their own internal reality. The authors present several clinical examples that have given rise to an initial attempt at theorizing the concept of the internal couple: some patients fail to internalize a positive representation in which they can see themselves as part of a couple. Psychoanalysts tend to think of the father and mother (or their internal representatives) as separate entities, differentiated and complementary, and not in terms of a single object that brings them together as an internal couple. In the author's view, an internal parental couple is also internalized by the infant under the guise of various internal objects. The internal parental couple thus appears as a complex construction that goes beyond the mere addition of father plus mother. Underneath their neurotic veneer, these patients have severely damaged representations of internal parents and of the internal parental couple. Unable to identify with a stable and procreative internal parental couple, they tend also to attack the psychoanalytic couple. Early distortions in the oedipal sphere may be one cause of the stumbling-block they encounter on the way to introjecting a well-attuned couple-as-object and the idea of parenting.

(Final version accepted 23 September 2009)

responseSet.title: Is Translation Possible?
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Is Translation Possible?

Dana Birksted-Breen

Dana Birksted-Breen

57 Wood Lane, Highgate, London N6 5UD, London — dana.birksted-breen@dib.me.uk

Dana Birksted-Breen  

Translation is always interpretation and the psychoanalyst is a translator/ interpreter. The psychoanalyst translates the unconscious. Words, images, affects, bodily signs are translated into interpretations. Mahony (1980) describes Freud as “one of the great thinkers and innovators in the domain of translation. […] for he gives the concept a scope, extension, and depth in his work that appeared nowhere previously in history” (p. 462).

Freud's ‘talking cure’ translates blocked emotions, hysterical symptoms, and impulses into words. But just as there is no one-to-one equivalence of words and sentences between national languages, so too there is no one-to-one equivalence between the productions of the patient and the possible translation by the psychoanalyst. In the gap between the two resides the whole field of psychoanalysis in its many cultural, theoretical and personal variations. It is inherent to the principle of overdetermination that various ‘translations’ can only each give a partial perspective. “If the psychical mechanism consists of stratification of transcriptions, then one can also assume the coexistence of multiple transcriptions or translations” (Hinz, 2008 p. 122).

Psychoanalysis is essentially polyglot, with its ‘social’ variations but also the variations of each dyad who come to learn each other's language through back and forth translations and often the construction of a common language, which would need translating to an outsider. The transformation/translation/ interpretation will be filtered through the particular psyche, theory, personal preference of the individual psychoanalyst. Ferro (2009), for example, following Bion's conceptualization of ‘transformation’ describes the many ‘languages’ which can be used to describe an aspect of the ‘hologram’.

Translation is sometimes near to construction when the original trace seems little available as in the non-neurotic structures where the work of ‘figurability’ is done between patient and analyst (Botella and Botella, 2005) via the reverie of the analyst (Ogden, 2004). Laplanche theorizes an earlier time when the infant has to translate and symbolize the ‘enigmatic messages’ received from the mother and this ‘seduction’ is one which also comes from the psychoanalyst (Laplanche, 1987, 1997).

responseSet.title: Benign and Disruptive Disturbances of the Supervisory Field
responseSet.authorMast: Germano Vollmer Filho and  Antonio Carlos J. Pires
responseSet.authorCitation: Filho, G. V. & Pires, A. C.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Benign and Disruptive Disturbances of the Supervisory Field11

Germano Filho and Antonio Pires

Antonio Pires

Rua Itaqui, 89/202, 90460-140 Porto Alegre — RS, Brazil — vollmer.voy@terra.com.br

Av. Taquara, 110/404, 90460-210 Porto Alegre — RS, Brazil — acjpires@via-rs.net

Antonio Pires  

benign disturbances of the supervisory field, disruptive disturbances of the supervisory field, supervision, supervisory field

(Final version accepted 1 December 2009)

Inspired by Baranger, Baranger and Mom's notion of psychoanalytic bastion, we describe an unconscious collusion between analytic supervisors and supervisees. This collusion's purpose is to avoid the anxiety inherent in the psychoanalytic field and that enters unprocessed into the supervisory field. The supervisor/supervisee dyad's emotional experience can be fleeting, with moderate intensity, but can slow down the process. This can happen sometimes in any supervisory situation. Or it can become permanent and with greater intensity and bring the entire process to a standstill and to a disruption of the supervisory field'. We call the first situation a ‘benign disturbance of the supervisory field’, the second a ‘disruptive disturbance of the supervisory field. We illustrate both disturbances using supervisory material. Our purpose is to show how to detect these disturbances, understand them, and manage them.

responseSet.title: Some Observations on the Process of Mourning
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Some Observations on the Process of Mourning

Otto Kernberg

Otto Kernberg

21 Bloomingdale Road, White Plains, New York, NY 10605, USA — okernber@med.cornell.edu

Otto Kernberg  

depressive position, identification, love relations, mourning, object relations, spirituality, superego modification

(Final version accepted 13 October 2009)

The main proposal of this paper is that normal mourning is not completed after six months to a year or two as suggested in earlier literature, hut may bring about a permanent alteration of psychological structures that affect various aspects of the mourning persons' lives. These structural consequences of mourning consist in the setting up of a persistent internalized object relationship with the lost object that affects ego and superego functions. The persistent internalized object relationship develops in parallel to the identification with the lost object, and the superego modification includes the internalization of the value systems and life project of the lost object. A new dimension of spiritual orientation, the search for transcendental value systems, is one consequence of this superego modification.

responseSet.title: Levels of Analytic Work and Levels of Pathology: The Work of Calibration
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Levels of Analytic Work and Levels of Pathology: The Work of Calibration

Anne Alvarez

Anne Alvarez

45 Flask Walk, London NW3 1HH, UK — annealvrz@aol.com

Anne Alvarez  

borderline and autistic states, internal object deficit, introjective capacity, levels of meaning, priorities in technique

(Final version accepted 8 December 2009)

This paper identifies three points on a continuum of levels of analytic work and levels of meaning. The thinking arose out of many years of work with autistic and borderline children, who were not able, for reasons of profound ego impairment, to respond to the more traditional explanatory interpretation as a method of ascribing meaning. Their capacity for introjection was limited. The author suggests that a prior level — lending meaning via description or amplification — is more effective in helping them to think. The paper argues that this second method, where appropriate to the patient's developmental and psychopathological level, need not be seen as inferior to or less complete than the former type. A third, more intensified level of work — an urgent insistence on meaning — is illustrated with a patient where the deficit was not only in the ego, but in the self and the internal object. The suggestion is that the issue at this third level — with emptied out patients -concerns, not thinking about feeling, nor even identifying feeling, but gaining access to feeling itself. The paper involves an attempt both to elaborate certain of the author's earlier ideas on technique and to consolidate these into a wider schema of priorities.

responseSet.title: The Dual Aspect of Fantasy: Flight from Reality or Imaginative Realm? Considerations and Hypotheses from Clinical Psychoanalysis
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Dual Aspect of Fantasy: Flight from Reality or Imaginative Realm? Considerations and Hypotheses from Clinical Psychoanalysis11

Laura Colombi

Laura Colombi

Corso Porta Nuova 24, 201221, Milano, Italy - lauracolombi@libero.it

Laura Colombi  

withdrawal in fantasy, imaginative fantasy, earliest defences, relationship with reality, omnipotence-pleasure, emotional awareness

(Final version accepted 10 February 2010)

This paper discusses the view that fantasizing, understood as a flight into fantasy, belongs to a type of mental functioning distinct from imaginative fantasy. From this the idea emerges, proposed by Winnicott, that withdrawal into fantasy assumes a dissociative quality, which is formed early on as a defensive solution following the loss of hope in object relations. Such a defence becomes the foundation for a dangerous enclave in which the individual ends up enclosing himself, experiencing an illusory self-sufficiency. In this perspective, the author maintains that the flight into fantasy must be understood as a risk factor for the draining of the self or for a crystallization into psychopathological structures, becoming an automatic activity of ‘non-thought’ that substitutes for the working-through processes necessary for the development of the mind. The paper investigates this psychopathological dynamic, which was already present in Breuer and Freud's writings, examining subsequent contributions of various authors. Clinical material (of both children and adults) illustrates how the flight into fantasy may take the form of an anti-relational realm of the mind, compromising the operations necessary to the integration of psychic life. There is also a discussion of which therapeutic tools may help the patient to gradually abandon the withdrawal in favour of an authentically nourishing relational nature.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Introduction to the Paper by Pierre Marty1: The Narcissistic Difficulties Presented to the Observer by the Psychosomatic Problem1

Marilia Aisenstein and Claude Smadja

Claude Smadja

72 rue d'Assas, Paris, 75006, France — marliaais@hotmail.com

107 avenue du Général Bizot, Paris, 75012, France

Claude Smadja  

This article by Pierre Marty was first published in French in 1952 in La Revue Française de Psychanalyse. In order to more fully understand his contribution, we must situate this article in the course of Marty's life and work. During the 1950s he had just begun to elaborate his theory of psychoanalytic psychosomatics. His research at that time was based primarily upon his observations of patients suffering from movement disorders, back pain, nerve root syndromes, headaches and allergies.

In the same year as the meeting of the International Psychiatric Congress in Paris Marty met Franz Alexander and the two exchanged views on the subject of gastric ulcer disease. A year later Marty presented the case of ‘Marie’ — a patient suffering from headaches who had been cured by psychoanalysis — to the Parisian Psychoanalytic Society; this is the case to which he refers in the present paper. At this conference he made reference to ‘a painful inhibition of thought’ that, as for back-pain syndromes, he thought of as related to a failure of the dreamwork and therefore of the process of representation itself. Thus it was not a question of looking for the content to give sense to the somatic symptoms but rather of observing the inhibitions or failures of psychic elaboration that precede or accompany them.

Here we offer an illustrative simple schema based on the painful contractures of back pain: the blocking of the representation of a violent or raw movement too close to the drive — in the midst of a dream or a wakeful experience — could provoke the discharge of the same movement in the muscular system and preserve it as an abnormal symptom.

At that time Marty had at his disposal certain methodological tools:

his vast experience observing somatically ill patients in the general hospital and in the specialized services of neurology and gastroenterology

his knowledge of Freudian metapsychology

responseSet.title: Two Modalities of Manic Defences: Their Function in Adolescent Breakdown
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Two Modalities of Manic Defences: Their Function in Adolescent Breakdown

Catalina Bronstein

Catalina Bronstein

Brent Adolescent Centre, 51 Winchester Avenue, London NW6 7TT, UK — c.bronstein@btinternet.com

Catalina Bronstein  

acting-out, adolescence, adolescent breakdown, body, loss, mania, manic defences, paranoid anxieties, depressive anxieties, self-harm, superego

(Final version accepted 13 October 2009)

The aim of this paper is to explore two different modalities of manic defences and their specific underlying anxieties. I will describe the relation between these defences and the role of the superego and their specific function in adolescent breakdown. While one type of manic defence operates by the ego's identification with a sadistic superego the other one operates via evacuation of a guilt-inducing superego. I will illustrate the proposed ideas with clinical examples from the analysis of two adolescents. This paper stresses the specific differences between these two modalities and the clinical importance of both identifying and addressing the enactment in the transference of the unconscious phantasies and anxieties (paranoid and depressive) that give rise to these two types of defences.

responseSet.title: The Evolved Function of the Oedipal Conflict
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Evolved Function of the Oedipal Conflict

Lawrence Josephs

Lawrence Josephs

Garden City, Long Island, New York, NY 11530, USA — Josephs@adelphi.edu

Lawrence Josephs  

adaptation, evolutionary psychology, infidelity, oedipal conflict, sexual betrayal, sexual selection

(Final version accepted 7 November 2009)

Freud based his oedipal theory on three clinical observations of adult romantic relationships: (1) Adults tend to split love and lust; (2) There tend to be sex differences in the ways that men and women split love and lust; (3) Adult romantic relationships are unconsciously structured by the dynamics of love triangles in which dramas of seduction and betrayal unfold. Freud believed that these aspects of adult romantic relationships were derivative expressions of a childhood oedipal conflict that has been repressed. Recent research conducted by evolutionary psychologists supports many of Freud's original observations and suggests that Freuds oedipal conflict may have evolved as a sexually selected adaptation for reproductive advantage. The evolution of bi-parental care based on sexually exclusive romantic bonds made humans vulnerable to the costs of sexual infidelity, a situation of danger that seriously threatens monogamous bonds. A childhood oedipal conflict enables humans to better adapt to this longstanding evolutionary problem by providing the child with an opportunity to develop working models of love triangles. On the one hand, the oedipal conflict facilitates monogamous resolutions by creating intense anxiety about the dangers of sexual infidelity and mate poaching. On the other hand, the oedipal conflict in humans may facilitate successful cheating and mate poaching by cultivating a talent for hiding our true sexual intentions from others and even from ourselves. The oedipal conflict in humans may be disguised by evolutionary design in order to facilitate tactical deception in adult romantic relationships.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Working through and its Various Models11

René Roussillon

René Roussillon

4 Rue Barrème, 69006 Lyon, France — rene.roussillon@wanadoo.fr

René Roussillon  

drive-related impulses, interpretation, reconstruction, representation, resistance, working-through

(Final version accepted 20 April 2010)

Working through is an integral part of the psychoanalytic process, one could even say its epitome. It is therefore always present in the work of an analysis but, depending on the various phases and constraints that arise in that process, changes are brought to its form, to the issues involved in it and to its economic dimension. The author explores three forms or models of how working-through functions in relation to the dominant feature of any given analytical process. In the first of these, the issue that has to be worked over involves insight into a repressed representational complex; in the second, work has to be done on bringing into consciousness drive-related impulses or mental experiences that have until then not been able to be represented, so that the analysis itself is the first occasion on which retroactive [après-coup] processing can be initiated; and in the third, when representation and some kind of symbolization of the subjective experience and the drive-related issues that are part of it have been accomplished, the analysand then has to appropriate these subjectively and integrate them.

[[RunningHead]]

Affirming ‘That's not Psycho-Analysis!’ On the value of the Politically Incorrect Act of Attempting to Define the Limits of our Field

Rachel Blass

Rachel Blass

14 Aberdare Gardens, London NW6 3PY, UK — r.blass@ucl.ac.uk

Rachel Blass  

definition of psychoanalysis, pluralism, psychoanalytic identity

This paper is concerned with the value of the act of defining the field of psychoanalysis. It examines the reasons why adopting and especially giving voice to a definition that excludes approaches considered by some analysts to be analytic is commonly regarded as unacceptable within psychoanalytic discourse. It then explains the value and advantages of putting forth exclusive definitions. The author argues that clarifying the pros and cons of such acts of definition contributes to the understanding of the nature of psychoanalysis and the possibility of dialogue between opposing understandings of it. It may also contribute to greater freedom of thought and expression which is essential to the development of psychoanalytic theory and practice.

(Final version accepted 12 June 2009)

responseSet.title: Do Cyborgs Dream? Post-Human Landscapes in Shinya Tsukamoto's Nightmare Detective (2006)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Do Cyborgs Dream? Post-Human Landscapes in Shinya Tsukamoto's Nightmare Detective (2006)11

Giuseppe Civitarese

Giuseppe Civitarese

gcivitarese@gmail.com

Giuseppe Civitarese  

“My interest in nightmares comes from my childhood. The most horrible thing for me, then, was falling asleep, because suddenly I would descend into dreams which frightened me”. As Shinya Tsukamoto himself explains in an interview, the inspiration for Nightmare Detective [Akumu Tantei, 2006] came from the ghosts which have haunted him since his childhood. It is not surprising that he has become one of the most gifted directors when it comes to depicting the world of dreams, and that he has chosen Cronenberg and Lynch as his mentors. Even though he is not extremely well known, Tsukamoto is one of the most talented auteurs of contemporary Japanese cinema and he has already won numerous prizes for his work, among these, the Special Jury Prize for Snake of June (2002) at the Venice Film Festival.

The key theme that emerges from the director's poetic world is human alienation which arises when trauma leaves its mark on the developing human subject. In order to explore this sadness, which we can also observe every day in our consulting rooms, Tsukamoto makes use of the fictional world of cyberpunk. This genre, inaugurated by Blade Runner (1982), imagines a future age dominated by computers in which humans have been enslaved by machines. This is the new society of cyborgs in which, just as science (fiction) promises, minds have become transferable as though they were files. New technologies live parasitically on the body and shape it in their own image. Devices that are ever more invasive, or, in fact, ‘engulfing’ of the body, deprive the individual and society of their humanity. The boundaries between human and non-human have shifted.

By developing the central metaphor of the man-machine to address the topic of dehumanization, Nightmare Detective is also a meditation on modern civilization and its discontents. By portraying the culture of the so-called post/transhuman in a harsh light, Tsukamoto shows us how subjectivity is being redefined. In essence, he is returning to the poetic world of his first films, a world which is both brilliant and bewildering. However, in Nightmare Detective the style he uses is a lot less expressionistic and he succeeds in making us see that the technosphere has already filtered insidiously into our daily lives — this is the surprise that Tsukamoto has in store for us.

responseSet.title: Some thoughts on Mayer Subrin's ‘Stumbling towards Termination’
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Some thoughts on Mayer Subrin's ‘Stumbling towards Termination’

Nilde Franch

Nilde Franch

Rua Tabapuã 1590, apt 12, 04533-005 São Paulo, SP Brazil
E-mail: franch@attglobal.net, nilde@franch.com.br

Nilde Franch  

The end of analysis is always an instigating topic, and the clinical material presented by Dr. Mayer Subrin gives us an excellent opportunity to exchange ideas and experiences.

I see this exercise proposed by the IJPA in its section entitled ‘The Analyst at Work’, as an invitation for different analysts to offer their own viewpoints on the material presented, “providing a space where differences and commonalities are brought to the fore for the benefit of learning from another analyst's experience” (Scarfone, 2008, p. 5).

My reading of the material presented by Dr. Subrin does not always coincide with his own, and this may be a factor for growth for both of us as well as an expansion of the field of observation of psychic phenomena.

Dr. Subrin was certainly very discerning in formulating the following questions: ‘When does it occur to the analyst that termination is possible, and when to the patient? What has changed, what has not? What has yet to be done? What will be left undone or for another time?’ I will come back to these questions shortly.

Dr. Subrin introduces us to his patient by telling us she was in analysis with him for 13 years and that she had been through a previous analysis at a time when she was very depressed. She had even thought of committing suicide by stabbing herself in the abdomen with a kitchen-knife after undergoing two abortions, for lack of financial support from her husband and her extended family.

Subrin did not bring up these particular details by chance. He is telling us about two important aspects of the patient's personality. Firstly, that she feels she has been a victim and has been traumatized for lack of understanding, containment and support in her unfortunate circumstances. Secondly, she also sees herself as the victimizer, a person who has caused violence and death. These two aspects appear condensed into the fantasy of killing herself so she would be both victim and perpetrator of the violence.

I wonder about the possible presence of a ‘deaf-mute’ internal object, one that is unable to perceive and listen with the mind and with emotion, and also unable to communicate internally in a way that would allow her to work through certain traumatic emotional experiences (López-Corvo, 2006). Violence is even present in the fantasy of having the power to ‘destroy’ her first analyst's family if she became unable to control her own sexual impulses.

responseSet.title: The Analyst's Response to the Effects of the Transference: On Lacan and Bion
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Analyst's Response to the Effects of the Transference: On Lacan and Bion11

Carlos Barredo and Laura Etchegoyen

Laura Etchegoyen

6 Sandwell Crescent, London, NW6 1PB, UK — letchegoye@yahoo.co.uk

Laura Etchegoyen  

Three papers were presented at this panel: The platypus (Bion—Lacan) by Jacques Dufour, Subjective involvement of the analyst in the transference process: From ‘without memory and desire’ in Bion to ‘the desire of the analyst’ in Lacan by Dr Carlos A. Barredo and Dr Oscar A. Paulucci and The analyst's response to the effects of the transference by Dr Rogelio Sosnik.

J. Dufour entitled his paper The platypus as a reference to old scientific controversies regarding its being a mammal or an oviparous animal. A new category of ‘monotrems’ was created to accommodate the type. The author suggests that the binomial Bion—Lacan can be conceived of as such an animal, in which two divergent theoretical perspectives co-exist so that a new category or interpretive dynamic is created.

The author sees as the point of convergence between Bion and Lacan the issue of the lies of language in the psychoanalytic cure. However, this convergence entails a divergence in theorization: Lacan conceptualizes the lie as the irreductible alienation of the subject by language, whereas Bion conceptualizes the lie as a defensive manoeuvre against the subject's deep and intense anxieties. The different conceptualizations would lead to a different clinical approach, particularly regarding psychotic phenomena. The author proposes a line of reflection and opens up technical issues suggesting that perhaps one should bear in mind a two-way path at the core of the analytic process: Lacan's path which starts off at the surface of language and via the disruptions of the discourse re-finds the lost voice of the subject's unconscious, and Bion's path which starts off from the deep emotional disturbance experienced by the analyst in his/her contact with the patient so that he/she can then facilitate a developmental transformation into thought and language.

Dr C. Barredo and Dr O. Paulucci begin their paper, Subjective involvement of the analyst in the transference process: From ‘without memory and desire’ in Bion to ‘the desire of the analyst’ in Lacan

responseSet.title: Object Relations in Clinical Psychoanalysis
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Object Relations in Clinical Psychoanalysis11

Harold Blum

Harold Blum

23 The Hemlocks, Roslyn, NY 11576-1721, USA — Haroldpblum@cs.com

Harold Blum  

Harold Blum introduced the panel noting that, while object relations has a central role in psychoanalysis, the many roles and meanings of object and object relations are diverse, varying among different analytic frameworks. Controversies abound. The concept of the object as ‘object of the drive’ has been modified and expanded in the current theoretical pluralism of psychoanalysis. Different concepts of object, e.g. drive-object, part object, transitional object, narcissistic object, new object and external real objects, have advanced within different analytic frameworks. In developmental theory object relations refers to the importance of the primary caregiver-infant relationship, bonding, attachment, communication, and identification. In the theory of technique, object-relations has contributed to the change to a two-person analytic process evolving within an ever-present transference-counter transference field which has transformed classical, traditional views of clinical psychoanalysis. Do most psychoanalysts work today with a two-person model, a one-person model, or a one- and a two-person model? Object relations theories have importantly contributed to current interest in intersubjective and interpersonal effects, unconscious communication, projective identification, and other forms of bilateral analyst-patient influence in analytic work. Does the concept of technical neutrality need further inquiry and refinement? Is transference based primarily on unconscious childhood fantasy or is it co-constructed in the present related to the co-creation of an analytic narrative? What are the boundaries of the analyst's self-disclosure; does it benefit the analyst, the patient, or the analytic process? The new analytic object relationship, especially with very disturbed patients, is now accepted with interpretation as important in therapeutic action.

Daniel Widlöcher presented Object relations and the figure in the fantasy. He indicated that he does not keep the term of object relations in his vocabulary as a useful concept. He distinguished between the drive explanatory model and the unconscious fantasy. He presented a brief vignette: Mrs. X, 35 years old, learned that her mother has been rushed to the hospital for a dangerously severe depressive state with suicidal ideation and malnutrition. She had fraternal twin siblings, and her brother remained her mother's favorite.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

La mente estatica [The Ecstatic Mind]1 by Elvio FachinelliAdelphi, Milano, 2009; 201 pp.

Cristiana Cimino

Cristiana Cimino

E-mail: cristiana.cimino@fastwebnet.it

Cristiana Cimino  

In September 2009 the last of Elvio Fachinelli's books, The Ecstatic Mind, was reprinted, 20 years after the publication of the first edition and the author's death. This reprinting sought to refocus attention on a work by one of the most distinguished, underrated2 and probably misinterpreted figures of Italian psychoanalysis.3

A careful translator of Freud, a prominent figure during the ‘red-hot’ years of the radical sixties, a refined and declaratively ‘engage’ intellectual, Fachinelli is known for his experiments which were socially innovative and anti-institutional, such as the foundation of the refuge of Porta Ticinese (Milan) which he managed himself, to cite only the best known of his many ‘anti-authority’ pedagogical initiatives. Even his own position in relation to the psychoanalytic institution was often criticized, although he was someone who loved psychoanalysis deeply and valued his membership (he was a member of the Italian Psychoanalytic Society).

1Translated by Harriet Cooper.2Or, at any rate, Fachinelli's work has not been appreciated widely enough, particularly outside of Italy.3In 2008 Sergio Benvenuto and Anthony Molino published a collection of conversations with distinguished figures from the international psychoanalytic scene (Benvenuto and Molino, 2008). Among these figures was Elvio Fachinelli.1022

responseSet.title: Castration and Intolerance
responseSet.authorMast: Robert D. Hinshelwood and  Marilyn Charles
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Castration and Intolerance11

Robert Hinshelwood and Marilyn Charles

Marilyn Charles

Charles@AustenRiggs.net

Marilyn Charles  

Anatomy is destiny, said Freud; but does it have to be so? It is in the nature of social interaction that difference so easily becomes opposition and invites the urge to oppress. People become trapped in those social and gender roles, and entrapping of each other. In this panel, the speakers looked at the internal processes and mechanisms that complement the social attitudes, and which in turn respond to them. Psychoanalysis can nuance these life problems of reality and point to hidden aspects not touched by the more rational and perhaps simplistic political processes.

The first speaker, Marilyn Charles (North America), considered how gendered knowledge inhibits thought and the hegemony of the voice of authority. Reiterating Freud's question ‘what does a woman want?’ she uses her own experiences of marginalization as a way of considering how prejudice, intolerance, and stigma serve the regressive forces in groups that can literally cause us to ‘lose our minds’ - to not know what we thought we knew. This type of opposition to thinking exacerbates what LaCapra (1999) refers to as a ‘structural trauma’ that marks a deficit within the social fabric. LaCapra suggests that confusion between structural and individual trauma invites projection of badness onto the other and inhibits the type of mindful action that might invite more constructive social practice.

Charles (in press) discussed ways in which the gendering of knowledge and power invites ongoing structural trauma, noting Felman's (1993) argument regarding the difference between men and women such that the woman as subject inevitably adds something to the conversation in a way that cannot be superseded by the male voice. Without the voice of an Other, potentially useful information can be trivialized and discarded without reflection or consideration, and the voice of authority easily becomes a conjectured projection that cannot be located or disconfirmed or elaborated upon in actual discourse. She then discussed a case in which a young woman had become de-subjectified. Failing to recognize ways in which she becomes implicated in her parents’ stories left this woman disembodied, lost in the abstract, generalized realm of the type of absence described by LaCapra.

responseSet.title: Sándor Ferenczi's Clinical Diary in our Current Psychoanalytic Practice
responseSet.authorMast: Franco Borgogno and  Ana Rita Nuti Pontes
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Sándor Ferenczi's Clinical Diary in our Current Psychoanalytic Practice11

Franco Borgogno and Ana Pontes

Ana Pontes

120, 14110-00 Royal Park, Bonfim Paulista (SP), Brazil - mobydick@netsite.com.br

Ana Pontes  

Pedro Boschan, the first speaker, opened the panel arguing how important Ferenczi's ideas are for our current clinical practice. From a quite ‘Classical’ perspective he pointed out that, in Ferenczi's view, the transference-counter-transference field was much wider than the verbal exchange alone since it includes an archaic level of communication. The circulation of thoughts and feelings, sometimes transmitted almost telepathically, has to be carefully explored in the session and in the long wave of an analysis in order to make accessible to symbolization a material not ‘figurable’ and representable before. When the perception of this kind of thoughts and feelings is subjected to denial and disawoval by the analytic dyad, and above all by the analyst, according to Ferenczi the original traumatic situation is inevitably re-enacted, so that in our work we need in this regard to be sincere both with ourselves and with our patients to the point of, somehow, being mutual and symmetric in the analytic exchange.

The other two speakers, Jonathan Sklar and Josette Garon, presented from slightly different points of view (one ‘British Independent’, the latter strongly linked to a ‘French stance’), the clinical application of Ferenczi's theory with two very difficult patients, whose analysis - characterized by a negative transference - was stuck with no end to be seen. Sklar showed in his paper, Somatic resistance to termination: The contemporary use of active technique, his use of Ferenczi's ‘active therapy’ in a treatment where, after nine years of work, the patient started to have persistent severe physical symptoms related to the end of the analysis. Basically, for bringing up the affect, Sklar had to invite his patient to scream to help her to feel and talk about her profound rage and sadness, which she had always kept away from the emotional level.

responseSet.title: The use of the Alpha Function in Analytical Construction
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The use of the Alpha Function in Analytical Construction11

Alfredo Lombardozzi

Alfredo Lombardozzi

alfredo.lombardozzi@gmail.com

Alfredo Lombardozzi  

The panel held at the Chicago IPA Congress, ‘The Use of the Alpha Function in Analytical Construction’ was a really good occasion to reflect on Bion's thinking. Lucilla Ruberti and Marina Parisi (Italian Psychoanalytical Society) presented a very interesting paper, discussed by Lia Pistiner De Cortiñas, from Argentina (Buenos Aires Psychoanalytic Association) and Lawrence J. Brown, from the USA (American Psychoanalytic Association).

Following the outlines of the congress, the paper dealt with the way in which Italian psychoanalysis approaches some aspects of Bion's thinking that gained acceptance relatively early in Italy compared to other European Societies. This was perhaps because it arrived at the right time to give space to call for authenticity in a group which had then just recently been formed, and which clearly needed to start feeling freer from rigid elements of theory.

In their paper, Lucilla Ruberti and Marina Parisi refer significantly to Francesco Corrao who was one of the first Italian analysts to be influenced by the profoundly innovative quality of Bion's thought. Corrao described the change that took place in his way of conceiving the process of knowledge in psychoanalysis, which has become our own, “replace the impatient pretension of finding a method to capture ‘the truth’ with the patience of the never-ending search”. This call ‘to be patient’ and to ‘renounce’ was supplemented by a proposal to use indirect or ‘metaphorical’ knowledge in approaching reality respecfully, introducing ‘narrative construction’ - which, within the psychoanalytical space, becomes ‘narrative interpretation’ - a tool of analysis that transforms emotive turbulence and lends meaning to un-thought thoughts. This way of rethinking interpretation was new, but also profoundly linked to Freud in Constructions in Analysis.

In this perspective Parisi and Ruberti present two clinical cases. Marina Parisi (in Laura: Construction of narration), through fragments of the treatment of a girl, describes the emotive and mental work that Laura went through in analysis. She was a little girl with a violent streak who was adopted more than once and showed strong deprivation traumas.

responseSet.title: Spontaneity: A Psychoanalytic Inquiry by Gemma Corradi Fiumara Routledge, Hove, New York, NY, 2009; 149 pp.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Spontaneity: A Psychoanalytic Inquiry by Gemma Corradi FiumaraRoutledge, Hove, New York, NY, 2009; 149 pp.

Marcia Cavell

Marcia Cavell

375 Riverside Drive 9B, New York, NY 10025, USA
E-mail: mcavell@earthlink.net

Marcia Cavell  

Spontaneity is a rich and difficult concept, tied as it is, as Professor Corradi Fiumara shows, to other equally deep concepts in a conceptual web that comes close to defining what we mean by a human being. The concepts are subjectivity, autonomy, the capacity to live with paradox, integration, agency versus passivity, the capacity for forgiveness, for empathy versus sympathy. She might have placed most of the others at the centre of this web but spontaneity is the most surprising and, except for paradox, the least understood. Spontaneity for her is a condition of the whole soul; it is a moral virtue.

It will serve to locate the tradition in which Corradi Fiumara writes to cite the names she mentions most often - among analysts, Fromm, Bion, Symington, Winnicott, Klein, among philosophers, Nietzche and Weil, and Kristeva, who is both. Like Fromm, her central interest is freedom and all the ways in which we attempt to escape from it, for example, identifying with the other in a way that leaves him outside oneself; a feared figure rather than one whose values one has internalized. An illuminating idea here is that the patient who hopes to be transformed by incorporating someone else's knowledge wants what freedom exactly precludes. Freedom - or agency - comes in acknowledging that we are always interpreting what happens to us. This is one of Sartre's ideas in support of his claim that we are always free.

In Chapter Two, ‘Rethinking Internalization’, a continuation of the discussion of spontaneity, she writes: “Spontaneity is perhaps developed by being capable of resisting the joint threat of outer persuaders and inner agents” (p. 20). This sets the stage for the more general claim that, no matter how many outward or inner influences, spontaneity and the valuing capacity always come from the core of oneself. Of course, one may have learned values from society or from one's beloved objects. Without love or guidance, the child will become an amoral monster. What she needs is a loving parent who teaches her what it is to cherish an ideal, a community, or a person. This is the ground from which spontaneity may grow.

L'intima stanza. Teoria e tecnica del campo analitico [The intimate Room: Theory and Technique of the Analytic Field]1 by Giuseppe Civitarese Boria, Roma, 2008; 230 pp.; €22.99

Giuseppe Di Chiara

Giuseppe Di Chiara

Via Corridoni, 144, 20122 Milano, Italy
E-mail: vincenzo.bonaminio@gmail.com

Giuseppe Di Chiara  

Eight urgent and intense chapters, to engage us - as Antonino Ferro puts it in his preface - on the topics of transference, relating and the analytic field. This work comes from an author who can be counted ‘among the very few’ who work on the analytic field. What is more, he is not an ‘easy’ author (to quote Ferro again), by which he means, it seems to me, that it is not Civitarese's style to illustrate and thus dilute the bold positions of an experienced analyst, which would entail abandoning the complexity of psychoanalytic work and its great resources of knowledge and experience.

We shall start with the first chapter, ‘Fire at the Theatre’. The reader finds himself suddenly caught up in fierce tension between transference as an event which is eminently repetitive and transference as a new and original creation of the analytic encounter. Civitarese takes up the challenge to save both of these positions. Therefore he does not yield either to Fonagy's position (“a mode of being with the other”) or to Stern's (“moments of meeting”), or to Bion's (“being in unison”) (p. 35). Instead, along with Ferro, he works “in the today of the meeting”, but this is done so as to work through “what happens in the emotional field that the encounter constructs”. In my opinion, the challenge proposed by the author and the risk it entails is that of the ‘hystericization’ of the transference. This means allowing both the greatest possible exposition of the patient's story as well as maximizing exposure to it (en abîme, a term which will be used later in the book); the analyst ‘saves’ himself, that is, steps out of this ‘mirror play’ only at the last moment - at the latest possible moment - so that the patient's ‘secret’ is not ripped out.

Not without reason is the second chapter dedicated to ‘The Symbiotic Bond and the Setting’. It takes an off-beat route which starts with Bleger, with his ‘agglutinated nucleus’ and then picks up the ideas of post-Kleinian authors, with Marcelli's ‘fusional-symbiotic level’, Ogden's ‘autistic-contiguous position’, Hautman's ‘film of thought’, Meltzer's ‘adhesive identification and dismantling’, Stern's ‘now moments’ or the ‘fusion’ described by Soavi, Pallier, Tagliacozzo, Neri and Petacchi. These writers all agree on a particular line of thought in relation to the therapeutic process: “What counts is that the patient can live the symbiotic bond in conditions of security” (p. 65). The crucial point, in these situations, is the “conditions of security”.

Mauro Manica  

There are books, and above all there are ideas which can never be ‘marked’ or impoverished by time. Rather, the passage of time continually enriches and transforms them. There are thoughts which will never be tamed, not once, not ever: thoughts which have something ‘wild’ about them (as Bion would have said), which bring with them the potential for ever more new meanings to develop from them.

‘Time is active and it produces. What does it produce?’ Without causing the irrevocable death of the question, Thomas Mann, in The Magic Mountain

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Collateral Damage: Grief and Recovery after Losing a Training Analyst Due to an Ethical Violation11

Glen Gabbard and Elizabeth Wallace

Elizabeth Wallace

3rd Floor, 520 — 13 Avenue S.W., Calgary, AB T2R 0X7, Canada — elizabethm.wallace@albertahealthservices.ca

Elizabeth Wallace  

This panel broke new ground in exploring the ‘collateral damage’ experienced by three candidate-analysands whose training analysts had committed ethical violations with other patients. In his opening remarks, Dr. Glen Gabbard noted that these first-person accounts constitute a unique expansion of our knowledge about boundary violations, and emphasized that collateral damage related to boundary violations is always present, including deleterious effects on other patients, institutes, and the reputation of psychoanalysis.

Dr. Candace Young from the USA provided a moving account of the personal and institutional fall-out that ensued in the third year of her training analysis after her training analyst admitted to a sexual boundary violation and subsequently lost his licence. She was given six months to deal with this profound rupture within a previously productive analysis. This period of analysis was characterized by a shared grief as well as her feelings of outrage, betrayal and loss, complicated by the analyst's growing vulnerability and neediness that evoked protectiveness and loyalty in Dr. Young. She described the experience as a unique three-fold trauma, involving the absolute loss of an analyst in a mid-phase analysis, as certainly as to a death; the traumatic collapse of idealization of the analyst; and the profound disillusionment in and collapse of idealization of her chosen profession. Dr. Young faced the dilemma of finding a new training analyst in a small professional community, and described the initial experience of analysis with a ‘replacement analyst’ as characterized by high anxiety and negative expectations typical of post-traumatic experience. Her experience of confusion, hypervigilance, and hypersensitivity to changes in frame or minor shifts arose from a profound sense of internal disorganization that was disturbing to both herself and her new analyst. She noted that a replacement analyst serves as a magnet for unmetabolized introjects of the former analyst, and requires considerable resilience and tolerance for anger and grief.

Corrective Emotional Experience Revisited11

Steven Cooper and Megan McCarthy

Megan McCarthy

elena.mccarthy@gmail.com

Megan McCarthy  

André Haynal: Corrective emotional experience revisited

André Haynal's paper, Corrective emotional experience revisited, examines Alexander as a biographical figure and, in particular, explores his relationship with Sándor Ferenczi in order to understand the sources of Alexander's analytic thought and innovations.

Haynal reminds us that Alexander arrived in Berlin in 1920 from Budapest and joined the Berlin Institute, where analysts were exploring variations in analytic technique, including session length, session frequency, and the absence of fees, in order to better identify and distill how analytic change occurs. Alexander adopted several analytic ideas from Ferenczi, including the desire to make analysis as efficient as possible, the relative unimportance of neutrality in the analyst, the importance of flexible analytic technique and of countertransference as an instrument to understand the patient, and the conveyance of such reactions in an emotional message to the patient. Alexander believed that the curative components in analysis are comprised not just of insights, but also of emotional experiences in the relationship between analyst and analysand.

Haynal reminds us that neither Ferenczi nor Alexander was an “exclusively relational analyst,” but that Alexander believed that analysts should “be able to adapt to the psychotherapeutic requests and to the modifications of attitude they imply.” Haynal notes that Alexander's idea of a corrective emotional experience is similar to the concept of Balint's new beginning, of building a new object in therapy, different from the old object, which has been introjected on the basis of painful past relationships. Haynal dates the origins of the idea of corrective emotional experience to Alexander's oft-cited paper, The therapeutic factors in analytic treatment (1950), in which Alexander discusses inadvertently revealing his irritation to a patient, thereby participating in an enactment. Haynal notes that Alexander generally favored the use of such negative reactions on the part of the analyst in analysis.

responseSet.title: On: ‘Affirming: “That's not Psychoanalysis!’” by R. Blass
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On: ‘Affirming: “That's not Psychoanalysis!’” by R. Blass

Steven Stern

Steven Stern

Massachusetts Institute for Psychoanalysis 217 Commercial St., Ste. 203, Portland, ME, 04101, USA
E-mail: ssternpsyd@aol.com

Steven Stern  

Dear Editors,

In her recent article, Affirming ‘That's not psycho-analysis!’, Rachel Blass (2010) provocatively argues for an exclusive, limiting definition of psychoanalysis on the grounds that it is reasonable, intellectually honest, and conducive to constructive debate, to articulate the ‘truth’ about what psychoanalysis is. She implies that such a definition would somehow transcend the historical proliferation and progression of ideas regarding the nature of mind and therapeutic action in our field, and would aid in the determination of which among these ideas are truly psychoanalytic and which are not.

In making her assertions, although seeking to avoid committing herself to a particular definition, Blass clearly has in mind a type of definition that I believe has, at this point in our history, outlived its usefulness. It is problematic because it perpetuates a longstanding category error (Aron, 2009; Stern, 2009a, 2009b) - namely, equating our field of professional practice with a particular theory, treatment arrangement, technique, or conception of therapeutic process. No other comparable profession does this. Imagine if the field of medicine had defined itself in terms of the practice of bloodletting (the most common medical practice for 2000 years ending in the late 19th century), or if the field of clinical psychology had, in the 1970s, defined itself as following Skinner's laws of operant conditioning. I wouldn't say our situation is quite that dire, but the extreme examples dramatize the problem. These other professional fields wisely define themselves in terms of the domain of human problems or needs they address and/or their general fields of inquiry and the evolving body of knowledge, skill, and wisdom that are the constant products of those fields of inquiry. While psychoanalysis may not have been in a position to define itself in this way when Freud invented it, I believe it is in such a position now. We now have a substantial, complex, and evolving body of knowledge and competencies that can and should form the foundation for both psychoanalytic education and psychoanalytic practice.

The Narcissistic Difficulties Presented to the Observer by the Psychosomatic Problem11

Pierre Marty

Pierre Marty

Pierre Marty  

Dealing with psychosomatic realities often generates narcissistic neurotic resistances in the observer that form an obstacle to carrying out scientific work. The tendencies towards schematization and the enforced investigation of arbitrarily defined objects of study, such as they appear in some theoretical standpoints, are aspects of these resistances. The self-destruction evoked by the problem of the ‘death drives’ in its superficial dimension constitutes an obstacle of the same kind.

[[RunningHead]]

Perversion11

Adrienne Harris and Francisco Gonzalez

Francisco Gonzalez

1988 Bush Street, San Francisco, CA 94115, USA— f.j.g@comcast.net

Francisco Gonzalez  

The question of what constitutes a perversion, and of what perversion constitutes, has been with us at least since Freud's early formulation of perversion as the negative of the neuroses in the Three Essays (Freud, 1905). The controversial concept, as Adrienne Harris indicated in her opening remarks, is a magnet for all manner of moral, ethical, and unconscious elements in the field. The congress theme of ‘convergence and divergence’ was fully satisfied in this panel, which aptly demonstrates that more than 100 years after Freud's paper, we are still not in accord as to what constitutes perversion.

Ruth Stein presented the first paper, a sophisticated examination that acknowledged and actively engaged this complex history. For Stein the hallmark of perversion is ‘false love’ based on a perverse pact that disavows reality in favor of illusion. It origins are often in an enmeshed alliance between child and parent that employs seductive specialness to cover an underlying corruption of privacy and mutual respect. Stein takes great care to avoid a reductionistic reading of perversion as a deviation from heterosexual genitality; indeed, such a devaluation of ‘fore pleasures’ could itself be a perverse disavowal of socio-cultural evolution. But she is interested in preserving Freud's notion of means—ends reversal, if we can free this idea of its concrete sexual trappings and view it relationally. The false pact is demonstrated in the declaration of a stated end that is then used as a means to another, disavowed and hidden end. As an example, Stein relates her experience of an online psychoanalytic discussion group in which one participant denigrated others out of an espoused concern for the poor state of psychoanalytic thinking, masking his true aim of degrading others to achieve a sense of superiority. Similarly, perverse patients present to analysis with the ostensible aim to grow and develop, when their real end is to mock, deceive, or degrade the analyst. The perverse patient pulls the analyst into a theater of cynicism, one whose project it is to demonstrate that intimacy is bankrupt, that love is corrupt, and that humans are little more than hypocritical cowards. Because of the dissimulation involved, such strategies work insidiously and can take quite a long time to uncover.

responseSet.title: The Two Time Vectors of Nachträglichkeit in the Development of Ego Organization: Significance of the Concept for the Symbolization of Nameless Traumas and Anxieties
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Two Time Vectors of Nachträglichkeit in the Development of Ego Organization: Significance of the Concept for the Symbolization of Nameless Traumas and Anxieties11

Gerhard Dahl

Gerhard Dahl

Berliner Psychoanalytisches Institut (DPV/IPA), Beuckestr. 31, D-14163 Berlin, Germany — drdahl@t-online.de

Gerhard Dahl  

après-coup, deferred action, early traumas, ego organization, factual reality, Nachträglichkeit, symbolization, two time vectors, unconscious phantasy, unconscious scene

(Final version accepted 28 January 2009)

The author describes Freud's conception of Nachträglichkeit as an active process that bridges the gap between past affective vicissitudes and the cognitive present by way of meaning. Symbolization is thereby subsequently [nachträglich] conferred on early traumatic events, which thus become susceptible to omnipotent control. The two time vectors of Nachträglichkeit are discussed: the first is a causal process operating in the forward direction of time against the background of a factual reality, while the second is a backward movement that permits an understanding of unconscious scenes and phantasies taking place at primary-process level. This twofold temporal motion was observed and described by Freud early on. However, its significance often remained hidden prior to his study of Moses. It was mostly overlooked in English and French translations, thus giving rise to a one-sided understanding of the concept in the various psychoanalytic cultures, as either deferred action or après-coup. Freud's Moses study addresses both temporal aspects of Nachträglichkeit, seeking not only to reconstruct a past event on a causal, deterministic basis, but also to understand the subjective truth of that event in the transference along the retrograde time line. The decisive criterion for the conceptual and clinical separation of the two time vectors is the development of ego organization and the capacity for symbolization. The two vectors should not be separated on the factual level, as both aspects of Nachträglichkeit are essential to the understanding of unconscious processes, combining as they do in a relationship of circular complementarity.

responseSet.title: Murder on the Mind: Tyranical Power and other Points Along the Perverse Spectrum
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Murder on the Mind: Tyranical Power and other Points Along the Perverse Spectrum

Richard Tuch

Richard Tuch

Los Angeles, 1800 Fairburn Ave., Ste. 206, Los Angeles, CA 90025, USA — rtuch@aol.com

Richard Tuch  

disavowal, fetish, fetishization, perversions, perversity, sadomasochism

This paper illustrates the breadth and depth of the spectrum of perversion and perversity as currently represented in the psychoanalytic literature, raises questions about recent tendencies to include a host of diverse-seeming phenomena under the same conceptual umbrella, and strives to demonstrate what these phenomena have in common that justifies lumping them together under the same rubric. One end of this spectrum is represented by the employment of simple fetishes introduced into a sexual scene in order to promote sexual arousal. Moving along the continuum, one encounters increasing complex behavioral patterns including the enactment of scripts that actualize one's perverse fantasies, including the assumption of complementary roles (e.g. sadomasochism) that equally serve the needs, and represent the desires, of both parties involved. A unique clinical entity, ‘perverse modes of relatedness,’ lies on the extreme end of the spectrum, representing the reification of the relationship as it becomes little more than a vehicle to take possession and control one's object for the gratification of one's sole needs and desires. What each of these phenomena share in common is both the insertion of a thing or condition — ranging from a simple fetishistic object to an elaborate style of relating that reduces the other into pawn played upon the pervert's chessboard, between the two ‘relating’ objects as well as a less than honest relationship with reality.

(Final version accepted 4 August 2009)

responseSet.title: From Freud's Dream-Work to Bion's Work of Dreaming: The Changing Conception of Dreaming in Psychoanalytic Theory
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

From Freud's Dream-Work to Bion's Work of Dreaming: The Changing Conception of Dreaming in Psychoanalytic Theory

John Schneider

John Schneider

2340 Ward Street, Suite 106, Berkeley, CA 94705, USA — drjaschneider@yahoo.com

John Schneider  

Bion, dreaming, dreamwork, Freud

(Final version accepted 8 September 2009)

Bion moved psychoanalytic theory from Freud's theory of dream-work to a concept of dreaming in which dreaming is the central aspect of all emotional functioning. In this paper, I first review historical, theoretical, and clinical aspects of dreaming as seen by Freud and Bion. I then propose two interconnected ideas that I believe reflect Bion's split from Freud regarding the understanding of dreaming. Bion believed that all dreams are psychological works in progress and at one point suggested that all dreams contain elements that are akin to visual hallucinations. I explore and elaborate Bion's ideas that all dreams contain aspects of emotional experience that are too disturbing to be dreamt, and that, in analysis, the patient brings a dream with the hope of receiving the analyst's help in completing the unconscious work that was entirely or partially too disturbing for the patient to dream on his own. Freud views dreams as mental phenomena with which to understand how the mind functions, but believes that dreams are solely the ‘guardians of sleep,’ and not, in themselves, vehicles for unconscious psychological work and growth until they are interpreted by the analyst. Bion extends Freud's ideas, but also departs from Freud and re-conceives of dreaming as synonymous with unconscious emotional thinking — a process that continues both while we are awake and while we are asleep. From another somewhat puzzling perspective, he views dreams solely as manifestations of what the dreamer is unable to think.

responseSet.title: Creating Analysts, Creating Analytic Patients
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Creating Analysts, Creating Analytic Patients11

Howard Levine

Howard Levine

124 Dean Road, Brookline, MA 02445, USA — hblevine@aol.com

Howard Levine  

initiating analysis, intensifying frequency, intersubjectivity, unrepresented mental states, treatment process, analyzability, case finding, transformation, countertransference, psychoanalytic psychotherapy

(Final version accepted 16 March 2010)

This paper applies a contemporary, ‘two-track’ — transformational as well as archaeological — perspective on psychoanalytic process to clinical issues in the creation of analytic patients: case finding, recommending analysis, and recommending and negotiating the intensification of frequency of sessions in analytic psychotherapy. Central importance is assigned to the role of the mind and analytic identity of the analyst, including the analyst's capacity to maintain an internal analytic frame and analyzing attitude from the very first contact with the patient and throughout the treatment, the analyst's confidence in and conviction about the usefulness of analysis for a given analytic dyad and the role of the analyst's theory, which must be broad and consistent enough to allow the analyst to feel that he or she is operating analytically when addressing non-neurotic (unrepresented and weakly represented mental states) as well as neurotic structures.

responseSet.title: Why read Fairbairn?
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Why read Fairbairn?

Thomas Ogden

Thomas Ogden

306 Laurel Street, San Francisco, CA 94118, USA — togden68@aol.com

Thomas Ogden  

addictive love, character, contempt, Fairbairn, internal objects, internal saboteur, metapsychology, resentment, trauma, transference

The author offers a close reading of portions of Fairbairn's work in which he not only explicates and clarifies Fairbairn's thinking, but generates ideas of his own by developing concepts that he believes to be implicit in, or logical extensions of, Fairbairn's work. Among the unstated or underdeveloped aspects of Fairbairn's contribution that the author discusses are (1) the idea that the formation of the internal object world is always, in part, a response to trauma (actual failure on the part of the mother to convey to her infant a sense that she loves him and accepts his love); (2) the notion that the infant's unceasing efforts to transform the internalized relationship with the unloving mother into a loving relationship — thus reversing the effect on his mother of his (imagined) ‘toxic love’ — is the single most important motivation sustaining the structure of the internal object world; and (3) the idea that attacks on oneself for the way one loves, while self-destructive, contain a glimmer of insight into one's own self-hatred and shame regarding one's endless, futile attempts to change oneself (or the rejecting object) into a different person. The author, using his own clinical work, illustrates the way he makes use of his understanding of the ‘emotional life’ of internal objects to facilitate the patient's emotional growth.

(Final version accepted 1 July 2009)

responseSet.title: Prejudice, Transgenerational Transmission, and Neutrality
responseSet.authorMast: Fanny Blanck-Cereijido and  Myriam Grynberg Robinson
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Prejudice, Transgenerational Transmission, and Neutrality11

Fanny Blanck-Cereijido and Myriam Robinson

Myriam Robinson

cereijidofanny@gmail.com

myriamgc32@hotmail.com

Myriam Robinson  

Dr Fanny Blanck Cereijido, the moderator, began by stating that this panel sprang from theoretical and clinical questions surrounding how to handle the problem of prejudice within the psychoanalytic session. Departing from the conception that the psychoanalytic cure includes the psychoanalyst in a dialectic analyst-analyzand process to which both are committed, the effect of the analyst is recognized in the transference of the analyzand, who responds to his own history and also to the way in which he is awaited and heard.

The discourse of the patient that was presented provoked a strong countertransferential impact in the analyst. The patient is the granddaughter of Nazis and has filicide fantasies about the baby she is carrying. The analyst is Jewish and has a history of a family that disappeared in the lagers and a father who spent years of imprisonment in Soviet Russia.

All the panel participants worked with the above clinical material. The different geographical origins and non-homogenic frames of reference created the possibility of different psychoanalytic focuses among the panelists.

The impossibility of thinking without the pre-existing baggage of criteria and values colors and modulates the analytic relationship and the countertransference. Prejudice appears necessarily in the minds of both analyst and analyzand through family beliefs, sense of belonging, theoretical positions, and therapeutic objectives. Conscious or unconscious prejudices towards someone who presents different beliefs, particularly in situations that are meaningful to the analyst, can become a countertransferential obstacle. Rather than denying his/her feelings of rejection of certain beliefs and attitudes of the patient, the analyst should but make them conscious and work with them.
