responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Clinical Treatment of Psychosomatic Symptoms11

Marilia Aisenstein

Marilia Aisenstein

72 rue d'Assas, Paris, 75006, France - marilia.aisenstein@gmail.com

Marilia Aisenstein  

Dr de Aisemberg spoke of the question of whether the clinical approach to somatic patients was psychotherapy or psychoanalytic therapy. She reminded the audience that, thanks to Alexander, Chicago became the cradle of Psychosomatic Medicine. However, we have come a long way in order to gain a psychoanalytic approach to the somatic phenomenon, or somatosis. What began as an extension of psychoanalysis, giving rise to important theoretical developments, has currently been established at the very core of Contemporary Psychoanalysis. This is a polemical position as the clinical approach to somatosis sometimes requires the commencement and/or continuance of treatment within a face-to-face setting, on a twice-weekly basis. All this involves asking ourselves whether we have embarked upon a psychoanalytic psychotherapy or upon the approach to non-neurotic or narcissistic functioning, which coexists with psychoneurotic functioning within Contemporary Psychoanalysis. In fact, this is her own position as she believes that somatosis points to another psychic functioning, which is the expression of the genuine unconscious or unconscious proper, the unconscious that was never conscious, while psychoneurotic functioning refers to the repressed unconscious.

She understands somatosis as the mise-en-scène of primitive, sensory traces - traces that are potentially traumatic - which have not been transformed, which are probably in between soma and psyche, within the Freudian id, deeply rooted in the soma. These traces, on being invested by excessive endosomatic excitation, a product, in turn, of the exteroceptive and interoceptive perception of the relationship with the object, fail to be transformed into drives and short-circuit to the soma. It is her view that this excessive quantity could be the essence of Thanatos. These traces are primitive experiences which have taken place before the acquisition of language, which have never been conscious and which are unchained by a trauma or mourning taking place at the present time.

responseSet.title: Distinguishing Psychoanalysis from Psychotherapy
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Distinguishing Psychoanalysis from Psychotherapy

Daniel Widlöcher

Daniel Widlöcher

79, boulevard Vincent Auriol, 75013 Paris, France — d.h.widlocher@wanadoo.fr

Daniel Widlöcher  

There is a little story, quite old by now, which seems to me to epitomize quite well how ambivalent French psychoanalysts feel towards the notion of psychotherapy. It refers to the first translations into French of the well-known sentence of Freud's 1919 paper: “It is very probable, too, that the large-scale application of our therapy will compel us to alloy the pure gold of analysis freely with the copper of direct suggestion”. One finds in that context that the word plomb [lead] appears instead of the word cuivre [copper]. In fact, in the 1990s, when I used to refer to this text and talked about copper, my French colleagues would protest and ‘remind’ me that the correct word was lead! We had to wait for recent translations for this mistake to be corrected. It is noteworthy that, in French, the words lead and copper have very different connotations. Lead symbolizes heaviness and the attempts of alchemists to transform it into gold are well known. When gold is mixed with lead, lead takes away its qualities, and one is reminded of Racine's famous verse in Athalie: “Comment en ce plomb vil l'or pur s'est-il changé' [‘How into this vile lead did this pure gold change itself’]. In contrast, copper with gold make up an alloy widely used in jewellery and in metallurgy. One may regard this mistake in translation as a kind of Freudian slip, and the French, although so careful with words, have indeed been quick to adopt the slip of the translator, Anne Berman, who perhaps not insignificantly was close to Marie Bonaparte! Why this negative attitude?

This may be understood in terms of developments that took place after World War II when psychoanalysis became popular in France. At that time psychoanalytic psychotherapies began to proliferate, either in order to be more adapted to special ages or specific pathologies, or to satisfy a great need for treatment in the population. These were distinguished from traditional psychoanalytic treatment with the patient lying on the couch, the analyst sitting behind the couch in his armchair, four or five sessions a week, which was referred to as ‘cure-type’ practice.1 In contrast to traditional practice the new methods rendered the setting of the treatment less strict, leading to what I have often denounced as the art of treating more difficult cases with a lighter technique, a simple variety of light psychoanalysis.

responseSet.title: The Work of Confluence: Listening and Interpreting in the Psychoanalytic Field by Madeleine Willy Baranger (Edited by Leticia Glocer Fiorini). London: Karnac Books, 2009. 254 pp; £24.99.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Work of Confluence: Listening and Interpreting in the Psychoanalytic Field by Madeleine Willy Baranger (Edited by Leticia Glocer Fiorini). London: Karnac Books, 2009. 254 pp; £24.99.

Andrea Sabbadini and Antonino Ferro

Antonino Ferro

Via Cardano 77, Pavia 27100, Italy
E-mail: antonino.ferro3@tin.it

Antonino Ferro  

This is a valuable collection in the English language of several of the many articles written by Willy and Madeleine Baranger. It was a clever editorial decision to add to those papers which I would describe as psychoanalytic classics (those concerning the field) also other essays which demonstrate those authors' depth of thinking well beyond their main interest (the bipersonal field). It is inevitable, however, that I shall mostly focus in this review on those articles about the field which in my opinion have represented a sort of historical turning point, or even revolution or catastrophic change, in psychoanalysis, the consequences of which we have not yet been able fully to appreciate. It is almost as if the community of physicists had been kept deprived for a long time of Einstein's relativity equation or of Heisenberg's uncertainty principle. It is for this reason that I could only begin from an article which is not included in this book, as it is the starting point of the Barangers' work and contains within itself the seeds of all their later theoretical developments.

It may perhaps be useful in the first place to make a reference to the cultural climate within which the concept of field emerged. Precursors to such a concept for the study of psychic phenomena were already present in Gestalt psychology and in Kurt Lewin's work, where the field had acquired a wider epistemological autonomy by being disconnected from its physicalistic origins. Perhaps less well-known is the revision of this concept operated by Merleau-Ponty, an author who occupies an important position in the Barangers' philosophical background. He refers to the notion of field, from a similar perspective to that used by Koffka and Lewin, in so far as it is useful as a foundation for a psychology of man in situation, allowing us to observe and understand psychic facts in terms of their meaning in the context of intersubjective relationships. This would be a way to overcome the historical conflict between naturalistic objectivization and solipsistic introspection (Bezoari and Ferro 1992).

responseSet.title: Envy and Gratitude Revisited edited by Priscilla Roth and Alessandra Lemma International Psychoanalytical Association, London, 2008; 252 pp; £25.99
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Envy and Gratitude Revisited edited by Priscilla Roth and Alessandra LemmaInternational Psychoanalytical Association, London, 2008; 252 pp; £25.99

Gabriella Grauso

Gabriella Grauso

Via Solari 23, Milano, 20144 Italy
E-mail: mariagabriella_grauso@fastwebnet.it

Gabriella Grauso  

J. Ruskin, in his book The Stones of Venice, describes Envy as depicted by Giotto amongst the others vices and virtues in the Scrovegni Chapel in Padova:

Giotto has, however, represented her, with still greater subtlety, as having her fingers terminating in claws, and raising her right hand with an expression partly of impotent regret, partly of involuntary grasping; a serpent, issuing from her mouth, is about to bite her between her eyes: she has long membranous ears, horns on her head and flames consuming her body.

(Ruskin, 1911[1853], p. 345)

I will not attempt a detailed analysis of Giotto's impressive representation of this allegorical figure, which is the most complex of all and the only one not painted in monochrome. Suffice it to say that it seems to describe the ultimate, most destructive attack, namely the attack on one's own self, the perceiving self (the snake which tries to bite between the eyes). Also relevant to Envy and Gratitude Revisited is the fact that Giotto, unlike Klein, contrasts Envy with Caritas, the catholic virtue equated with Love (by Saint Paul and Saint Augustine).

Priscilla Roth, in her beautiful introduction to this book, gives a summary of the content, eliciting the various themes running through it and linking the relevant ideas. I will therefore just give my own impressions of each contribution.

I will start with Ron Britton's paper, He thinks himself impaired: The pathological envious personality. This is a scholarly paper in which Britton uses Milton's description of Satan in Paradise Lost and Trollope's character Mr Reverend Crawley in The Last Chronicle of Barset (Trollope, 1867) to describe the complexity of what he calls ‘the triad of pride, despair and envy’. In the opening of the paper he tries to follow the development of the concept of Envy from Abraham, through Klein up to the present and also, most interestingly, in various religions, Jewish, Christian and Muslim. Britton makes the connection to the contrast in Catholic catechism between Envy and Charity as depicted in Giotto. He poignantly describes Mr Crawley's difficulty in accepting much needed charity.

[[RunningHead]]

Hidden Gifts of Love: A Clinical Application of Object Relations Theory

Naomi Steinberg

Naomi Steinberg

2066 NW Irving St., Suite 3, Portland, OR 97209, USA — naomial@msn.com

Naomi Steinberg  

countertransference, depressive position, enactment, psychoanalytic psychotherapy, trauma

(Final version accepted 2 November 2009)

Using an extended clinical example, the author applies aspects of Kleinian, Fairbairnian, and Bionian theory to demonstrate how individuals may come to hide away feelings of both love and aggression. In the clinical material presented, a version of a schizoid retreat was understood as a pervasive response to trauma. The author attempts to explore more specifically the nature of a ‘traumatizing outer world’ (Guntrip, 1969) and how these experiences cause an individual to retreat and undermine movement toward healthy adult dependency. An understanding of these dynamics helps inform the psychoanalytic treatment process and can serve as a type of roadmap in navigating through challenging transference-countertrans-ference enactments.

responseSet.title: Kohut Today
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Kohut Today11

David Terman and James Fisch

James Fisch

jmfisch123@comcast.net

James Fisch  

In his introductory remarks David Terman recalled his early years at the Chicago Institute: Heinz Kohut's masterful seminar on psychoanalytic theory (1965), his 1966 presidential address, ‘Forms and Transformations of Nacissism’, then his new seminar laying out new ideas culminating in The Analysis of the Self (1971). One felt they were hearing the most important psychoanalytic ideas since publication of The Ego and the Id. The essence of these ‘new phenomena’ is the selfobject… the centrality of the ‘other’ as an intrinsic part of Self…and the systematic understanding of the crucial role interactions play in the development of structure. The analyst participates in the revival, repair and growth of that structure. In today's panel, a common thread in all three presentations is the nature, meaning, and importance of the analyst's responses at critical moments in the analysis. Normal development requires the participation of the caregiver, now the analyst, to gain access to affects central to an emerging self within a developmental context being re-lived in the analytic relationship.

Paul Ornstein then presented a paper, Heinz Kohut: Then and now: The essence of his contributions to psychoanalysis. When Kohut began working on a new paradigm in psychoanalysis, many analysts felt that the leading theory, ego psychology, had become too abstract and distant. In On narcissism: An introduction, Freud had stated that he was leaving to one side the themes of “[t]he disturbances to which the child's original narcissism is exposed, the reactions with which he seeks to protect himself from them and the paths into which he is forced in doing so” (1914); Freud had alluded to these themes as “an important field of work which still awaits exploration”, and this was the field of exploration of Kohut.

Freud's ego psychology aims at elucidating the nature of the patient's psychopathology and underlying conflict, essentially showing the patient how his mind works, and transference analysis demonstrates to the patient the distorting impact of the transference on the patient's current experience in relation to the analyst. The idealized analytic ambience of ego psychology is studied neutrality

responseSet.title: Psychosis and Regression
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychosis and Regression11

Liliane Abensour

Liliane Abensour

liliane.abensour@wanadoo.fr

Liliane Abensour  

The panel I organized and chaired had been the occasion of a vivid and even passionate exchange between the presenter of a very rich clinical case, Riccardo Lombardi (a training member of the Italian Psychoanalytic Society) and two discussants, Anne Silver (member of the American Psychoanalytic Society) and David Rosenfeld (a training analyst of the Psychoanalytic Society of Buenos Aires), all deeply experienced in psychosis, yet with very different theoretical as well as practical approaches.

Through the presentation of a very difficult case, Riccardo Lombardi aimed to show how Rose, a patient who suffered from several heavy psychotic crises and suicide attempts, was able to develop an ability to contain her emotional storms and realize important achievements in her life. Her analysis had the frequency of four sessions a week for its entire duration of, in all, 12 years. The analytic work was never interrupted even in periods of acute psychotic relapse which, with the help and support of both a pharmacologist and a consultant for the family, could be managed each time without needing further hospitalization. In the course of this analysis R. Lombardi presented four periods, lasting several months at a time, of psychotic destabilization, during which there emerged important elements of working through, that were consolidated in subsequent sessions during non-turbulent phases. During the first five years the patient achieved a first important contact with reality and resolved the profound dissociation which had caused her to deny the existence of the psychotic periods and not to recognize as her own the psychic elements that emerged during these crises. The growth she had consequently brought about of the non-psychotic part of her personality could make possible a further confrontation with the deepest aspects of her psychosis. The clinical presentation explored especially the clinical material of the fourth psychotic crisis in analysis, which involved the delusion of the grey men and the theft of time. R. Lombardi showed how the patient's working through of death and recognition of the value of time were central in catalysing a complete freedom from psychotic symptoms. The patient acquired a better contact with reality and was able to recognize the psychic elements that had emerged during the crises as well as the profound dissociation that made her deny them.

[[RunningHead]]

Das Labyrinth der Borderline-Kommunikation. Klinische Zugänge zum Erleben von Raum und Zeit [The Labyrinth of Borderline Communication: Clinical Access to the Experience of Space and Time]1 by Heinz WeissKlett-Cotta, Stuttgart, 2009; 220 pp; €29,90.

Gerhard Dammann

Gerhard Dammann

PO Box 154, Münsterlingen, 8596, Switzerland
E-mail: gerhard.dammann@stgag.ch

Gerhard Dammann  

In this book the psychoanalyst and clinical director Heinz Weiss deals with borderline disorders from a Kleinian viewpoint inclusive of concepts such as mentalization theory. As John Steiner makes clear in his foreword, the complexity of its subject-matter is immediately apparent in its terminology, and thus borderline mechanisms, borderline experiences, borderline phenomena and borderline structures are not always easy to distinguish from each other.

The concept of ‘borderline’ as it appears in the title and in a number of other places in this book is always somewhat loosely employed. Some of the patients presented in the very interesting case vignettes can hardly be termed ‘borderline patients’, and in some cases they are relatively successful academics. These patients are more likely to suffer from severe neuroses or to exhibit elements of structural disorder that could lead to greater problems in therapy. In some cases it might have been more appropriate to speak of ‘narcissistic’ disorders or ‘disorders of the self’. In other patients described by Weiss borderline disorders amount to severe distortions verging on psychoses.

None of this, however, diminishes the value of this book. Weiss convincingly suggests how work can be done where specific forms of resistance and negative therapeutic reaction occur. He demonstrates how these phenomena, in many cases with fears of dependence, lingering resentment and strong feelings of shame difficult to interpret, can be understood without causing further shame or involving the concept of destruction of inner space and how appropriate interpretation is possible in these transferences.

This book thus constitutes an outstanding and practical introduction to major developments in Neo-Kleinian theory over the past few years. Concepts such as ‘containment’ (Bion), ‘enactment’ (Jacobs), ‘psychic retreats’ (Steiner) and ‘expulsion of good objects’ (Brenman) in particular are presented with reference to clinical cases. Weiss strikingly shows how, even for experienced therapists, projective mechanisms can lead to loss of the capacity for clear thinking and accurate evaluation of countertransference phenomena, and the transference can also become a ‘total situation’ (Joseph) in its spatial dimension.

responseSet.title: The Expulsion of Evil and its Return: An Unconscious Fantasy Associated with a Case of Mass Hysteria in Adolescents
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Expulsion of Evil and its Return: An Unconscious Fantasy Associated with a Case of Mass Hysteria in Adolescents

Nashyiela Zavala

Nashyiela Zavala

Bosques de Caobas 67, Bosques de las Lomas, Deleg. Miguel Hidalgo, 11700, Mexico - nashyiela@yahoo.com

Nashyiela Zavala  

adolescent, development, hysteria

(Final version accepted 25 January 2010)

The formal study of hysteria started with psychoanalysis, which opened paths to studying the unconscious. However, we have found no psychoanalytic or psychiatric studies in the literature reporting epidemics of hysteria in hundreds of adolescent girls affected for several months like the one we describe. This epidemic occurred in a religious boarding-school in a rural area of Mexico. Our study aimed to determine psychoanalytic and sociocultural elements contributing to explain a behavioural epidemic outbreak during which young girls were unable to walk normally and which led to a temporary cessation of routine activities at the boarding-school. Key informers were interviewed, including the first cases of affected adolescent girls and the nuns. Interviews included questions concerning informers’ life history and their life at the boarding-school before and during the epidemic. We found that this boarding-school functioned as a large family affected by a psychotic episode which resulted from modes of communication of its members. This article describes the phenomenon and emphasizes perceived communication among members of the boarding-school and visitors at the time of the outbreak.

responseSet.title: ‘There is a Remedy for Everything but Death’: To Bear Witness, to Recover and Transform Psychic Experiences of Annihilation
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

‘There is a Remedy for Everything but Death’: To Bear Witness, to Recover and Transform Psychic Experiences of Annihilation11

Ronny Jaffè and Ilany Kogan

Ilany Kogan

ronnyj@tin.it

Ilany Kogan  

The aim of the panel was to discuss through Mark, a third-generation offspring of a Holocaust survivor, the traumatic elements emerging in the clinical material. The second and third generations are contaminated with the trauma of the Shoah through the encounters with the encapsulated memories of the previous generation. The following generations received from the previous generation the task of interpreting and understanding what at the time was impossible for the previous one to interpret and understand.

The presenting reasons why Mark comes to analysis are a feeling of painful loss which he connects to his mother and a bulimic type of eating disorder (binge eating).

The patient, a man in his 50s, married and father of two children, suffered from the death of many relatives and lives in the constant expectation of additional losses. He complains above all of a persistent inability to overcome the sadness over his mother's natural death; during the analysis the patient also evokes the appearance of an ulcer on the mother's leg at his birth “a wound which has not healed and never will all her life long”. Mark seems unwilling to lose contact with these painful, continuous tragic events. The active and constant reproduction of his pain prevents the mental representation of his losses, and he is scared that positive work on mourning could result in forgetfulness (which is felt to be lethal).

The panellists have analysed the clinical material according to three different models of analytical mental functioning in a patient who seemed to lack an apparatus for thinking losses. The three models proposed are (1) the duty to bear witness, (2) the necessity to recover, and (3) the need to transform the experiences.

Function of witnessing

Mark: “My mother did not hand down tragedy to me in my DNA, but a sense of tragic.”

1Panel held at the 46th Congress of the International Psychoanalytical Association, Chicago, Illinois, USA, 29 July 2009. Panellists: Simonetta Diena. Valeria Egidi-Morpurgo, Anna Ferruta.

Il bambino e le sue relazioni. Attaccamento e individualità tra teoria e osservazione [The Child and His Relationships: Attachment and Individuality Between Theory and Observation]1 by Cristina Riva Crugnola Raffaello Cortina, Milano, 2007; 268 pp.

Giuseppe Civitarese

Giuseppe Civitarese

E-mail: gcivitarese@gmail.com

Giuseppe Civitarese  

Although, from Freud onwards, the central paradigm guiding analytic work has been that of the dream, the model of the mother-baby relationship has become, in its various forms, a reference point of almost equal importance as psychoanalysis has moved from a unipersonal psychology to a bipersonal or intersubjective psychology. But the baby, as conceived of (in both clinical and ‘theoretical’ terms) by psychoanalysis, is not always the same as the one which interests researchers using an experimental or observational approach to investigate the infantile world. Riva Crugnola's book seeks to bridge this gap, to integrate the psychoanalytic perspectives on this subject with infant research, attachment theory and also with some of the findings of neuroscience.

responseSet.title: Mourning and Psychosis: A Psychoanalytic Perspective[Duelo y psicosis: Una perspectiva psicoanalitica]
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Mourning and Psychosis: A Psychoanalytic Perspective1[Duelo y psicosis: Una perspectiva psicoanalitica]1

Jorge Tizón

Jorge Tizón

Spain — eappp.pbcn@ics.scs.es (jtizong@meditex.es)

Jorge Tizón   Prevención en Salud Mental, Equip d'Atenció Precoç als Pacients en risc de Psicosis (EAPPP) [Early Care Equipment for At Risk of Psychosis Patients], Institut Catalá de la Salut [Catalan Health Institute], Barcelona (Córsega 544, bajos; 08025 Barcelona)
psychosis, psychotherapy, integrated treatment, combined therapy, mourning, affective loss

(Final version accepted 2 November 2009)

The author attempts to develop some of the basic models and concepts relating to mourning processes in psychotic patients on the assumption that situations of loss and mourning are key moments for psychoanalysis, psychotherapy, and therapeutic approaches in general. Secondly, he reminds us that ‘mourning processes in psychotics’ are not always ‘psychotic mourning processes’, that is to say, that they do not necessarily occur within, or give rise to, a psychotic clinical picture. These ideas are illustrated by a number of sessions and vignettes concerning two psychotic patients in psychotherapeutic and psychoanalytic treatment. In theoretical terms, it seems vitally important in this context to combine a relationship-based approach within a framework of special psychoanalytic psychopathology with an updated view of processes of mourning and affective loss. A fundamental requirement at clinical level is to determine the role to be played by psychoanalytically based treatments in combined, integrated or global therapies when working with psychotic patients. For this purpose, the paper ends by outlining a set of principles and objectives for such treatments.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Very Thought of Education: Psychoanalysis and the Impossible Professions by Deborah P. BritzmanState University of New York Press, Albany, NY, 2009; 166 pp; $60 (hardcover), $23.95 (paperback)

Jane Tillman

Jane Tillman

25 Main Street, Stockbridge, MA 02162, USA
E-mail: Jane.Tillman@austenriggs.net

Jane Tillman  

Deborah Britzman, Distinguished Research Professor in Education at York University in Toronto, and productive scholar at the intersection of education and psychoanalysis, creatively deepens and broadens our understanding of both fields in her most recent book, The Very Thought of Education: Psychoanalysis and the Impossible Professions. The topic of psychoanalytic education is often written about in terms of various training paradigms (Kernberg, 2000; Levy, 2009; Wallerstein, 2007), problems of institute life (Kernberg 1996), or the design of curricula (Rees, 2007). Inviting the reader into a totally different realm of reflection about the enterprise of education, Britzman's book is not a summary of previous discussions or controversies about psychoanalytic education. Rather, she poses interesting and thought-provoking questions about our entire experience of education and the ways our learning and teaching are inhabited by childhood experiences, various transferences and countertransferences to teaching and being taught, and the affects of love and hate. In her opening statement Britzman asserts that we have a transference to the history of our education and that this suffuses all our interactions in the classroom and beyond, as our education is indeed interminable. Put succinctly, this book explores Britzman's observation that: “Education is our transference playground of love and hate” (p. 131).

Further complicating educational practices is that the subterranean psychic history of our education often resists conscious reflection, making teaching and learning all the more fraught with unmetabolized bits of prior experience. Britzman uses both her first vocation, Distinguished Professor in Education, and her more recent foray into psychoanalytic education as a candidate in training, to explore how disavowed affects haunt the landscape of education. Her bifocal perspective, rich in knowledge from both disciplines, illuminates the history of psychoanalysis in its clinical and educative efforts, as well as using psychoanalytic theory to analyze conflicted aspects of education in the most intimate encounter between teacher and student.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Representante-Representativo, Représentant-Représentation, Ideational Representative: Which One is a Freudian Concept? On the Translation of Vorstellungsrepräsentanz in spanish, French and English11

Marcos Herrera

Marcos Herrera

Sociedad Peruana de Psicoanálisis — mpherrer@pucp.edu.pe

Marcos Herrera  

idea, ideational representative, instinctual representative, mental representation, presentation, psychic representative, psychoanalytic concepts, representation, translation of metapsychological writings

(Final version accepted 8 February 2010)

The Freudian expression Vorstellungsrepräsentanz (Freud, 1915b, 1915c), which is rendered in the Standard Edition as ideational representative, is commonly translated in Spanish as representante-representativo and in French as représentant-représentation, among other renderings. An interdisciplinary conceptual inquiry, which applies linguistic semantics to the evaluation of the available Spanish and French renderings, concludes that this compound expression should be translated in these languages as representante ideativo and représentant idéatif, respectively, renderings which happen to correspond to Strachey's translation into English in the SE. In contrast to most Spanish and French translations, this proposal conforms to the semantic principle of compositionality. On the one hand, it provides a suitable translation of the two parts of the compound. Thus it renders Vorstellung as idea, with the classical meaning of image or mental representation, which can be traced back to Hume's empiricist philosophy, and it renders Repräsentanz as representative, with the meaning of delegate. On the other hand, its linguistic form preserves the attributive meaning relationship which exists between both concepts in the original German expression. Against the background of these semantic considerations, a theoretical question concerning Freudian metapsychology is discussed: the drive has a psychic representative, but is there a (mental) representation of the drive?

Bernard Chervet  

In this book, Liliane Abensour shares with us the development of her thinking based on her long experience of the psychoanalytic treatment of psychotic patients. In a delicate and well-balanced manner, she brings together the respectful and subtle attention that she pays to the tragic inner experience of the psychotic, her detailed knowledge of psychoanalytic papers on mental functioning in psychotic patients, and clinical illustrations either directly through a discreetly confidential evocation of her own patients or through works of literature and art.

This approach is an offshoot of the two great traditions that are characteristic of French psychiatry: humanism

responseSet.title: Current Analytical Practice and the Analyst's Position,
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Current Analytical Practice and the Analyst's Position1,212

Stefano Bolognini and Paola Marion

Paola Marion

Via Claudio Monteverdi 15, 00109 Roma - p.marion@iol.it

Paola Marion  

This report gives an account of the work of a central panel in the Congress, which compared the positions of three analysts, coming from different analytical traditions and representing three continents, in respect to the challenges posed by current clinical conditions.

Stefano Bolognini, the co-ordinator of the panel, in his welcome speech to the participants and members of the public present, introduced the theme to be discussed and mentioned its topicality and its potential implications. He specified that each contribution represented a reflection on the expansion of the clinical field and on the resulting consequences for clinical practice and theoretical reflection. Even within the IPA there is now an acknowledgement of a plurality of theoretical approaches, which is paralleled by a diversity in clinical practice. These very developments force us to rethink and make new connections between meta-psychology and clinical practice, and again between clinical practice and theoretical models.

The first paper was presented by Norberto Marucco from Argentina (Current analytical practice and the analyst's position), who chose to develop his theme along two lines:

the present-day patient:

his/her psychopathology

emotional distress and consciousness of time

the ‘unconscious state’ (‘unconscious-ized’) and the analyst's position.

In Marucco's view there is a different approach to psychopathology in one present-day psychoanalytical orientation, with which he personally identifies: a preference for reasoning not in terms of structure, but of modes of functioning of the psychic apparatus. The author describes as ‘psychic zones’ those areas of the mind which are distinguished by different modes of ‘unconscious-ization’.

He sees the present-day patient as new not in terms of his/her pathology.

responseSet.title: Dante's Comedy: Precursors of Psychoanalytic Technique and Psyche
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Dante's Comedy: Precursors of Psychoanalytic Technique and Psyche

Nathan Szajnberg

Nathan Szajnberg

Jerusalem, Israel 91905 — nmoshe@gmail.com

Nathan Szajnberg  

Freud, psychoanalytic technique, literature, therapeutic alliance

This paper uses a literary approach to explore what common ground exists in both psychoanalytic technique and views of the psyche, of ‘person’. While Western literature has developed various views of psyche and person over centuries, there have been crystallizing, seminal portraits, for instance Shakespeare's perspective on what is human, some of which have endured to the present. By using Dante's Commedia, particularly the Inferno, a 14th century poem that both integrates and revises previous models of psyche and personhood, we can examine what features of psyche, and ‘techniques’ in soul-healing psychoanalysts have inherited culturally. Discovering basic features of technique and model of psyche we share as psychoanalysts permits us to explore why we have differences in variations on technique and models of inner life.

(Final version accepted 14 July 2009)

responseSet.title: Self-Criticism and Unconscious Grandiosity: Transference—Counter Transference Dimensions
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Self-Criticism and Unconscious Grandiosity: Transference—Counter Transference Dimensions

Steven Cooper

Steven Cooper

875 Massachusetts Ave., Suite 54, Cambridge, MA 02139, USA - stevencooper03@gmail.com

Steven Cooper  

character, countertransference, defense, resistance, symptoms, transference

(Final version accepted 12 February 2010)

The problems posed in understanding and working through the patient's layers of self-criticism are challenging for both patient and analyst. In particular, this paper explores some countertransference phenomena related to underlying grandiosity embedded in self-criticism. For patients who are self-critical, analyzing grandiose elements may create further grounds for self-reproach or open up new modes of self-experience and freedom. The paper tries to focus on how the analyst's experience of the patient's self-criticism often shifts over the course of analytic work. It is important for the analyst to not be crippled by a fear of considering the relevance of underlying grandiosity in relation to self-reproach. Understanding this dimension of self-reproach can help elucidate why it is so durable and refractory to interpretation. The patient has a stake in holding on to this self-punishment because it perpetuates self-regulatory fantasies. These fantasies sometimes relate to the feeling that the patient will be more successful or better loved by holding on to aspects of self-reproach. Sometimes these fantasies are based in competitive or dominant strategies related to winning out or retaliating over parents or siblings.

[[RunningHead]]

Partners in Thought: Working with Unformulated Experience, Dissociation, and Enactment by Donnel B. SternRoutledge, New York, NY, 2010; 229 + xix pp; $34.95

Lucy LaFarge

Lucy LaFarge

239 Central Park West, Ste. 1-BE, New York, NY 10024, USA
E-mail: lafzim@earthlink.net

Lucy LaFarge  

In his 1997 volume, Unformulated Experience: From Dissociation to Imagination in Psychoanalysis, Donnel Stern (1997) laid the groundwork for an interpersonal psychoanalytic theory centering upon the dialogic creation of meaning by analyst and patient. Drawing upon the work of interpersonal

1037

and relational analysts and of hermeneutic philosophers, particularly Hans-Georg Gadamer, Stern presented a view of the analytic process in which neglected and warded-off aspects of self-experience acquired meaning and were integrated into identity as they were recognized and put into language by patient and analyst. The central defense mechanism keeping elements of experience outside awareness in this model was dissociation, the active or passive restriction of experiences available to be known; and dissociated experiences were felt to be unformulated — that is, it was only through interpretation that these elements became organized and assumed a shape. The interpersonal field was the central force determining what could be known and interpreted by both patient and analyst, and Stern envisioned an analytic process in which the analyst's curiosity and attention to what was missing or contradictory in the analytic situation enabled him to become disembedded from the interpersonal field of the current analytic situation and thus to open himself and his patient to new elements and formulations.

Although Stern's concept of the interpersonal field pointed to a notion of perpetual enactment, from which analyst and patient could only partially and transiently remove themselves in order to reflect and formulate, this earlier book focused very much on language, and the key moment of the analytic process was often seen as the entry of the previously unformulated into the linguistically determined world of meaning. In Partners in Thought, the successor volume to his 1997 work, Stern expands his previous model and shifts its emphasis. The collaborative creation of meaning continues to serve as a guiding framework for the analytic endeavor, but enactment and the expansion of perception are now seen, along with verbalization, as important steps in the progression from unformulated to formulated. Similarly, the entry of the unformulated into language is no longer seen as the ultimate goal, but rather as a means of attaining greater freedom of imagination and self-experience.

responseSet.title: L'Esprit du mal [The Spirit of Evil] by Nathalie Zaltzman Editions de l'Olivier, Paris, penser/rêver, 2007; 109 pp; €10.00
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

L'Esprit du mal [The Spirit of Evil]1 by Nathalie ZaltzmanEditions de l'Olivier, Paris, penser/rêver, 2007; 109 pp; €10.00

François Villa and Eva Weil

Eva Weil

E-mail: villa@univ-paris-diderot.fr

E-mail: evaweil@gmail.com

Eva Weil  

The principal question posed by Nathalie Zaltzman in her last book entitled L'Esprit du mal [The Spirit of Evil] is whether we can think about evil today.2 She confronts each of us with a crucial question: can the psychoanalytic method of psychic investigation shed any light on the phenomenon of evil? This gives rise to two further questions. Firstly, can psychic treatment, as a form of cultural work, help to overcome the operation of evil in culture? And, secondly, are the barbaric tragedies repeated over history mere returns to earlier states or do they generate unprecedented forms?

Paraphrasing Baudelaire's preliminary address to the readers of Les Fleurs ciu Mal, we would say that this book invites us, its hypocritical readers, to confront the question of evil by recognizing that this is in fact a ‘delicate monster’ that exercises all too familiar a mastery and role in our actions. The inquiry into the psychic dimension of evil is connected with the disagreement that Zaltzman experiences with Civilization and Its Discontents (Freud, 1930). This leads her to tackle evil to examine whether there is any treatment that may be applied to it by the cultural work.

In what is generally designated as evil, Zaltzman identifies the return to the psychic scene and the world scene of a psychological truth that the ideals and commandments created by society and civilization have sought to deny in rejecting the effort that its transformation would have imposed.

1Translated by Sophie Leighton.2Today here means after the Shoah.667

[[RunningHead]]

Convergences and Divergences in Treatments of So-Called ADHD Children11

Dieter Bürgin , Marianne Leuzinger-Bohleber and Alan Sugarman

Alan Sugarman

4180 La Jolla Village Drive, Suite 550B, La Jolla, CA 92037, USA — mumford2@cox.net

Alan Sugarman  

Marianne Leuzinger-Bohleber introduced the panel by noting the increasing interest in ADHD, particularly from a psychoanalytic perspective. Several reasons for this were postulated. One was the recent dialogue between psychoanalysis and contemporary neurobiology that promises to expand the ability to understand and treat psychopathology that has some neurobiological contributor. Others involve new developments from within psychoanalysis. For example, infant attachment and memory research have fostered alternative models and therapeutic techniques to treat early disturbances of affect regulation, symbolization, and mentalization — all central dysfunctions in ADHD. The last reason for the growing discussion about ADHD in psychoanalytic circles was thought to be an effort by self-critical psychoanalysts to respond to competition with other treatment modalities, most prominently, cognitive-behavioral and psychopharmacological ones. She emphasized that psychoanalytic concepts offer the possibility of shedding light on the complex and multiple determinants of ADHD, thereby allowing for more differentiated diagnoses and treatment strategies that accept the likelihood of different psychodynamic subgroupings of children who might all receive the same descriptive diagnosis of ADHD.

Dieter Bùrgin expanded on this introduction, emphasizing the symptom triad of ADHD: (1) attentional problems; (2) overactivity; and (3) poor impulse control. Each of the mental functions underlying these symptoms is dependent on the quality of the relationship between the infant and caretaker. Thus one can assume that a child with longstanding disturbances in these three functions had difficulty finding adequate help in developing them. ADHD children often have histories reflecting disturbances in infantile development, impaired attachment, and other ego disruptions. Early object loss and/or psychic conflict are also often found in their histories. However, the multitude of factors prohibits generalizations about etiology or pathogenesis.

responseSet.title: Phallic and Seminal Masculinity: A Theoretical and Clinical Confusion
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Phallic and Seminal Masculinity: A Theoretical and Clinical Confusion

Karl Figlio

Karl Figlio

24 Rosebery Avenue, Colchester CO1 2UJ, UK — kfiglio@essex.ac.uk

Karl Figlio  

ambivalence, castration anxiety, masculinity, procreation, semen

Both inside and outside psychoanalysis, the word, ‘seminal’, is used to praise a creative contribution to science and culture. Rarely, however, does it refer to male procreativity, to the structures and functions that subserve it or to the anxiety related to a threat to it. This situation becomes evident in the concept of castration anxiety, which typically refers, with Freud, to cutting off the penis and not to extirpating the testicles. This phallic theory has been debated, repudiated and ignored. While there is an alternative literature on non-phallic masculinity, it is scattered and rarely refers to seminal function. Freud's theory meets his requirement for a well-articulated representation of absolute loss as an experience, but this clear structure — and its repudiation — obscure the observation and theory of the internal world of the male. I propose the concepts of ‘seminal masculinity’ and ‘seminal castration’, which I ground in Melanie Klein's concept of depressive anxiety. I contrast them with phallic masculinity and phallic castration anxiety, which I ground in her concept of paranoid-schizoid anxiety. I argue that they meet Freud's requirement that castration be a potential experience and that understanding masculinity demands such a basis.

(Final version accepted 15 July 2009)

responseSet.title: An Interruption in Unconscious Communication in the Analytic Couple
responseSet.authorMast: Hilda María Feuerhake and  Marta Lebrero
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

An Interruption in Unconscious Communication in the Analytic Couple

Hilda Feuerhake and Marta Lebrero

Marta Lebrero

Camino del Labrador 1681-A, Lo Barnechea, Santiago, Chile — hmfeuerhake@vtr.net

Napoleón 3565, Oficina 1501, Las Condes, Santiago, Chile — mlebrero@vtr.net

Marta Lebrero  

basic assumptions, dream activity, enactment, narcissistic collapse, persecutory guilt, shared unconscious phantasy, ‘super’-ego, transformation into hallucinosis, working through of countertransference

The authors describe an interruption in communication in the analyses of two patients, which gradually brings the analytic process to a halt/standstill. They propose several hypotheses for understanding this situation. One explanation is mutual identification of primitive superegos in the analytic couple which generates a moralizing effect thereby hindering investigation and discovery. They emphasize the importance of countertransference involvement which partly provokes this particular type of impasse. They also suggest the idea of shared acting out, with complementary participations of analyst and patient. In this way the analytic couple supports a ‘bastion’ which protects against the risk of breaking the omnipotence of patient and analyst or contributes to this omnipotence. Their shared unconscious phantasy feeds collusion linked to unconscious persecutory guilt. The authors also describe movements to break free from this impasse. The enclave created by the analytic couple is detected and subsequently worked through by way of the patient's contribution of dream material and the analyst's work with her countertransference.

(Final version accepted 21 May 2009)
